stores.intro: buybuy BABY focuses on strollers, cribs, car seats, and other baby gear. Purchases typically code as a department store rather than as groceries, so supermarket bonuses will not apply to formula or diapers bought here. A flat-rate card, or one rewarding department-store or online spending, captures the most value on big-ticket nursery items.

stores.name: Cabela's
stores.intro: Cabela's, the outfitter merged under the Bass Pro umbrella, sells firearms, gear, and apparel that frequently code as a department store at its large stores, so supermarket or restaurant multipliers do not help. Purchases at cabelas.com generally code as online shopping. A co-branded card exists, but most buyers do fine with a high flat-rate or online-shopping card.

stores.name: Caesars Rewards
stores.intro:
Caesars Rewards is the loyalty program spanning Caesars Entertainment's casino resorts,
including Caesars Palace, Harrah's, Horseshoe, and Paris Las Vegas properties across the
US. Unlike most brands, Caesars does have a co-brand credit card lineup: the no-annual-fee
Caesars Rewards Visa and the premium Caesars Rewards Prestige Visa Signature, both issued
by Comenity/Bread Financial, which earn bonus Reward Credits on stays, dining, and
entertainment at Caesars properties. Shoppers without one of those cards can still lean
on a general hotel or travel rewards card when booking a room.

stores.name: California Pizza Kitchen
stores.intro:
California Pizza Kitchen operates about 170 casual-dining restaurants known for hearth-baked
pizzas with creative toppings like the original BBQ Chicken, Thai Chicken, and California
Veggie, plus a broader menu of pastas and entree salads. CPK transactions, whether dine-in,
takeout, or through CPK Rewards online ordering, code as restaurants/dining. That means
cards offering elevated dining multipliers (Amex Gold at 4x Membership Rewards, Chase
Sapphire Reserve at 4x Ultimate Rewards, Capital One SavorOne at 3% cash) earn full rate.

stores.name: Calvin Klein
stores.intro:
Calvin Klein is a global fashion house under PVH Corp known for its underwear, denim, and
ready-to-wear lines, sold through its own stores and site as well as major department
stores like Macy's. There is no Calvin Klein co-brand credit card, so a general online
shopping or apparel rewards card is the right tool for a direct purchase, while a
department-store card can work well if you're buying through Macy's or a similar
retailer instead.

stores.name: Calzedonia
stores.intro:
Calzedonia is an Italian hosiery and swimwear retailer, part of the Calzedonia Group that
also owns Intimissimi and Tezenis, known for tights, leggings, and beachwear sold through
boutiques across Europe and a US e-commerce site. It has no co-brand credit card, so
shoppers ordering online should use a select-clothing or general online shopping rewards
card rather than looking for a store-specific option.

stores.name: Camp Chef
stores.intro:
Camp Chef makes outdoor cooking gear built for campsites and backyards alike, including
portable propane stoves, pellet grills and smokers, griddles, and dutch ovens, sold
through its own site and outdoor retailers like Cabela's and Bass Pro Shops. There is no
Camp Chef co-brand credit card, so a sporting goods or general online shopping rewards
card is the better fit for a direct order, and a flat-rate cashback card works fine too.

stores.name: Cannondale
stores.intro:
Cannondale is an American bicycle manufacturer known for road, mountain, and gravel bikes,
plus the distinctive fat-tubed aluminum frames it pioneered decades ago, sold through
independent bike shops and its own website. There is no Cannondale co-brand credit card,
so a sporting goods or general online shopping rewards card is the right choice for parts,
gear, or a direct bike purchase.

stores.name: Care.com
stores.intro:
Care.com is an online marketplace connecting families with babysitters, nannies, senior
caregivers, pet sitters, and housekeepers, plus background-check and payroll tools for
managing caregivers directly. It carries no co-brand credit card, so members paying
membership fees or premium background checks through the site should use a general online
shopping or flat-rate cashback rewards card.

stores.name: Carhartt
stores.intro:
Carhartt is a private, family-owned workwear brand based in Dearborn, Michigan, with
about 30 company stores plus wide distribution through Tractor Supply, farm and ranch
retailers, and carhartt.com. Most cards code direct purchases at company stores or the
website as department store or online shopping, while buying Carhartt at a tractor or
farm supply retailer typically codes under that merchant's category. There is no
Carhartt branded credit card today.

stores.name: Caribou Coffee
stores.intro:
Caribou Coffee, founded in Minnesota in 1992, runs about 700 cafes concentrated in the Upper
Midwest plus a growing licensed presence in airports, hospitals, and grocery stores.
Signature drinks include the Caribou Cooler frozen blends, Northern Lite sugar-free options,
and seasonal Pumpkin White Mocha. Every Caribou purchase, whether at a corporate cafe or
through the Caribou Perks app, codes as dining. That means strong dining cards (Amex Gold
4x, Chase Sapphire Preferred 3x, Capital One SavorOne 3% unlimited) all hit the full bonus.

stores.name: Carl's Jr.
stores.intro:
Carl's Jr. is the western US half of CKE Restaurants, known for charbroiled burgers like
the Western Bacon Cheeseburger and the Famous Star. The chain runs about 1,000 domestic
locations, mostly west of the Mississippi, alongside sister brand Hardee's in the east.
In-store, drive-thru, and Carl's Jr. app purchases all code as fast-food/restaurants on
every credit card network, so cards paying elevated rates on dining (Chase Sapphire
Preferred at 3x, Amex Gold at 4x, Capital One Savor at 3%) all earn their full bonus here.

stores.name: CarMax
stores.intro:
CarMax is the largest used-car retailer in the United States, with over 240
stores and a national online buying platform at carmax.com. The company offers
no-haggle pricing, seven-day returns, and home delivery on used vehicles. Credit
cards generally cap how much can go toward a vehicle purchase, with $1,000 to
$5,000 typical limits, and the transaction codes as a vehicle dealer rather than a
rewards category.

Down payments at CarMax are useful for clearing signup bonus minimums on cards like
the Chase Sapphire Preferred or Amex Gold. Some cardholders also use CarMax for
manufactured spend on Visa or Mastercard rewards cards. Service and parts visits at
CarMax service centers code separately and may earn standard retail rates.

stores.name: Carnival Cruise Line
stores.intro:
Carnival Cruise Line is the largest U.S. cruise operator by passenger volume,
owned by Carnival Corporation (which also owns Princess, Holland America, and
Cunard). Cruise bookings code as travel on every card we track. Pre-booked
shore excursions, drink packages, and gratuities purchased through the cruise
line generally code as travel as well. Onboard charges (folio purchases) often
code as travel but sometimes as cruise-specific — worth confirming on a small
charge before relying on a 3x travel multiplier.

stores.name: Carrabba's
stores.intro: Carrabba's Italian Grill is a sit-down chain serving wood-grilled Italian fare, part of the same Bloomin' Brands family as Outback. Your check codes as dining, so a card with a restaurant bonus category earns more than a flat-rate card. Tipping on the same card keeps the whole meal in that dining bucket.

stores.name: Carvana
stores.intro:
Carvana is an online-only used-car retailer known for its glass-block car vending
machine towers and same-week home delivery model. Customers shop, finance, and
buy entirely through carvana.com or the Carvana app. Credit cards are accepted only
for down payments, typically capped around $2,000 to $5,000, and the charge codes
as a vehicle dealer rather than online shopping.

Carvana down payments work well for clearing signup bonus minimums on cards like
the Capital One Venture X or the Citi Strata Premier. Cards with selectable online
shopping categories generally do not capture Carvana transactions because of the
vehicle dealer MCC, so flat-rate earners like the Citi Double Cash often produce
the most predictable rewards.

stores.name: Casey's
stores.intro:
Casey's is a Midwest convenience-store and pizza giant with more than 2,600 locations
across 17 states, anchored in Iowa and the surrounding farm belt. Fuel pumps code as
gas, while in-store purchases (including the famously good made-to-order pizza) can
code as convenience or restaurant depending on the location. Casey's runs its own
Casey's Rewards loyalty program and issues a Casey's Visa co-brand card with elevated
earn on Casey's purchases, including pizza and gas at the pump.

stores.name: Casper
stores.intro:
Casper is a direct-to-consumer mattress brand that helped define the bed-in-a-box
category, with foam, hybrid, and Wave mattress lines plus pillows, sheets, and
bedroom furniture. Most Casper purchases happen online at casper.com, which means
rewards cards that pay bonuses for online shopping see the most upside.

Bank of America Customized Cash and U.S. Bank Cash+ can route Casper orders into a
selectable 5 percent online shopping category, while the Capital One SavorOne pays
3 percent on entertainment, dining, and streaming but only base earn on mattresses.
Casper occasionally appears in shopping portals like Rakuten or Capital One
Shopping, where portal cash back can stack with credit card rewards.

stores.name: Cathay Pacific
stores.intro: Cathay Pacific is Hong Kong's flagship carrier and a oneworld member, so tickets bought directly typically code as airline travel and earn on cards with an airfare or general travel bonus. Award seats are bookable through partner programs, and a travel card with strong transfer partners or a flat travel multiplier usually beats paying out of pocket.

stores.name: Cavender's
stores.intro:
Cavender's is a Texas-based western wear retailer with stores across the South and
Southwest, selling boots, hats, jeans, and western apparel from brands like Ariat and
Wrangler alongside its own house lines. Cavender's does not offer a store credit card, so
a select-clothing or general online shopping rewards card is the better option for boots
or apparel bought online or in one of its stores.

stores.name: CB2
stores.intro:
CB2 is the modern, design-forward offshoot of Crate & Barrel under Otto family-owned
Euromarket Designs, with about 25 U.S. stores plus cb2.com selling contemporary
furniture, lighting, and decor. The Crate & Barrel credit card from Comenity covers
CB2 purchases too and earns rewards across the family of brands. General-purpose
cards typically code CB2 as home improvement or department store in person and
online shopping for web orders.

stores.name: Celebrity Cruises
stores.intro: Celebrity Cruises positions itself in the premium contemporary segment under the Royal Caribbean Group umbrella. Fares paid directly usually code as travel and earn on cards with a travel category bonus, though cruise lines can code unpredictably. To be safe, lean on a card that explicitly counts cruises as travel or a strong flat-rate everyday card.

stores.name: Champs Sports
stores.intro: Champs Sports, part of the Foot Locker family, sells sneakers and athletic apparel from major brands. Online checkout codes as online shopping or general retail, so a shopping portal may apply, but in-store buys typically fall to a flat-rate card since athletic gear rarely earns a category bonus.

stores.name: Charles & Keith
stores.intro:
Charles & Keith is a Singapore-founded fashion label specializing in affordable-luxury
shoes, handbags, and accessories, with a large footprint across Asia and the Middle East
and a growing US e-commerce presence. The brand has no co-brand credit card in the US
market, so shoppers buying shoes or bags through its site should rely on an online
shopping rewards card or a select-clothing category card rather than a store-specific one.

stores.name: Charlotte Tilbury
stores.intro:
Charlotte Tilbury is a prestige British makeup and skincare brand owned by Puig, known for
its Pillow Talk range and sold direct at charlottetilbury.com, in its own counters, and
through Sephora and department stores. There is no Charlotte Tilbury co-brand credit card,
so a direct purchase codes as online shopping, while the same products at Sephora or a
department store can earn under that retailer's rate.

stores.name: CheapOair
stores.intro:
CheapOair is an online travel agency that books discounted flights, hotels, car rentals,
and vacation packages, competing with sites like Expedia and Priceline for budget-focused
travelers. It has no co-brand credit card, so a travel rewards card that earns bonus
points on travel purchases broadly, rather than a specific airline or hotel card, is the
best match when booking through the site.

stores.name: Checkers
stores.intro: Checkers is a double-drive-thru fast-food chain known for its seasoned fries and value menu, operating alongside its sister brand Rally's. A swipe at the window codes as dining or restaurants on most rewards cards, so a dining bonus applies. Since locations are drive-thru only with no dining room, mobile-order pickup still rings up in the same restaurant category.

stores.name: Cheddar's
stores.intro: Cheddar's Scratch Kitchen, owned by Darden, leans on made-from-scratch comfort food at value prices. As a full-service restaurant, your bill codes as dining on virtually all cards, so the best play is one that pays a higher rate on restaurants instead of leaving the meal on a general-purpose card.

stores.name: Chegg
stores.intro:
Chegg is an online education platform best known for textbook rentals and homework help
and study tools, along with tutoring and writing assistance subscriptions aimed at
college students. There is no Chegg co-brand credit card, so a general online shopping or
student-friendly flat-rate cashback card is the right fit for textbook rentals or
subscription payments.

stores.name: Chevron
stores.intro:
Chevron and Texaco-branded stations cover about 8,000 U.S. locations primarily in the
West and South. Pumps code cleanly as gas on every card we track. The Chevron Visa
offers extra cents-per-gallon discounts at Chevron and Texaco stations, but most
general-purpose gas-bonus cards beat it on absolute cashback unless you're a heavy
Chevron-only buyer.

stores.name: Chewy
stores.intro:
Chewy is the largest dedicated U.S. online pet retailer, with autoship being the
dominant purchase pattern for repeat customers. All Chewy purchases code as online
shopping on every card we track. Pet-supply spend doesn't fall into a dedicated card
category on most general-purpose cards, so the right answer here is whichever U.S.
online retail card you carry — Blue Cash Everyday, Hilton Surpass, or a flat 2% card
depending on cap considerations.

stores.name: Chick-fil-A
stores.intro:
Chick-fil-A is one of the highest-grossing U.S. fast-food chains by per-store revenue,
with about 3,000 locations and a closed-loop app/loyalty program. All Chick-fil-A
purchases code as dining/restaurants on every card we track. App reloads of Chick-fil-A
One credits also trigger the dining category, which is a clean way to capture the bonus.

stores.name: Chico's
stores.intro: Chico's offers women's apparel and jewelry with a relaxed, artisan-leaning style, part of the same group as White House Black Market. Online orders code as online shopping or retail and may earn through a portal, while store visits usually default to flat-rate since clothing is rarely its own bonus category.

stores.name: Chili's
stores.intro:
Chili's is a U.S. casual-dining chain with about 1,200 domestic locations, owned
by Brinker International. Charges code as dining on every card we track, so
any dining-bonus card applies (Amex Gold 4x, Sapphire Preferred 3x, SavorOne 3%).
My Chili's Rewards offers a free chips-and-salsa or non-alcoholic drink with
each visit and frequent BOGO entree promotions, stackable with card bonuses.

stores.name: Chipotle
stores.intro:
Chipotle is one of the largest U.S. fast-casual chains at about 3,500 locations, with a
well-developed digital ordering app and Chipotle Rewards loyalty program. All Chipotle
purchases code as dining/restaurants on every card we track, so 3-5% dining-bonus cards
earn at the same rate whether you order in-store, on the app, or for delivery direct
from Chipotle.

stores.name: Choice Hotels
stores.intro:
Choice Hotels operates roughly 7,500 properties worldwide, dominated by mid-scale and
economy brands — Comfort Inn, Quality Inn, Sleep Inn, Clarion — with the Cambria and
Ascend Hotel Collection covering its upscale push. The 2022 acquisition of Radisson
Americas folded Country Inn & Suites and Park Plaza into Choice Privileges, broadening
the footprint considerably. Award nights start as low as 6,000 points, and the program
is one of the better economy-tier values on a cents-per-point basis. The Wells Fargo
Choice Privileges co-brand cards earn elevated points on Choice stays and gas, with
the Select tier adding automatic Platinum elite status and an annual bonus night.
Direct bookings earn points; OTA stays do not.

stores.name: Church's Chicken
stores.intro: Church's Chicken is a Texas-founded fried-chicken chain (branded Church's Texas Chicken in many markets) known for its honey-butter biscuits. Purchases code as dining or restaurants on most rewards cards, so a dining bonus is ideal. A walk-up or drive-thru order stays in the dining bucket, while delivery apps may reroute the category.

stores.name: Cinnabon
stores.intro:
Cinnabon, part of the Focus Brands family alongside Auntie Anne's and Jamba, operates more
than 1,500 worldwide bakeries famous for the Classic Roll, MiniBon, and Cinnabon Stix made
with Makara cinnamon. Most US locations are in malls, airports, and travel plazas. Every
Cinnabon purchase codes as a dining/restaurant transaction, so dining bonus cards return
their full rate here. Amex Gold earns 4x Membership Rewards, Chase Sapphire Preferred earns
3x, and the Capital One SavorOne earns 3% cash back on every cinnamon roll order.

stores.name: Circle K
stores.intro:
Circle K is one of the largest U.S. convenience-store chains, with about 7,000
domestic locations operating both branded fuel and convenience retail. Fuel
purchases code as gas on every card we track; in-store purchases code as
convenience or grocery depending on the bank. The Inner Circle membership
($1.99/mo) layers on per-gallon discounts and is usually a stronger rebate per
visit than any Circle K-specific credit card.

stores.name: Citgo
stores.intro:
Citgo operates roughly 4,200 branded gas stations across the United States, owned by
Venezuelan state oil company PDVSA. Fuel at Citgo pumps codes as gas for credit card
networks, so any gas category bonus card will earn its full rate. The Citgo Rewards
Card (issued by Citibank Retail Services) offers a small per-gallon discount or
statement credit on Citgo fuel and a sign-up bonus, though a general-purpose 3-5x gas
card from a major issuer will typically out-earn the closed-loop product.

stores.name: Clarins
stores.intro:
Clarins is a French skincare and cosmetics brand known for plant-based formulas and spa
treatments, sold through its own boutiques, department store counters at retailers like
Nordstrom and Macy's, and its US e-commerce site. There is no Clarins co-brand credit
card, so shoppers buying skincare or makeup online should use a general online shopping
rewards card, or a department-store card if purchasing through a counter instead.

stores.name: Clarks
stores.intro: Clarks is a heritage British shoemaker known for Desert Boots and cushioned comfort footwear. Online orders code as online shopping or general retail and can earn through a portal, but in-person purchases usually default to flat-rate since footwear rarely qualifies for a bonus rate.

stores.name: Clinique
stores.intro: Clinique, the allergy-tested Estee Lauder brand, sells its Dramatically Different lotion and three-step skincare through clinique.com, with checkout coding as online shopping or general retail. Cosmetics rarely trigger a bonus category, so a card that rewards online purchases or carries a high flat rate is the practical pick. Counter purchases at department stores follow that store's coding instead.

stores.name: Coach
stores.intro:
Coach is the flagship leather goods brand of Tapestry Inc, which also owns Kate Spade
and Stuart Weitzman. The U.S. footprint includes around 360 full-price and outlet
stores plus coach.com. Direct purchases typically code as department store in person
and online shopping for web orders, while Coach handbags bought at Macy's, Nordstrom,
or Bloomingdale's code under those host retailers. The free Coach Insider loyalty
program stacks with credit card multipliers.

stores.name: Cold Stone Creamery
stores.intro:
Cold Stone Creamery runs about 1,000 US shops centered on premium ice cream that's mixed
on a frozen granite stone with customer-selected mix-ins, plus signature Creations like
Birthday Cake Remix, Founder's Favorite, and Strawberry Blonde. Cold Stone is part of the
Kahala Brands portfolio. Every Cold Stone transaction codes as dining/restaurants, including
ice cream cakes ordered through the My Cold Stone Club app. Dining-bonus cards like the Amex
Gold (4x), Chase Sapphire Preferred (3x), and Capital One SavorOne (3% cash) earn full rate.

stores.name: Cole Haan
stores.intro:
Cole Haan is an American footwear and accessories brand known for dress shoes, loafers,
and its Grand series that blends dress styling with sneaker-like comfort technology, sold
through its own stores, outlets, and site. It has no co-brand credit card, so a
select-clothing or general online shopping rewards card is the better fit for a Cole Haan
purchase rather than a store-specific option.

stores.name: Columbia Sportswear
stores.intro:
Columbia Sportswear is a Portland, Oregon outdoor apparel and footwear company that
also owns Mountain Hardwear, Sorel, and prAna. Direct sales run through roughly 130
branded and outlet stores plus columbia.com, while a heavy wholesale footprint puts
Columbia gear in REI, Dick's, and big-box sporting goods. Direct purchases typically
code as department store or online shopping. The free Greater Rewards loyalty program
stacks with credit card multipliers.

stores.name: Cometeer
stores.intro:
Cometeer is a direct-to-consumer coffee subscription that ships flash-frozen pucks of
concentrated coffee brewed from beans sourced through well-known roasters, meant to be
thawed or blended with hot or cold water at home. There is no Cometeer co-brand credit
card, so a dining rewards card that covers coffee and beverage purchases, or a general
online shopping card, is the right way to earn on a subscription order.

stores.name: Corkcicle
stores.intro:
Corkcicle makes insulated drinkware, tumblers, wine chillers, and coolers designed to keep
drinks cold or hot for hours, sold through its own site as well as retailers like
Nordstrom and REI. There is no Corkcicle co-brand credit card, so a general online
shopping rewards card, or a home goods category card if you have one, is the best way to
earn on a purchase.

stores.name: Corsair
stores.intro:
Corsair is a major PC gaming hardware maker known for mechanical keyboards, mice,
headsets, power supplies, memory, cases, and liquid coolers, sold directly and through
retailers like Best Buy and Amazon. There is no Corsair-branded credit card, so buyers
upgrading a gaming rig through corsair.com should reach for an electronics category card
or a general online shopping rewards card instead of looking for a store-specific option.

stores.name: COS
stores.intro:
COS, short for Collection of Style, is a contemporary fashion label under the H&M Group
known for minimalist, architecturally cut clothing and accessories for men and women,
sold through standalone stores and its own e-commerce site. COS has no co-brand credit
card, so shoppers should default to a select-clothing or general online shopping rewards
card at checkout, since neither a department-store nor apparel-specific card exists
for the brand.

stores.name: Cosori
stores.intro:
Cosori is a consumer kitchen appliance brand best known for its air fryers, along with
blenders, kettles, and other small countertop appliances, sold heavily through Amazon as
well as its own site. It carries no co-brand credit card, so an electronics category card
or a general online shopping rewards card is the right choice for a Cosori purchase.

stores.name: Costco

also_earns.note: If selected as one of your 2 quarterly retailers (up to $1,500/quarter, then 1.5%)

stores.intro:
Costco Wholesale runs about 600 warehouses worldwide and a sizable online catalog at
costco.com. The pricing model revolves around member-only bulk buying, so card choice
matters most for people running weekly grocery and gas trips through the warehouse. Two
quirks worth knowing: Costco accepts only Visa cards in-warehouse (no Mastercard, Amex,
or Discover), and Costco gas pumps code as wholesale clubs — not gas — so a 5% gas card
will not trigger at the pump here.

stores.name: Cox Communications
stores.intro: Cox Communications is one of the largest privately held broadband providers in the US, bundling internet, cable TV, and home phone. Recurring Cox bills code as a cable or internet purchase, so they only earn elevated rewards on a card carrying an internet or cable category bonus. Without one, route the autopay to your best flat-rate card and pocket the points monthly.

stores.name: Cracker Barrel
stores.intro:
Cracker Barrel Old Country Store operates about 660 restaurants in 45 states, pairing a
Southern country-style restaurant with an attached retail country store stocked with candy,
toys, and home goods. Menu staples include Chicken n Dumplins, Hashbrown Casserole, and
Sunday Homestyle Chicken. Restaurant charges code as dining; retail-store purchases generally
code as general merchandise/department stores. Dining cards like Amex Gold (4x), Chase
Sapphire Preferred (3x), and Capital One SavorOne (3% cash) hit the full bonus on meals.

stores.name: Crate & Barrel
stores.intro:
Crate & Barrel is a U.S. home furnishings retailer with about 100 stores plus
the CB2 modern-design sister brand. In-store charges code as furniture; online
orders code as online shopping. The Crate & Barrel Credit Card and Reward
Mastercard issue percent-back in store credit on Crate & Barrel-family purchases;
the Reward Mastercard adds a flat rate on everyday spend. Pair otherwise with
an online-shopping-bonus card.

stores.name: Cricket Wireless
stores.intro: Cricket Wireless, AT&T's prepaid brand, sells no-contract phone plans and devices. Payments code as a wireless or telecom charge, so they earn extra only on a card with a cell-phone or wireless bonus, otherwise dropping to flat-rate. A bonus here is that some cards add cell-phone protection when you pay the monthly bill with them, which is worth checking before you set autopay.

stores.name: Crocs
stores.intro:
Crocs Inc is a Broomfield, Colorado footwear company best known for its foam clogs and
Jibbitz charms, plus the HEYDUDE acquisition. The U.S. footprint includes about 150
outlet and full-price stores along with a strong DTC business at crocs.com. Direct
purchases typically code as department store in person and online shopping for web
orders. The free Crocs Club loyalty program offers points and member discounts that
stack with credit card rewards.

stores.name: Cruise America
stores.intro:
Cruise America is the largest RV rental company in North America, renting motorhomes and
campervans from locations across the US and Canada for road trips and camping vacations.
There is no Cruise America co-brand credit card, so a travel rewards card, or a car-rental
category card if you have one, is the best match for booking an RV rental.

stores.name: Crumbl Cookies
stores.intro: Crumbl Cookies is a viral Utah-born franchise that rotates a weekly lineup of oversized gourmet cookies marketed heavily on social media. Whether you order in person or for pickup, the charge codes as dining or restaurants on most cards, so a dining bonus pays off. Delivery routed through a third-party app may instead code under that delivery service.

stores.name: Crunch Fitness
stores.intro: Crunch Fitness is a franchise gym chain known for budget pricing, group classes and a no-judgment vibe. As with most gyms, monthly dues seldom earn a bonus, so they typically code at the flat rate, and any value boost is more likely to come from a card offering a fitness or wellness credit than from a category multiplier.

stores.name: Cub Foods
stores.intro: Cub Foods is a Minnesota-rooted supermarket chain, also under the Albertsons banner, that helped pioneer the large warehouse-style grocery format. Purchases code as groceries on most cards, so a card with a supermarket bonus is the smart pick. Liquor bought at an adjacent Cub Wine and Spirits may ring up separately rather than as groceries.

stores.name: Culver's
stores.intro:
Culver's is a Wisconsin-based quick-service chain known for ButterBurgers, fresh
frozen custard with a rotating Flavor of the Day, and Wisconsin cheese curds. The
family-owned business operates over 950 restaurants concentrated in the Midwest
and Sun Belt. Culver's transactions code as restaurants on all major credit card
networks, which makes them eligible for standard dining bonus categories.

The Amex Gold leads on Culver's spending at 4x points per dollar on U.S.
restaurants, while the Chase Sapphire Reserve and Capital One Savor both pay 3x or
more on dining. No-annual-fee dining cards including the Capital One SavorOne and
Wells Fargo Autograph each earn 3 percent or 3x on restaurants, which makes them
practical defaults for everyday Culver's visits and family dinners.

stores.name: Cumberland Farms
stores.intro:
Cumberland Farms is a Northeast U.S. convenience and gas chain with about 600 stores
across New England, New York, Florida, and parts of the Mid-Atlantic, now operated by
EG Group. Fuel at Cumberland Farms pumps codes as gas, so any gas category bonus card
will earn its multiplier. The chain's signature SmartPay program is an ACH-linked
debit option offering a flat 10 cents per gallon discount, which generally beats most
credit-card gas rewards in absolute cents-per-gallon terms but does not earn credit
card points.

stores.name: Curology
stores.intro:
Curology is a direct-to-consumer skincare brand and telehealth service that pairs
customers with licensed providers to create custom prescription-strength formulas for
acne, anti-aging, and other skin concerns, shipped on a recurring subscription. There is
no Curology co-brand credit card, so a general online shopping or subscription-friendly
flat-rate cashback card is the right way to earn on monthly shipments.

stores.name: CVS
stores.intro:
CVS is the largest U.S. pharmacy chain by store count (about 9,000 locations) and a
major convenience-goods retailer alongside the prescription business. CVS codes as a
drugstore on every card we track, so any 3-5% drugstore-category card will earn here.
CVS's own ExtraCare loyalty program runs in parallel and stacks with card cashback.

stores.name: Daily Harvest
stores.intro: Daily Harvest delivers frozen smoothies, harvest bowls and plant-based meals on a subscription basis. Because everything is ordered through its website, charges usually code as online shopping or general retail rather than groceries, so a card with an online or everyday-spending bonus is typically the strongest pick.

stores.name: Dairy Queen
stores.intro:
Dairy Queen is the Berkshire Hathaway-owned QSR with more than 4,300 US locations split
between traditional treat-only stores and full-menu DQ Grill & Chill outlets serving Blizzards,
soft-serve cones, dipped cones, burgers, and chicken strip baskets. Whether you order at a
walk-up window, drive-thru, or through the DQ Rewards mobile app, the transaction codes as
dining/restaurants. Cards with strong dining multipliers (Amex Gold at 4x, Chase Sapphire
Preferred at 3x, Capital One SavorOne at 3% cash) all earn at the full bonus rate on DQ.

stores.name: David's Bridal
stores.intro:
David's Bridal is one of the largest wedding retailers in the United States, selling
bridal gowns, bridesmaid dresses, prom wear, and wedding accessories through hundreds of
stores and its online catalog. The chain has gone through multiple bankruptcy
reorganizations in recent years but continues operating as a go-to option for
budget-conscious wedding parties. There's no David's Bridal co-brand credit card, so
shoppers outfitting a wedding party should reach for a card that earns well on clothing
or general online shopping purchases, since a single order can easily run into four
figures once alterations and accessories are included.

stores.name: DC Shoes
stores.intro:
DC Shoes is a skate and snow footwear and apparel brand under the Boardriders group, sold
direct at dcshoes.com and through skate, sporting, and department stores. There is no DC
Shoes co-brand credit card, so purchases earn the most on a card with a clothing or online
shopping category bonus.

stores.name: Decathlon
stores.intro:
Decathlon is a French sporting-goods giant expanding across the United States, selling
affordable gear for dozens of sports through its growing store footprint and decathlon.com.
There is no Decathlon co-brand credit card, so purchases earn on whatever card you use. A
card that pays a bonus on sporting goods or on general online shopping is the best fit for
both in-store and online orders.

stores.name: Del Taco
stores.intro: Del Taco is a California-born quick-service chain offering both Mexican-style tacos and American burgers and fries, with crinkle-cut fries as a signature. Orders code as dining or restaurants on most cards, so use whatever dining bonus you carry. A drive-thru tap stays in the dining category, while third-party delivery may code to that app instead.

stores.name: Dell
stores.intro: Dell sells laptops, desktops, monitors and accessories directly through Dell.com, often configured to order. Purchases here generally code as online shopping or electronics retail, so a card with an online or general retail bonus earns well, and shopping through a card issuer's portal can layer on extra rewards.

stores.name: DeLonghi
stores.intro:
DeLonghi is an Italian appliance maker best known in the U.S. for espresso and coffee
machines, along with air fryers, heaters, and small kitchen appliances sold through its
own site, Amazon, and major retailers like Williams Sonoma and Best Buy. The brand
positions itself at the premium end of home espresso equipment, competing with names like
Breville and Jura. There's no DeLonghi co-brand credit card, so a purchase direct from
delonghi.com is best charged to a card that earns a bonus on home goods or general online
shopping, or simply a strong flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Delsey Paris
stores.intro:
Delsey Paris is a French luggage maker dating back to 1946, known for hardside spinner
suitcases, packing cubes, and travel accessories sold through department stores,
specialty luggage retailers, and its own site. There is no Delsey co-brand credit card,
so shoppers buying luggage should use a general online shopping rewards card, or a travel
card if they'd rather earn points toward their next trip instead.

stores.name: Delta Air Lines
stores.intro:
Delta Air Lines runs one of the strongest U.S. domestic networks and the SkyMiles
loyalty program. Delta tickets code as airfare across all major cards. Co-branded
SkyMiles Amex cards add extra miles per dollar on Delta plus perks like a free
checked bag, Sky Club access (Reserve only), and Medallion-status boost spend.
General travel cards usually beat them on raw cash-equivalent value, but the
Reserve's lounge access and MQD-waiver path can swing the math for frequent flyers.
OTA bookings still earn miles when your SkyMiles number is attached.

stores.name: Denny's
stores.intro:
Denny's operates roughly 1,500 US diners famous for 24-hour service, the Grand Slam breakfast,
Moons Over My Hammy, and a value-driven all-day menu that draws road-trippers, third-shift
workers, and late-night crowds. Most Denny's are highway-adjacent or in travel-corridor
suburbs. Denny's transactions code as dining/restaurants on every credit card, including
orders through the Denny's Rewards app and Denny's On Demand delivery portal. Dining-bonus
cards earn full rate: Amex Gold at 4x, Chase Sapphire Preferred at 3x, Capital One SavorOne 3%.

stores.name: Dermalogica
stores.intro:
Dermalogica is a professional-grade skincare brand founded in Los Angeles and long
associated with spas and licensed estheticians before expanding into direct-to-consumer
sales online. Its product lines focus on cleansers, serums, and treatments built around
ingredient-driven formulas rather than mass-market drugstore positioning. There's no
Dermalogica co-brand credit card, so shoppers buying direct from dermalogica.com should
lean on a card that earns a solid rate on online shopping purchases or a flat-rate
cashback card, since the brand doesn't run its own store-branded financing or rewards
program.

stores.name: Dermstore
stores.intro:
Dermstore is an online beauty and skincare retailer that curates dermatologist-recommended
and prestige skincare brands, from SkinCeuticals and Obagi to smaller clinical labels that
aren't always available at mainstream beauty retailers. It's owned by Target Corporation
but operates as a standalone online storefront rather than being sold in Target stores.
There's no Dermstore co-brand credit card, so shoppers should use a card that earns well on
online shopping purchases or a flat-rate cashback card when checking out, since Dermstore
itself doesn't offer branded financing.

stores.name: DEWALT
stores.intro:
DEWALT is one of the largest power tool and outdoor equipment brands sold in the United
States, covering cordless drills, saws, batteries, and job site accessories aimed at both
tradespeople and serious DIYers. It's owned by Stanley Black & Decker and sold through big
box retailers like Home Depot and Lowe's as well as its own online store. There's no DEWALT
co-brand credit card, so shoppers buying direct at dewalt.com are best served by a card
that earns a strong rate on home improvement purchases or a flat-rate cashback card, since
DEWALT itself doesn't run its own rewards program on card spend.

stores.name: DHgate
stores.intro:
DHgate is a Chinese business-to-business and business-to-consumer marketplace that connects
U.S. shoppers directly with manufacturers and wholesalers across categories like electronics,
apparel, home goods, and gadgets, similar in spirit to AliExpress or Alibaba's consumer arm.
Prices tend to be low and shipping times longer since most goods ship from overseas sellers.
There's no DHgate co-brand credit card, so the best approach is a card that earns a solid
flat rate or bonus category on general online shopping, since purchases typically code as
a marketplace or general merchandise transaction rather than a specific retail category.

stores.name: Diane von Furstenberg
stores.intro:
Diane von Furstenberg is an American fashion house best known for the wrap dress it
popularized in the 1970s, now selling ready-to-wear, accessories, and footwear through its
own boutiques, DVF.com, and department stores like Nordstrom and Bloomingdale's. The brand
remains privately held and is closely tied to its founder and namesake designer. There's no
DVF co-brand credit card, so shoppers buying direct should use a card that earns well on
clothing or general online shopping purchases, or lean on a department-store card if the
purchase runs through Nordstrom or a similar retail partner instead.

stores.name: Dick's Sporting Goods
stores.intro:
Dick's Sporting Goods is the largest U.S. sporting goods retailer with about
720 namesake stores plus Public Lands, Field & Stream, Going Going Gone, and
Golf Galaxy banners. In-store charges code as department stores; online orders
code as online shopping. The free ScoreCard loyalty program issues 1 point per
$1 plus periodic 2-3x bonus events redeemable for store credit, stackable with
card bonuses. There's a Dick's-branded Synchrony credit card with 8% in-store
cashback for cardholders.

stores.name: Dickies
stores.intro:
Dickies is a workwear brand dating back to 1922, known for durable work pants, coveralls,
and jackets that have also become a streetwear staple well beyond its original job site
audience. It's owned by VF Corporation and sells through its own site, Amazon, and retailers
like Zappos and Tractor Supply alongside dedicated Dickies stores. There's no Dickies
co-brand credit card, so a purchase at dickies.com is best charged to a card that earns
well on clothing or general online shopping, or a flat-rate cashback card for simplicity.

stores.name: Diesel
stores.intro:
Diesel is an Italian fashion brand best known for premium denim, along with apparel,
footwear, and accessories sold through its own boutiques, Diesel.com, and department
stores. It's part of the OTB Group, the same holding company behind Maison Margiela and
Marni. There's no Diesel co-brand credit card, so shoppers buying jeans or apparel direct
from the brand should use a card that earns a strong rate on clothing or general online
shopping purchases, since Diesel itself doesn't extend its own store financing or rewards.

stores.name: DIFF Eyewear
stores.intro:
DIFF Eyewear is a direct-to-consumer sunglasses and optical brand built around a one-for-one
giving model, where each pair purchased helps fund a pair of prescription glasses or eye
care for someone in need through partner nonprofits. It sells trend-forward, affordably
priced frames online and through select retail partners rather than running its own
physical storefronts. There's no DIFF Eyewear co-brand credit card, so shoppers should lean
on a card that rewards general online shopping purchases or a flat-rate cashback card when
buying frames directly from the brand's website.

stores.name: Dillard's
stores.intro:
Dillard's is a mid-tier to upscale department store chain with about 270 locations
primarily in the South, Midwest, and Sun Belt. The merchandise mix leans heavily into
apparel, shoes, cosmetics, and home goods. Dillard's codes as a department store, so
any department-store category bonus card will earn its multiplier. The Dillard's
American Express card (issued by Wells Fargo) offers elevated rewards on Dillard's
purchases plus general earn elsewhere, and Dillard's also issues a closed-loop store
card with lower earn but more accessible approval.

stores.name: Dinnerly
stores.intro:
Dinnerly is a budget-focused meal kit delivery service operated by Marley Spoon, positioned
as a lower-cost alternative to HelloFresh or Blue Apron by trimming packaging and recipe
complexity to keep per-serving prices down. Subscribers pick weekly recipes online and
receive pre-portioned ingredients by mail. There's no Dinnerly co-brand credit card, so the
weekly or monthly charge is best routed through a card that earns a bonus on dining or
online grocery-adjacent purchases, since meal kit charges typically code closer to food
delivery than standard retail.

affiliate.offer: up to $185 off your first 5 boxes

stores.name: Discount Tire
stores.intro:
Discount Tire, doing business as America's Tire in California, is the largest
independent tire and wheel retailer in the United States with more than 1,200
stores. The chain sells passenger, truck, performance, and trailer tires plus wheels
and TPMS service. Tire purchases generally code as auto services or general retail
rather than as a dedicated bonus category on most cards.

Cards with selectable categories such as Citi Custom Cash and Bank of America
Customized Cash can reach tire purchases when the right category is active. A new
set of four tires plus installation often runs $800 to $1,500, which makes
Discount Tire useful for clearing signup bonus minimums on cards like the Wells
Fargo Autograph or Chase Freedom Unlimited.

stores.name: Disney+
stores.intro:
Disney+ is The Walt Disney Company's streaming service, with Disney/Pixar/Marvel/
Star Wars/National Geographic content. Monthly subscriptions code as streaming on
every card we track. Streaming-bonus cards (Blue Cash Preferred 6%, U.S. Bank
Cash+ 5% on selected categories) are the strongest pairings. Disney+ is also
bundled with Hulu and ESPN+ as the Disney Bundle, often the cheapest path to
all three services.

stores.name: DJI
stores.intro:
DJI is the dominant global maker of consumer and prosumer drones, alongside a growing line
of stabilized cameras, gimbals, and action cams like the Osmo Pocket and Osmo Action. The
Chinese company sells directly through its own online store as well as electronics
retailers such as Best Buy and B&H. There's no DJI co-brand credit card, so buyers ordering
direct from dji.com are best off using a card that earns a bonus rate on electronics
purchases or a strong flat-rate cashback card, since gear here tends to run into the
hundreds or thousands of dollars.

stores.name: Do it Best
stores.intro: Do it Best is a member-owned hardware and lumber cooperative serving thousands of independent stores across the US, so a purchase usually codes under home improvement or hardware. Orders placed through doitbest.com generally code as online shopping, while in-store buys at a local affiliate often fall to your flat-rate earn rate. A card with a home improvement bonus, or a strong everyday card, fits these project runs best.

stores.name: Dollar General
stores.intro:
Dollar General is a U.S. discount retail chain with about 20,000 domestic stores,
often the only general-merchandise option in rural ZIP codes. Codes as discount
stores or department stores depending on the card. The DG Rewards loyalty program
in the app issues digital coupons and frequent buyer rewards on top of any
card's bonus rate.

stores.name: Dollar Rent A Car
stores.intro: Dollar Rent A Car is a budget brand within the Hertz family aimed at cost-conscious renters. Its charges code as car rental, part of the travel umbrella, and earn best on cards with a travel or rental-car category bonus. A travel card that also includes rental collision coverage can let you skip the expensive counter insurance, so review your card's benefits first.

stores.name: Dollar Shave Club
stores.intro: Dollar Shave Club pioneered the razor-by-mail model and now ships grooming and personal-care products on a flexible subscription. Charges code as online shopping rather than groceries or drugstore, so a card with an online-purchase bonus earns the most. Adjusting your delivery cadence to batch items into one shipment can concentrate the rewards on a single charge.

stores.name: Dollar Tree
stores.intro:
Dollar Tree is a U.S. discount retail chain with about 8,000 domestic stores,
selling household goods, snacks, and party supplies at fixed price points.
Codes as discount stores or department stores depending on the card; some banks
classify it as grocery in mixed-merchandise transactions. Average ticket sizes
are very small, so the absolute earn from any bonus card is modest. A flat-rate
2% cashback card is usually the cleanest pairing.

stores.name: Domino's
stores.intro:
Domino's is the largest U.S. pizza chain at about 6,800 locations and a heavy digital
ordering footprint. All Domino's transactions — phone, app, and dominos.com — code as
dining on every card we track, regardless of whether the food is delivered or picked up.
The Domino's Rewards program (free pizza after a points threshold) stacks on top of card
cashback.

stores.name: Dooney & Bourke
stores.intro:
Dooney & Bourke is an American leather goods maker founded in 1975, known for handbags,
wallets, and accessories sold through its own stores and website as well as department
stores like Macy's and Nordstrom, and outlet locations. The brand sits in the accessible
luxury tier, priced above mass-market handbag brands but below European designer houses.
There's no Dooney & Bourke co-brand credit card, so a direct purchase is best charged to a
card that earns well on online shopping, while purchases made through a department store
can also benefit from that retailer's own category bonus.

stores.name: DoorDash
stores.intro:
DoorDash is the largest U.S. food-delivery service by market share, covering both
restaurant delivery and (via DashMart and grocery partners) household goods. Restaurant
orders code as dining/restaurants on every card we track, and several cards specifically
call out DoorDash for elevated dining bonuses or DashPass perks. DashMart and
grocery-partner orders typically code as the underlying merchant rather than as dining.

stores.name: Dorothy Perkins
stores.intro:
Dorothy Perkins is a British women's fashion brand selling affordable everyday clothing,
from workwear to occasionwear, primarily through its online store after the closure of its
UK high street locations. It now operates as an online-only retailer shipping to customers
in the UK and select international markets including the U.S. There's no Dorothy Perkins
co-brand credit card, so shoppers ordering from the site should rely on a card that earns
well on general online shopping and, if applicable, one with favorable foreign transaction
terms since charges may process through a UK-based merchant account.

stores.name: Dr. Scholl's
stores.intro:
Dr. Scholl's is a long-running American footwear and foot care brand, historically known
for insoles and foot health products and now selling its own line of comfort-focused shoes
and sandals direct-to-consumer online as well as through department stores and shoe
retailers. The foot care side of the brand, including insoles and callus treatments, is
licensed separately and sold mainly in drugstores. There's no Dr. Scholl's co-brand credit
card, so a shoe purchase at drschollsshoes.com is best charged to a card that earns well on
footwear or general online shopping purchases.

stores.name: Drizly
stores.intro: Drizly is an on-demand alcohol delivery marketplace, now owned by Uber, connecting shoppers with local liquor stores. Charges typically code as online shopping or as the fulfilling retailer rather than as dining or groceries. A flat-rate card or one rewarding online purchases is the most reliable way to earn on quick delivery orders.

stores.name: Drunk Elephant
stores.intro:
Drunk Elephant is a skincare brand built around a "clean" formulation philosophy that
avoids ingredients like essential oils, silicones, and certain fragrances, popular with a
younger, social-media-driven audience. It's owned by Shiseido but sells direct through its
own website as well as Sephora and Ulta. There's no Drunk Elephant co-brand credit card, so
a purchase made directly on the brand's site is best charged to a card that earns well on
online shopping, while a Sephora or Ulta purchase can instead take advantage of that
retailer's own beauty rewards program.

stores.name: Drury Hotels
stores.intro:
Drury Hotels is a privately held family-owned chain operating about 150 mid-tier hotels
concentrated across the Midwest, South, and Southwest under the Drury Inn, Drury Inn & Suites,
Drury Plaza Hotel, and Pear Tree Inn brands. The chain is best known for the 5:30 Kickback
hot food and drinks reception and free hot breakfast included with every stay. Drury charges
code as lodging/hotels on every card. Cards with hotel bonuses (Chase Sapphire Reserve 4x on
direct, Amex Platinum 5x on Amex Travel prepaid, Capital One Venture X 2x base) earn full rate.

stores.name: DSW
stores.intro:
DSW, short for Designer Shoe Warehouse, is the flagship footwear retailer of Designer
Brands Inc, with around 500 U.S. stores and dsw.com. The DSW VIP loyalty program is
free, offers tiered rewards and a birthday gift, and stacks with credit card earnings.
The DSW Visa from Comenity adds elevated points on DSW spend and a base earn rate
everywhere else, while general purpose cards code DSW as department store in person
and online shopping on the web.

stores.name: Duane Reade
stores.intro:
Duane Reade is the New York City banner of Walgreens, with roughly 250 locations
concentrated in the five boroughs and surrounding suburbs. The format is essentially
a Walgreens drugstore adapted to dense urban real estate, often with a heavier prepared
food and convenience selection. Duane Reade codes as a drugstore, so any drugstore
category bonus card (Amex Blue Cash Preferred, Citi Custom Cash on drugstores) will
earn its full multiplier. The myWalgreens loyalty program works at Duane Reade
registers as well.

stores.name: Dunkin'
stores.intro:
Dunkin' is a U.S. coffee-and-donut chain with about 9,000 domestic locations.
Charges code as dining on every card we track, so any restaurant-bonus card
applies — Amex Gold's 4x dining (capped), Capital One SavorOne's 3%, Sapphire
Preferred's 3x. Heavy regulars also use the free DD Perks/Dunkin' Rewards app
for free drinks and bonus-point promotions, which stack on top of card bonuses.

stores.name: Dutch Bros
stores.intro:
Dutch Bros Coffee has grown from a single Oregon pushcart in 1992 to more than 950 drive-thru
coffee shops across roughly 18 states, with a menu that leans heavily on flavored Rebel
energy drinks, blended Freezes, and customizable lattes. Every Dutch Bros purchase codes as
dining/restaurants regardless of whether you swipe in the drive-thru lane or reload your
Dutch Rewards account in the app. Cards with strong dining multipliers (Amex Gold at 4x,
Capital One SavorOne at 3%, Chase Sapphire Preferred at 3x) all earn full bonus rewards.

stores.name: Dyson
stores.intro:
Dyson is a British technology company known for its vacuums, air purifiers, hair-care tools,
and fans, sold direct at dyson.com, in Dyson Demo stores, and through retailers like Best
Buy and department stores. There is no Dyson co-brand credit card. Dyson products carry high
price tags but rarely go on deep discount, so shoppers usually earn the most with a card
that has a strong online shopping multiplier, or by buying at a retailer whose own category
bonus applies.

stores.name: e.l.f. Cosmetics
stores.intro: e.l.f. Cosmetics built a following on affordable, cruelty-free makeup, and orders at elfcosmetics.com typically code as online shopping or general retail. With most items priced in the single digits, the brand leans heavily on volume and its own loyalty program. Since beauty has no rewards multiplier, a card rewarding online spend or a solid flat rate gives the best return.

stores.name: Eagle Creek
stores.intro:
Eagle Creek is a travel gear brand best known for packing cubes, duffels, and rugged
backpacks aimed at frequent and adventure travelers, sold direct online as well as through
outdoor and luggage retailers. The brand went through a bankruptcy closure in 2020 before
being relaunched under new ownership. There's no Eagle Creek co-brand credit card, so
shoppers stocking up on packing gear should use a card that earns well on general online
shopping, or consider a travel-focused card since the purchases directly support upcoming
trips even if they don't code as a travel merchant.

stores.name: Eastern Mountain Sports
stores.intro:
Eastern Mountain Sports, widely known as EMS, is a New England-founded outdoor retailer
selling hiking, camping, and climbing gear along with technical apparel from both in-house
and name-brand labels. The chain has changed hands several times and scaled back its store
footprint in recent years while continuing to operate online. There's no EMS co-brand
credit card, so shoppers stocking up on outdoor gear should use a card that earns a bonus
on sporting goods purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card if their spending doesn't fit a
dedicated category.

stores.name: eBay
stores.intro:
eBay is the largest U.S. online auction and resale marketplace and codes as online
shopping on essentially every credit card we track. Unlike Amazon, eBay rarely has a
card-specific bonus carve-out, so the right card here is the strongest generic
online-shopping earner you carry. Watch for cards with a U.S. online retail cap — eBay
payments via PayPal still count as eBay for category coding, but third-party processor
purchases on eBay's classifieds side may not.

stores.name: ECCO
stores.intro:
ECCO is a Danish footwear company known for comfort-engineered leather shoes and boots,
manufactured largely through its own tanneries rather than outsourced production, which is
unusual for a shoe brand at its scale. It sells through its own retail stores, ecco.com,
and department stores and shoe retailers worldwide. There's no ECCO co-brand credit card,
so a direct purchase from ecco.com is best charged to a card that earns a bonus on
footwear or general online shopping purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card for simplicity.

stores.name: ecobee
stores.intro:
Ecobee is a Canadian smart-home company best known for its Wi-Fi thermostats with
built-in room sensors and voice-assistant support, competing directly with Google's Nest
in the connected-thermostat category. The company, now owned by generator maker Generac,
has expanded into smart cameras, sensors, and a smart doorbell. There's no ecobee co-brand
credit card, so an electronics category card or a general online shopping rewards card is
the better fit for purchases made through ecobee.com.

stores.name: Eddie Bauer
stores.intro:
Eddie Bauer is an outdoor and casual apparel brand now under Sparc Group, with a long
catalog heritage, roughly 250 stores and outlets, and a steady online business at
eddiebauer.com. Direct purchases typically code as department store in person and
online shopping for web orders. The free Adventure Rewards loyalty program offers
points and birthday perks, but there is no current Eddie Bauer branded credit card in
the U.S. market.

stores.name: Edible Arrangements
stores.intro: Edible Arrangements builds fresh-fruit bouquets and chocolate-dipped treats sold for delivery and pickup. Despite the food angle, orders usually code as online shopping rather than as groceries or dining, so those bonuses will not apply. Reach for a flat-rate card or one that rewards online retail when ordering gift arrangements.

stores.name: Eileen Fisher
stores.intro:
Eileen Fisher is an American women's clothing brand known for minimalist, relaxed-fit
pieces made from natural fibers like organic cotton, linen, and wool, sold through its own
boutiques, website, and select department stores. The company has also built a reputation
around sustainability, including a take-back and resale program for used garments. There's
no Eileen Fisher co-brand credit card, so a purchase direct from eileenfisher.com is best
charged to a card that earns well on clothing or general online shopping purchases.

stores.name: Einstein Bros. Bagels
stores.intro:
Einstein Bros. Bagels operates about 700 US bakery-cafes serving fresh-baked bagels, shmears,
egg sandwiches, and coffee, with most locations in college towns, hospital campuses, and
suburban shopping centers. The chain is owned by Bagel Brands alongside Bruegger's. Every
Einstein Bros. purchase codes as dining/restaurants, including orders placed through the
Einstein Bros. Rewards app. Cards with strong breakfast and dining bonuses (Amex Gold at 4x,
Chase Sapphire Preferred at 3x, Capital One SavorOne at 3%) all earn at full multiplier.

stores.name: El Pollo Loco
stores.intro: El Pollo Loco is a Southwest fast-casual chain centered on citrus-marinated, fire-grilled chicken served with tortillas and salsas. Spending codes as dining or restaurants on most rewards cards, so a dining bonus is the right play. Catering placed through a separate corporate portal can occasionally code differently from an in-store order.

stores.name: Emirates
stores.intro: Emirates is Dubai's long-haul carrier, known for its A380 fleet, premium cabins, and connections from US gateways to the Middle East, Asia, and Africa. Airfare codes as an airline or travel purchase, ideal for a card with an airline or travel bonus. A US co-branded Emirates Skywards card exists, and transferable points from major issuers can also fund Skywards awards.

stores.name: Enterprise
stores.intro:
Enterprise is the largest U.S. car-rental company by fleet, owner of National and
Alamo. Rentals code as car rentals on every card we track. Status matches into
Enterprise Plus are less common than at Hertz/Avis, so most cardholders pair
Enterprise with a general travel card for the bonus on car rentals plus secondary
rental insurance. Booking through a card's travel portal earns the portal multiplier
but may not credit Enterprise Plus points.

stores.name: Epic Games Store
stores.intro: The Epic Games Store sells PC titles and is known for giving away free games every week, plus a lower revenue cut that draws exclusives. Spending codes as online shopping or digital entertainment, so a card with an online-purchase bonus is the natural choice. Watch for its periodic coupon drops, which often stack on top of whatever rewards your card pays.

stores.name: Equinox
stores.intro: Equinox positions itself as a premium fitness brand with high-end clubs, spa amenities and steep monthly dues. Because gym memberships rarely qualify for a bonus, those dues generally earn at the flat rate, though a few premium cards bundle wellness or fitness credits that can meaningfully offset the cost.

stores.name: ESPN+
stores.intro: ESPN+ is Disney's sports-streaming add-on, offering exclusive games, UFC pay-per-views, and deep coverage beyond cable ESPN. The subscription codes as streaming, making a card with a streaming bonus the best earner, with others defaulting to flat-rate. Bundling it with Disney+ and Hulu can lower the combined price while still charging to your streaming-category card.

stores.name: Estee Lauder
stores.intro:
Estee Lauder is the flagship prestige beauty brand of The Estee Lauder Companies, selling
skincare, makeup, and fragrance through department stores, its own boutiques, and its
direct-to-consumer website, alongside sister brands like Clinique, MAC, and Bobbi Brown
under the same corporate umbrella. There's no Estee Lauder co-brand credit card, so a
purchase direct from esteelauder.com is best charged to a card that earns well on general
online shopping, while a department store purchase can instead lean on that store's own
category bonus if one applies.

stores.name: Ethan Allen
stores.intro: Ethan Allen sells higher-end American furniture and interior-design services through design centers that tend to code as general retail or a furniture store. Because it is not a hardware warehouse, home-improvement bonuses may not trigger on a purchase here. Online orders at ethanallen.com generally code as online shopping, and given the large tickets a strong flat-rate card pays off.

stores.name: Etihad Airways
stores.intro:
Etihad Airways is the national airline of the United Arab Emirates, based in Abu Dhabi,
operating long-haul routes that connect the U.S. and Europe to the Middle East, South Asia,
and beyond, often positioned alongside Emirates and Qatar Airways as one of the major Gulf
carriers. Its loyalty program, Etihad Guest, is not directly tied to any U.S. co-brand
credit card, so American travelers booking flights should instead rely on a premium travel
rewards card that earns bonus points on airfare and offers strong transfer partners, or a
flat-rate travel card that can be redeemed broadly for flight purchases.

stores.name: Etsy
stores.intro:
Etsy is the marketplace for handmade, vintage, and craft goods sold by independent
makers, with checkout handled by Etsy itself. Purchases code as online shopping on every
general-purpose card we track, which means Etsy is a good fit for any card with a flat
U.S. online retail bonus (Blue Cash Everyday, Hilton Surpass, etc.). Etsy doesn't have a
co-branded card and rarely shows up in card-specific bonus lists.

stores.name: Eventbrite
stores.intro:
Eventbrite is a self-service ticketing and event-registration platform used by everyone
from local meetup organizers to concert promoters and conference producers, letting anyone
create a listing and sell tickets without going through a traditional box office. It's
distinct from resale marketplaces like StubHub or SeatGeek in that most listings are
primary-market tickets sold directly by the event organizer. Eventbrite has no co-brand
credit card, so the best approach is a card that earns bonus rewards on entertainment
purchases, or a broad travel or dining card if entertainment isn't a defined category,
since ticket purchases there are typically coded as entertainment spend.

stores.name: EveryPlate
stores.intro: EveryPlate markets itself as a budget-friendly meal-kit service, also under the HelloFresh umbrella, with low per-serving pricing. Orders bill online and tend to code as online shopping or general merchandise instead of groceries, so the best approach is a card that rewards online or flat-rate spending rather than supermarket purchases.

stores.name: Expedia
stores.intro:
Expedia is one of the largest online travel agencies, owned by Expedia Group
(which also owns Hotels.com, Vrbo, and Travelocity). Bookings code as travel on
every card we track. The honest catch: hotel and airline programs typically do
NOT credit Expedia bookings toward elite-night or elite-qualifying-mile counts,
and many properties exclude OTA stays from earning their own loyalty points —
so booking direct usually wins for status-conscious travelers. The Expedia
Rewards Cards from Citi layer extra OneKeyCash on Expedia-family bookings.

stores.name: Express
stores.intro:
Express is a mall-based apparel chain known for workwear and going out looks for men
and women, operating through a mix of full-line stores, Express Factory Outlet, and
express.com after the brand emerged from Chapter 11 restructuring. The Express Next
store credit card from Comenity earns A-List points on every purchase and pairs with
the free loyalty tier. General-purpose cards typically code Express as department
store at the register and online shopping for web orders.

stores.name: Extended Stay America
stores.intro:
Extended Stay America operates more than 600 budget long-stay hotels across the US, each
room equipped with a full kitchen, dishware, and weekly housekeeping options, targeting
business travelers, contractors, and relocating families who need stays of a week or longer.
The chain also runs Extended Stay America Suites and Extended Stay America Premier Suites.
ESA charges code as lodging/hotels on every credit card. Hotel-bonus cards earn full rate:
Chase Sapphire Reserve at 4x direct, Capital One Venture X at 2x base, Amex Platinum at 5x prepaid.

stores.name: ExxonMobil
stores.intro:
ExxonMobil operates Exxon and Mobil-branded stations at about 11,500 U.S. locations,
plus the ExxonMobil Rewards+ app for in-station discounts. ExxonMobil pumps code as gas
on every card we track, and one merchant-specific note: ExxonMobil purchases earn 3% on
the Apple Card when paid via Apple Pay.

stores.name: FabFitFun
stores.intro: FabFitFun is a seasonal subscription box delivering beauty, wellness, and lifestyle products, billed quarterly or annually to members. The recurring charge codes as online shopping, so a card with an online-purchase bonus is the right pick, with others earning flat-rate. Paying the annual plan up front lowers the per-box cost while still earning the online bonus on one larger charge.

stores.name: Fabletics
stores.intro:
Fabletics is an athleisure brand co-founded by Kate Hudson that sells leggings, sports
bras, and activewear through a VIP membership model, where members get discounted pricing
in exchange for a recurring monthly commitment unless they skip. It's part of TechStyle
Fashion Group alongside sister brands like JustFab and ShoeDazzle. There's no Fabletics
co-brand credit card, so shoppers are better off leaning on a card that rewards online
shopping or general retail purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card, since apparel spend
there doesn't fall into a specialized bonus category on most issuers' rewards charts.

stores.name: Factor
stores.intro: Factor sells fully prepared, ready-to-heat meals on a weekly subscription and is part of the HelloFresh family. Despite the food angle, these orders bill online and usually code as online shopping or general merchandise rather than groceries, so a card with an online or everyday-spending bonus tends to earn the most.

stores.name: Family Dollar
stores.intro:
Family Dollar is a discount variety chain with roughly 8,000 stores across the U.S.,
owned by Dollar Tree (which announced plans to sell the banner in 2025). The format
carries a mix of groceries, household basics, apparel, and seasonal goods at low price
points. Family Dollar typically codes as a discount store or department store for
credit card networks, not as a supermarket, so a grocery rewards card will generally
not earn its bonus here. A department-store or general 1.5-2% cash back card is the
practical pick.

stores.name: Famous Footwear
stores.intro:
Famous Footwear is the family footwear chain of Caleres Inc, with about 850 U.S.
stores and famousfootwear.com, selling athletic and casual shoes from Nike, Adidas,
Skechers, and others. The free Famously You Rewards program offers tiered cashback
and a birthday gift, layering on top of credit card rewards. Direct purchases
typically code as department store at the register and online shopping for web
orders, and there is no current branded credit card.

stores.name: Fandango
stores.intro: Fandango sells advance movie tickets for most major US theater chains, plus gift cards and digital rentals through Vudu. Ticket purchases generally code as entertainment, so a card with an entertainment category bonus earns the most. Some rewards cards once treated Fandango as a movie-specific perk, but for most buyers it simply rewards an entertainment-bonus card today.

stores.name: Farberware
stores.intro:
Farberware is a longtime American cookware and small kitchen appliance brand, known for
stainless steel and nonstick pots, pans, bakeware, and countertop gadgets sold through its
own site and major housewares retailers. The brand dates back over a century and today
operates under license by different manufacturers depending on the product line. There is
no Farberware co-brand credit card, so a shopper buying cookware there is best served by a
card that bonuses home goods or department-store purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card
if no specialized category applies to the specific charge.

stores.name: FedEx Office
stores.intro:
FedEx Office, formerly Kinko's, operates around 2,000 U.S. retail locations
offering shipping, printing, copying, signage, packing, and computer rental. Most
FedEx Office stores code as office supplies or stationery on Visa, Mastercard, and
Amex networks, which makes spending eligible for office supply bonus categories on
small business cards.

The Chase Ink Business Cash pays 5 percent on the first $25,000 in combined office
supplies and select utilities each year, making it the standard pick for printing
and shipping spend. The Amex Business Gold also includes U.S. office supply
retailers as a top-two category. Personal cards with selectable office supply
buckets, including U.S. Bank Cash+, can match those rates.

stores.name: Fender
stores.intro:
Fender is one of the most recognizable names in musical instruments, best known for the
Stratocaster and Telecaster electric guitars, along with basses, amplifiers, and the
Squier budget line it also owns. Beyond instruments, Fender has expanded into the Fender
Play learning app and audio accessories. There is no Fender co-brand credit card, so a
purchase there is general retail spend best matched to a card that rewards online shopping
or department-store categories, or a flat-rate cashback card for a big-ticket instrument
or amp purchase where no specialty bonus applies.

stores.name: Fenty Beauty
stores.intro:
Fenty Beauty is the cosmetics line founded by Rihanna in 2017, widely credited with
pushing the beauty industry toward broader foundation shade ranges and now sold both
direct-to-consumer and through retailers like Sephora. It operates under LVMH's Kendo
Brands portfolio. There is no Fenty Beauty co-brand credit card, so shoppers should lean
on a card that earns bonus rewards on online shopping or drugstore-adjacent beauty
purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card, and remember that buying through Sephora instead
of fentybeauty.com may open up Sephora-specific card benefits.

stores.name: Ferguson
stores.intro:
Ferguson is the largest U.S. wholesale distributor of plumbing, HVAC, lighting,
appliances, and waterworks products, with over 1,600 locations including Ferguson
Bath, Kitchen & Lighting Gallery showrooms. Many Ferguson locations code as home
improvement on credit card networks, which makes large remodel and new construction
purchases eligible for home improvement bonus categories.

Cards like the U.S. Bank Cash+ with home improvement selected and the Bank of
America Customized Cash can earn 3 to 5 percent on Ferguson invoices. Kitchen and
bath renovations frequently run five figures, which makes the retailer useful for
clearing premium signup bonus minimums on the Capital One Venture X or the Chase
Sapphire Reserve.

stores.name: Finish Line
stores.intro: Finish Line is an athletic footwear and apparel retailer with a large presence inside Macy's stores. Buying from its website codes as online shopping or retail, where a portal or rotating bonus can add value, while in-store sneaker purchases generally earn the flat base rate.

stores.name: Firehouse Subs
stores.intro:
Firehouse Subs is a Jacksonville-based sub sandwich chain with over 1,200
restaurants across the United States, owned by Restaurant Brands International
alongside Burger King and Popeyes. The chain is known for steamed meat subs like
the Hook & Ladder and Italian, plus the Firehouse Subs Public Safety Foundation
that funds first responder equipment. Transactions code as restaurants on most
credit card networks.

The Amex Gold earns 4x on U.S. restaurants, the highest baseline rate available
on Firehouse Subs orders, while the Chase Sapphire Reserve pays 3x on dining. No
annual fee dining picks include the Capital One SavorOne at 3 percent and Wells
Fargo Autograph at 3x. The Firehouse Rewards program adds points that stack with
credit card cash back, and rounding up at the register supports the Foundation.

stores.name: Firestone Complete Auto Care
stores.intro: Firestone Complete Auto Care, part of Bridgestone, operates hundreds of US service centers offering tires, brakes, and full maintenance. Spending generally codes as general retail or online shopping rather than a travel or gas category, so most cards do not award a service bonus. A dependable flat-rate everyday card is usually the cleanest way to earn on tires, oil changes, and bigger repair bills.

stores.name: First Watch
stores.intro:
First Watch operates more than 540 daytime-only restaurants serving breakfast, brunch, and
lunch, with menu staples like Avocado Toast, the Chickichanga, lemon-ricotta pancakes, and
fresh-pressed juices. Locations close mid-afternoon, distinguishing First Watch from all-day
breakfast chains like IHOP and Denny's. First Watch transactions code as dining/restaurants
on every US card. Dining-bonus cards earn full rate: Amex Gold at 4x Membership Rewards,
Chase Sapphire Preferred at 3x, Chase Sapphire Reserve at 4x, Capital One SavorOne at 3% cash.

stores.name: FitFlop
stores.intro:
FitFlop is a British footwear brand built around biomechanically engineered midsoles meant
to add cushioning and comfort to sandals, sneakers, and everyday shoes, positioning itself
between a fashion label and a comfort-orthotic brand. It sells through its own site as well
as department stores and specialty shoe retailers in the U.S. There is no FitFlop co-brand
credit card, so a shopper buying shoes there should reach for a card that rewards select
clothing or online shopping purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card if footwear isn't a
bonused category on their card.

stores.name: Five Below
stores.intro:
Five Below is a discount retailer with more than 1,700 stores across the U.S., built
around items priced at $5 or less, plus a Five Beyond section with goods up to $25.
The merchandise mix skews toward tweens and teens (candy, tech accessories, beauty,
hobby, seasonal). Stores typically code as a discount or department store for credit
card networks, so a department-store category bonus card can earn its multiplier
here. Five Below does not issue its own credit card, so a general 2-5% category card
is the cleanest play.

stores.name: Five Guys
stores.intro:
Five Guys is a U.S. fast-casual burger chain with about 1,700 domestic locations,
known for hand-formed patties and unlimited toppings. Charges code as dining on
every card we track, so any dining-bonus card applies (Amex Gold 4x within cap,
Sapphire Preferred 3x, SavorOne 3%). Average ticket sizes are higher than typical
fast-food, which makes the dining multiplier more meaningful per visit.

stores.name: Fjällräven
stores.intro:
Fjällräven is a Swedish outdoor brand best known for the boxy Kanken backpack and durable,
wax-treated Greenland jacket, built on a design philosophy that favors long-lasting,
repairable gear over disposable fast fashion. It's part of the Fenix Outdoor group, which
also owns Hanwag and Primus. There is no Fjällräven co-brand credit card, so purchases
there are best matched to a card that bonuses sporting goods or outdoor retail, or a card
that rewards general online shopping, since Fjällräven's own e-commerce site is a common
way U.S. shoppers buy its gear.

stores.name: FlixBus
stores.intro: FlixBus runs an expanding green-branded intercity bus network across the US and Europe, often undercutting rail on price. Tickets generally code as transit or ground transportation and earn on cards with a transit category bonus, otherwise at flat-rate. A card that treats buses and other ground transit as a bonus category is the best fit for regular riders.

stores.name: Floor & Decor
stores.intro:
Floor & Decor is a hard surface flooring warehouse with over 240 U.S. stores
carrying tile, wood, laminate, vinyl, stone, and installation accessories at
contractor-friendly pricing. The retailer generally codes as home improvement on
Visa, Mastercard, and Amex networks, which means it can earn the home improvement
bonus on cards that include the category.

U.S. Bank Cash+ can deliver 5 percent on Floor & Decor when home improvement is one
of the two selected categories, and the Bank of America Customized Cash offers a
similar 3 percent. Floor & Decor PRO members can layer trade discounts on top of
credit card rewards, and large flooring projects easily clear signup bonus minimums.

stores.name: Florsheim
stores.intro:
Florsheim is a heritage American men's shoe brand dating to 1892, known for dress shoes,
boots, and casual leather footwear sold through its own stores, department stores, and
online. The brand has changed ownership several times over its history and today competes
in the same lane as brands like Johnston & Murphy and Cole Haan. There is no Florsheim
co-brand credit card, so a shopper buying shoes there should use a card that bonuses
select clothing or department-store spend, or a flat-rate cashback card if footwear isn't
