a specialized reward category on their card.

stores.name: Food Lion
stores.intro:
Food Lion is a Southeastern U.S. grocery chain with about 1,100 stores, owned by
Ahold Delhaize. Codes cleanly as groceries on every card we track. Strongest
pairings are Blue Cash Preferred (6% on U.S. supermarkets), the Capital One SavorOne
(3% groceries), and U.S. Bank Shopper Cash Rewards if Food Lion is one of the two
selected retailers. The MVP loyalty program runs separate digital coupons that
stack on top of any card's bonus rate.

stores.name: Foot Locker
stores.intro:
Foot Locker is the flagship sneaker and athletic apparel banner of Foot Locker Inc,
which also runs Champs Sports, Kids Foot Locker, and WSS. The chain operates about
900 U.S. stores plus footlocker.com. The FLX Rewards Visa from Bread Financial earns
elevated XPoints on FLX-family purchases that can be redeemed for sneaker drops and
shoes. General-purpose cards code Foot Locker as department store in person and online
shopping for web orders.

stores.name: Forever 21
stores.intro:
Forever 21 is a fast fashion chain operated under Sparc Group with about 380 U.S.
stores plus forever21.com after returning from bankruptcy in 2020. Pricing skews very
low so category bonuses matter less in absolute dollars, but most cards still code in
person purchases as department store and web orders as online shopping. There is no
current Forever 21 branded credit card, so the free F21 Rewards program is the only
stacking option beyond credit card rewards.

stores.name: Four Seasons
stores.intro: Four Seasons is a luxury hotel and resort operator running properties worldwide, known for high service standards and notable suites. Room charges code as a hotel or travel purchase, so a premium travel card with a strong hotel bonus shines here. Booking through a card issuer's luxury hotel program can layer perks like credits and upgrades on top of the points you earn on the stay.

stores.name: Fox Rent A Car
stores.intro:
Fox Rent A Car is a value car-rental company operating at major US airports and vacation
destinations, booked at foxrentacar.com. There is no Fox co-brand credit card, so the best
pairing is a card that earns bonus rewards on car rentals or on general travel, and many
travel cards also add secondary or primary rental car coverage when you pay with them.

stores.name: Fred Meyer
stores.intro: Fred Meyer is Kroger's Pacific Northwest superstore banner, blending a full supermarket with general merchandise, apparel, and electronics under one roof. Food and household runs almost always code as groceries on most rewards cards, so a card with a supermarket bonus is the right play. Keep in mind that big-box departments still ring up under the same grocery code at these one-stop locations.

stores.name: Freddy's
stores.intro: Freddy's Frozen Custard and Steakburgers is a Wichita-born fast-casual chain pairing thin steakburgers with made-to-order frozen custard. Spending codes as dining or restaurants on most rewards cards, so a card with a dining bonus is the best fit. Pint and quart custard packed to go still rings up under the same restaurant code as a dine-in meal.

stores.name: Free People
stores.intro:
Free People is the bohemian women's brand under URBN and includes the FP Movement
activewear line, with roughly 150 retail stores plus a sizeable online presence at
freepeople.com. Most credit cards code in-store transactions as department store and
web orders as online shopping. Free People does not currently issue a co-brand credit
card, so shoppers usually optimize with rotating category cards or a flat-rate
online-shopping multiplier.

stores.name: Freshly
stores.intro: Freshly delivered prepared, chef-cooked meals via subscription before winding down, and similar services bill through their websites rather than a supermarket register. Such orders generally code as online shopping or general retail, not groceries, so a card rewarding online or flat-rate purchases is usually the better fit.

stores.name: Frontier Airlines
stores.intro:
Frontier Airlines is a U.S. ultra-low-cost carrier with a heavy hub presence in
Denver. Base fares are very low and almost everything else (bags, seats, carry-on)
is à la carte. Tickets code as airfare on every card we track. Frontier offers
a co-branded Mastercard but no widely-recommended option, so general travel cards
are the standard pairing — and the small fares mean trip-protection benefits
rarely move the needle.

stores.name: Fry's Food Stores
stores.intro: Fry's Food Stores is Kroger's Arizona banner (no relation to the old electronics chain), running large supermarkets across Phoenix and Tucson. Grocery spending codes as supermarket on most cards, so use whatever grocery bonus you hold. Fuel at a Fry's gas station codes as gas, and many shoppers stack the chain's loyalty fuel points on top.

stores.name: FTD
stores.intro: FTD is a long-running floral and gift delivery network that fulfills orders through local florists and its own site. Spending here generally codes as online shopping, with no special florist bonus on most cards. A flat-rate card or one with an online-shopping multiplier is the dependable way to earn on holiday and sympathy arrangements.

stores.name: fuboTV
stores.intro:
fuboTV is a live TV streaming service that started out sports-focused, bundling regional
sports networks, national broadcast channels, and cable news alongside cloud DVR, and has
since broadened into a general live-TV alternative to cable. It competes with services like
Hulu Live and YouTube TV rather than on-demand platforms like Netflix. There's no fuboTV
co-brand credit card, so a streaming or entertainment category card is the natural fit for
the monthly subscription, with a flat-rate cashback card as the fallback.

stores.name: Furla
stores.intro:
Furla is an Italian leather goods house founded in Bologna in 1927, best known for
structured handbags along with wallets, small leather goods, and accessories sold through
its own boutiques and department stores worldwide. It sits in the accessible-luxury tier,
priced below houses like Prada or Gucci but above mass-market handbag brands. There is no
Furla co-brand credit card, so a purchase there is best matched to a premium travel or
general rewards card that bonuses online or department-store shopping, or a flat-rate
cashback card for simplicity on a luxury accessory purchase.

stores.name: G-Star RAW
stores.intro:
G-Star RAW is a Dutch denim and streetwear brand founded in 1989, built around raw,
unwashed denim and technical, design-forward silhouettes that helped popularize premium
denim in Europe before expanding into the U.S. market. Its collections span jeans,
jackets, and everyday apparel sold direct-to-consumer and through select retailers. There
is no G-Star RAW co-brand credit card, so a shopper buying denim there should lean on a
card that rewards select clothing or online shopping purchases, or a flat-rate cashback
card if apparel isn't a bonused category.

stores.name: GameStop
stores.intro:
GameStop is the largest U.S. video-game specialty retailer with about 2,900
domestic stores. In-store charges typically code as electronics or hobby;
gamestop.com codes as online shopping. The PowerUp Rewards program is free
(Pro tier is paid) and issues per-purchase points plus a Game Informer
subscription on the Pro tier. There's no widely-recommended GameStop-specific
credit card; pair with an online-shopping-bonus card or flat-rate cashback.

stores.name: Gametime
stores.intro:
Gametime is a mobile-first ticket marketplace for sports, concerts, and live events,
built around a same-day, last-minute buying experience with a countdown-style interface
showing how ticket prices trend as an event approaches. It's a resale and secondary
marketplace in the same space as SeatGeek, Vivid Seats, and StubHub rather than a primary
box office. There is no Gametime co-brand credit card, so ticket purchases there are best
matched to a card that bonuses entertainment spend, or a broad travel or dining rewards
card if entertainment isn't a defined category on the cardholder's cards.

stores.name: Gap
stores.intro:
Gap is a U.S. apparel retailer with about 500 domestic stores plus a sister
brand portfolio (Old Navy, Banana Republic, Athleta) under Gap Inc. In-store
charges code as department stores or apparel; gap.com codes as online shopping.
The Gap Good Rewards co-branded Mastercard offers tiered cashback at Gap-family
brands. For non-cardholders, an online-shopping-bonus card or U.S. Bank Shopper
Cash Rewards (if Gap is selected) is the strongest pairing.

stores.name: Gazelle
stores.intro:
Gazelle is an online marketplace for buying and selling certified pre-owned smartphones,
tablets, and other consumer electronics, positioning itself as a trusted alternative to
trading a device in at a carrier store or selling peer-to-peer. Shoppers can buy refurbished
iPhones and other devices, or sell and trade in their own used electronics for cash. There
is no Gazelle co-brand credit card, so a purchase there is best matched to a card that
bonuses electronics or online shopping, or a flat-rate cashback card for simplicity on a
refurbished-device purchase.

stores.name: Gerber Gear
stores.intro:
Gerber Gear is an American maker of knives, multi-tools, and outdoor survival equipment
founded in Portland, Oregon in 1939, with a longstanding reputation among hunters,
campers, and military and first-responder users. It's part of the Finland-based Fiskars
Group, which also owns brands like Fiskars scissors and Gerber's household-tool lineage
is separate from Gerber baby food. There is no Gerber Gear co-brand credit card, so a
purchase there should be matched to a card that bonuses sporting goods or outdoor retail,
or a flat-rate cashback card if no specialty category applies.

stores.name: GetGo
stores.intro: GetGo is the fuel and convenience banner of grocer Giant Eagle, with cafe-style locations across Pennsylvania, Ohio, and neighboring states. At the pump the charge codes as a gas purchase, so a gas rewards card earns best there. Food and merchandise inside may code as convenience or retail, and GetGo also ties into Giant Eagle fuelperks, which can stack with credit-card rewards.

stores.name: ghd
stores.intro:
ghd is a British hair-styling brand best known for its ceramic flat irons and curling
wands, positioned at the premium end of the hair-tools market and sold direct online as
well as through salons and beauty retailers. The name is short for "good hair day," the
company's original tagline. There's no ghd co-brand credit card, so a general online
shopping rewards card or a flat-rate cashback card is the practical way to earn rewards on
styling tool purchases.

stores.name: Giant Eagle
stores.intro:
Giant Eagle is a Pittsburgh-based supermarket chain with roughly 200 stores across
Pennsylvania, Ohio, West Virginia, Indiana, and Maryland, plus its GetGo fuel and
convenience arm. The supermarkets code as U.S. groceries (full multiplier for Amex
Gold, Blue Cash Preferred, Citi Custom Cash), while GetGo gas stations typically code
as gas. The chain's fuelperks+ program lets shoppers turn grocery spend into pennies
off per gallon at GetGo, which can stack with a gas-category card at the pump.

stores.name: Giant Food
stores.intro:
Giant Food is the Mid-Atlantic flagship of Ahold Delhaize's U.S. grocery group, with
about 165 stores across DC, Maryland, Virginia, and Delaware. Codes cleanly as
groceries on every card we track. Best pairings are Blue Cash Preferred (6%),
Capital One SavorOne (3%), and U.S. Bank Shopper Cash Rewards (6% if selected).
The Giant Flexible Rewards program issues per-gallon fuel savings at participating
Shell and Giant stations that stack on top of card bonuses.

stores.name: Gilt
stores.intro:
Gilt is a flash-sale e-commerce site that built its name on time-limited discounts on
designer clothing, accessories, and home goods, with a companion Gilt City arm that sold
local deals on dining, spa, and entertainment experiences in major metro areas. It was one
of the pioneers of the members-only flash-sale model in the 2010s before being absorbed
into Rue La La's parent company. There is no Gilt co-brand credit card, so shoppers there
should rely on a card that bonuses online shopping or department-store purchases, or a
flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: GlassesUSA
stores.intro: GlassesUSA is an online-only eyewear retailer selling prescription glasses, sunglasses, and contacts, often pushing aggressive promo codes and virtual try-on. Because there is no storefront, every purchase codes as online shopping, making a card with an online-purchase bonus the natural fit. Stacking a coupon with that bonus, plus any FSA reimbursement, stretches each order further.

stores.name: Glossier
stores.intro:
Glossier is a direct-to-consumer beauty and skincare brand built out of the beauty blog
Into The Gloss, credited with popularizing the minimalist, dewy "no-makeup makeup" look
and a millennial-pink aesthetic that reshaped how beauty brands market on social media.
It sells primarily through its own site and a handful of flagship retail stores rather
than wide department-store distribution. There is no Glossier co-brand credit card, so a
purchase there is best matched to a card that bonuses online shopping, or a flat-rate
cashback card if beauty spend isn't a bonused category.

stores.name: GNC
stores.intro:
GNC is one of the largest specialty retailers of vitamins, supplements, sports nutrition,
and protein products in the U.S., operating both physical mall and strip-center stores and
an active e-commerce business, alongside a GNC-branded product line manufactured
in-house. There is no GNC co-brand credit card, so shoppers there should default to a card
that earns bonus rewards on online or general retail shopping, or a flat-rate cashback
card, since supplement purchases don't fall under drugstore or grocery bonus categories on
most issuers' charts even though GNC sells health products.

affiliate.offer: buy one get one 50% off vitamins

stores.name: GOAT
stores.intro:
GOAT is a sneaker and apparel resale marketplace operated by GOAT Group, which also
runs Flight Club. The platform authenticates every shoe and item before shipping to
the buyer, and lists both new and used pairs from hyped releases alongside everyday
retro and lifestyle styles. GOAT codes as online shopping for credit card networks,
so an online-shopping category bonus card will earn its full multiplier. Buyer fees
are added at checkout and post to the card as part of the transaction total.

stores.name: Godiva
stores.intro:
Godiva is a Belgian chocolatier founded in Brussels in 1926, known for premium truffles,
gift boxes, and seasonal chocolate assortments sold through its own boutiques, online
store, and gifting partnerships. The brand has changed hands several times, most recently
splitting its North American retail business from its international operations. There is
no Godiva co-brand credit card, so a gift or chocolate purchase there is best matched to a
card that bonuses online shopping or general retail, or a dining category card if the
purchase codes as food and beverage.

affiliate.offer: 15% off your first order with code WELCOME15

stores.name: Going
stores.intro:
Going, formerly known as Scott's Cheap Flights, is a subscription service that alerts
members to discounted and mistake-fare airfares out of their home airports, curated by a
team of flight-deal analysts rather than a booking engine itself. Members typically book
the flagged fares directly with the airline once alerted. There is no Going co-brand
credit card since it isn't a travel bookings site in the traditional sense, so a
subscription payment or any related travel booking is best matched to a travel rewards
card or a card that bonuses travel purchases broadly.

stores.name: Goldbelly
stores.intro:
Goldbelly is an online marketplace that ships iconic regional foods nationwide, from
Chicago deep-dish pizza to Katz's Deli pastrami and small-batch bakery goods, letting
shoppers order famous local restaurants and shops as packaged gifts or personal orders.
It functions as a food gifting and delivery platform rather than a single restaurant.
There is no Goldbelly co-brand credit card, so an order there is best matched to a card
that bonuses dining or online shopping purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card if neither
category is a strong fit on the cardholder's cards.

stores.name: Golden Corral
stores.intro: Golden Corral is the largest buffet chain in the US, built around all-you-can-eat spreads and its Sunday brunch. Even though you pay up front, the charge codes as dining or restaurants on most cards, so use one with a restaurant bonus to squeeze extra value out of the buffet line.

stores.name: GoodRx
stores.intro: GoodRx is a prescription-savings platform offering coupons and a membership that lower drug costs at the pharmacy counter. Its own membership and online charges typically code as online shopping or general retail, while the discounted prescription itself is paid at the pharmacy and codes as a drugstore purchase. Match the card bonus to where each charge actually posts.

stores.name: Goodyear Auto Service
stores.intro: Goodyear Auto Service runs company-owned shops selling tires and handling maintenance under the Goodyear brand. Charges here typically code as general auto retail or online shopping, not as fuel or travel, so category bonuses seldom apply at checkout. With no widely held card targeting tire service, a strong flat-rate everyday card tends to maximize earnings on a new set or routine work.

stores.name: Govee
stores.intro:
Govee makes smart-home lighting and consumer electronics, including LED strip lights,
smart bulbs, RGBIC ambiance lighting, and small appliances like ice makers and air
purifiers, sold primarily through its own site, Amazon, and other online marketplaces.
The brand has built a following among gamers and home-decor enthusiasts for affordable,
app-controlled lighting setups. There is no Govee co-brand credit card, so a purchase
there is best paired with a card that bonuses electronics or online shopping, or a
flat-rate cashback card if no specialty category applies.

stores.name: Greenworks Tools
stores.intro:
Greenworks Tools makes battery-powered outdoor power equipment, including electric lawn
mowers, string trimmers, chainsaws, and pressure washers, positioned as a cordless
alternative to gas-powered yard tools. The brand sells through its own site as well as
major home improvement and hardware retailers. There is no Greenworks co-brand credit
card, so a purchase there is best matched to a card that bonuses home improvement or
hardware spend, or a flat-rate cashback card if the retailer isn't a bonused category.

stores.name: Greyhound
stores.intro: Greyhound is the largest intercity bus carrier in North America, now operated under the FlixBus parent company. Fares typically code as transit or ground transportation and earn on cards with a transit bonus, otherwise falling to flat-rate. For occasional intercity bus trips, a card that rewards transit broadly is the most practical way to earn here.

stores.name: Grocery Outlet
stores.intro: Grocery Outlet is a discount, independently operated grocer selling overstock and closeout name-brand food at steep markdowns across the West and East Coasts. Your spending codes as groceries on most rewards cards, so a supermarket bonus applies even though prices are already low. Note that warehouse-club exclusions do not apply here, since it codes as a normal supermarket.

stores.name: Groupon
stores.intro: Groupon sells discounted vouchers for local experiences, dining, travel, and goods through its app and site. Charges code as online shopping rather than as dining or travel, even when the deal is for a restaurant or hotel, so those bonuses will not apply. A flat-rate or online-retail card earns the most on voucher purchases.

stores.name: Grove Collaborative
stores.intro:
Grove Collaborative is an online marketplace focused on natural and sustainable household
and personal care products, selling cleaning supplies, paper goods, and beauty items
through both one-time orders and a recurring subscription model, along with its own
Grove-branded product lines. The company has publicly emphasized plastic-free packaging
and carbon-neutral shipping goals. There is no Grove Collaborative co-brand credit card,
so a purchase there is best matched to a card that bonuses online shopping or general
retail, or a flat-rate cashback card for household staples.

affiliate.offer: 20% off your first order with code WELCOME

stores.name: Grubhub
stores.intro:
Grubhub is one of the three major US food-delivery marketplaces alongside DoorDash and Uber
Eats, connecting customers to roughly 365,000 restaurants and operating Seamless in the
Northeast under the same parent. Grubhub charges code as dining/restaurants on most card
networks, which means dining-bonus cards earn full rate. Amex Gold pays 4x Membership Rewards,
Chase Sapphire Reserve pays 4x Ultimate Rewards, and the Capital One SavorOne pays 3% cash.
Several cards also offer Grubhub-specific credits or Grubhub+ memberships as a perk.

stores.name: Guess
stores.intro:
Guess is a Los Angeles-founded denim and fashion label built around a distinctive black
and white advertising aesthetic, selling jeans, apparel, handbags, watches, and
accessories through its own stores, guess.com, and a large wholesale presence at
department stores like Macy's and Nordstrom. There is no Guess co-brand credit card, so
shoppers checking out online should lean on a card that pays bonus rewards on online
shopping or general merchandise, and department-store purchases can be routed through a
card that rewards spending at Macy's or similar retailers. A flat-rate cashback card
is a reasonable fallback if you don't want to track category bonuses.

stores.name: Gulf
stores.intro: Gulf is one of the oldest US fuel brands, now licensing its name to independently operated stations along the East Coast and beyond. Fueling up generally codes as a gas or fuel purchase, the category a gas rewards card is built to capture. Convenience-store items inside may code as general retail, so a dedicated gas card at the pump plus a flat-rate card for the store works well.

stores.name: Gymboree
stores.intro:
Gymboree is a children's clothing brand known for colorful, play-focused apparel for
babies and kids, sold primarily through gymboree.com after the retailer's brick and
mortar stores wound down and its assets were relaunched under new ownership. There is
no Gymboree co-brand credit card, so the best approach is a card that earns bonus
rewards on general online shopping or department-store-style purchases, since kids'
apparel does not have its own dedicated card category. A flat-rate cashback card also
works well for occasional purchases like these.

stores.name: H Mart
stores.intro:
H Mart is the largest Korean-American supermarket chain in the U.S., with roughly 100
stores concentrated in major metros with significant Asian-American populations
(Northeast, California, Texas, Georgia, Pacific Northwest). The chain specializes in
Korean and broader East Asian grocery, fresh seafood, and prepared foods, and most
locations include a food court. H Mart codes as a U.S. supermarket, so grocery
category bonus cards (Amex Gold, Blue Cash Preferred, Citi Custom Cash) earn their
full multiplier at the register.

stores.name: H-E-B
stores.intro:
H-E-B is the dominant Texas supermarket chain, with about 425 stores under H-E-B,
Central Market, and Joe V's banners. The chain is privately held and well-regarded for
store-brand products and Texas-only specialty items. H-E-B codes as groceries on every
card we track and supports curbside pickup and delivery on most product lines.

stores.name: H&M
stores.intro:
H&M is a Swedish-headquartered fast-fashion chain with about 540 U.S. stores.
In-store charges code as department stores or apparel; hm.com codes as online
shopping. The H&M Member program is free and issues birthday gifts plus tiered
perks; H&M's co-branded U.S. credit card layers an additional discount on top
of the loyalty program. For non-cardholders, an online-shopping-bonus card is
the strongest pairing.

stores.name: Half Price Books
stores.intro:
Half Price Books is a Dallas-based used and new book retailer with over 120 stores
across the United States, also selling vinyl records, movies, video games, and
collectibles. The chain is known for buying back books, music, and games from
customers in addition to its retail operation. Purchases typically code as bookstore
or general retail on most credit card networks.

Cards with selectable categories can sometimes capture bookstore purchases. U.S.
Bank Cash+ includes bookstores as one of its selectable 5 percent categories, which
can deliver meaningful value for heavy readers. Online orders at hpb.com may also
trigger online shopping bonuses on cards like Bank of America Customized Cash and
Chase Freedom Flex during rotating online quarters.

stores.name: Hanes
stores.intro:
Hanes is a longstanding American basics brand selling t-shirts, underwear, socks, and
loungewear for men, women, and kids, part of apparel conglomerate Hanesbrands alongside
labels like Champion and Bali. The brand sells directly through hanes.com in addition
to wide distribution at mass retailers and department stores. There is no Hanes
co-brand credit card, so a purchase at hanes.com is best routed through a card that
earns bonus rewards on general online shopping, or a flat-rate cashback card if you'd
rather not track categories.

stores.name: Hanna Andersson
stores.intro:
Hanna Andersson is a Portland, Oregon-founded children's clothing brand best known for
soft, durable cotton basics and its matching family pajama sets, sold through
hannaandersson.com and a small number of retail stores. There is no Hanna Andersson
co-brand credit card, so the best way to earn on a purchase is a card that pays bonus
rewards on general online shopping, since kids' apparel doesn't have its own dedicated
reward category. A flat-rate cashback card is a solid fallback for occasional orders.

stores.name: Hannaford
stores.intro:
Hannaford is a New England grocery chain with about 185 stores, owned by Ahold
Delhaize. Codes cleanly as groceries on every card we track. Strongest pairings
are Blue Cash Preferred (6%), Capital One SavorOne (3% groceries with no cap),
and U.S. Bank Shopper Cash Rewards (6% on selected retailers). Hannaford runs
the My Hannaford Rewards program with personalized digital coupons on top of
any card's earn rate.

stores.name: Harbor Freight Tools
stores.intro:
Harbor Freight Tools is a discount tool retailer with over 1,500 U.S. stores selling
hand tools, power tools, generators, and shop equipment under house brands such as
Bauer, Hercules, and Icon. Most card networks code Harbor Freight as a home
improvement merchant, which means cards with a home improvement bonus like the Wells
Fargo Bilt can earn elevated rewards on purchases.

The Harbor Freight Inside Track Club membership stacks deep coupon discounts with
credit card rewards, and online orders through harborfreight.com may also qualify
for rotating online shopping bonuses on Chase Freedom Flex and Discover it. Big
generator and welder purchases are useful for signup bonus spend.

stores.name: Hardee's
stores.intro:
Hardee's is the eastern and southern US counterpart to Carl's Jr. under the CKE Restaurants
umbrella, with around 1,700 domestic locations. The menu skews toward charbroiled Thickburgers,
Made From Scratch Biscuits at breakfast, and hand-breaded chicken tenders. Purchases at
Hardee's, including those routed through the Hardee's mobile app, code as fast-food dining,
which means cards like the Capital One SavorOne (3% unlimited on dining), Chase Sapphire
Preferred (3x), and Amex Gold (4x) earn at the full dining multiplier on every order.

stores.name: Harris Teeter
stores.intro:
Harris Teeter is a Southeast U.S. grocery chain with about 250 stores, owned by
Kroger. Codes cleanly as groceries on every card we track. Best pairings are
Blue Cash Preferred (6%), Capital One SavorOne (3%), and U.S. Bank Shopper Cash
Rewards (6% if Harris Teeter is selected). VIC card (loyalty) discounts and
per-gallon fuel savings at Harris Teeter and Kroger fuel stations stack on top
of card bonuses.

stores.name: Harry & David
stores.intro: Harry & David sells gourmet gift baskets, pears, and the Tower of Treats brand, mostly online and through catalogs. These orders code as online shopping rather than as groceries, even though the products are food, so supermarket bonuses do not apply. A flat-rate card or one with an online-shopping multiplier earns the most on holiday gifting.

stores.name: Harry's
stores.intro: Harry's sells razors, blades, and men's grooming products mostly through its own direct-to-consumer site and subscription refills. Purchases here generally code as online shopping, so a flat-rate card or one with an online retail bonus earns the most. There is no widely recognized co-branded store card, so lean on a general-purpose rewards card for the recurring shipments.

affiliate.offer: up to 15% off with Subscribe & Save

stores.name: Hawaiian Airlines
stores.intro:
Hawaiian Airlines is the dominant carrier between the U.S. mainland and Hawaii, plus
inter-island routes. Tickets code as airfare on every card we track. The co-branded
Hawaiian Airlines World Elite Mastercard offers a discount on round-trip companion
tickets to Hawaii each year and bonus miles on the airline. With Alaska Airlines'
acquisition closed, Hawaiian's loyalty program is integrating with Alaska's Mileage
Plan, so long-term value will increasingly route through Alaska's award chart.

stores.name: HelloFresh
stores.intro:
HelloFresh is the largest meal-kit delivery service in the United States, shipping pre-portioned
ingredients and recipe cards weekly to subscribers across most ZIP codes. The HelloFresh
group also operates Factor (prepared meals), Green Chef, and EveryPlate. HelloFresh charges
typically code as online retail or grocery delivery, not dining or in-store groceries, which
affects which cards earn bonus. Online-shopping bonuses (Chase Freedom Flex rotating quarters,
Discover it 5% categories) and flat-rate cards (Citi Double Cash 2%) are usually the best fit.

stores.name: Hers
stores.intro:
Hers is the women's-focused telehealth and wellness brand under Hims & Hers Health,
offering online consultations and prescription and non-prescription products for skin
care, hair loss, mental health, and sexual wellness, shipped directly to customers.
There is no Hers co-brand credit card, so a subscription or one-time order is best
charged to a card that earns bonus rewards on general online shopping or drugstore-type
purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card for simplicity.

stores.name: Hertz
stores.intro:
Hertz is one of the three large U.S. car-rental brands and runs the Gold Plus Rewards
program. Rentals booked direct at hertz.com code as car rentals on every card we
track. Several premium cards offer status matches with Hertz President's Circle
(Capital One Venture X, Amex Platinum), which adds a class upgrade and free
additional driver. Bookings made through a card's travel portal earn the portal
bonus rate but typically lose any direct-booking discount and may not always credit
to your Gold Plus Rewards account.

stores.name: Hey Dude
stores.intro:
Hey Dude is a casual, lightweight footwear brand known for its slip-on shoes and
everyday comfort styling, acquired by Crocs Inc in 2022 to complement the flagship
Crocs brand. Shoes are sold through heydude.com along with department stores and
footwear retailers. There is no Hey Dude co-brand credit card, so shoppers ordering
direct should use a card that earns bonus rewards on general online shopping, or a
flat-rate cashback card if simplicity matters more than chasing categories.

stores.name: Hickory Farms
stores.intro:
Hickory Farms is a longtime American gift retailer known for its summer sausage and
cheese gift boxes, sold online at hickoryfarms.com and through seasonal kiosks and
mall storefronts, especially around the winter holidays. There is no Hickory Farms
co-brand credit card, so a gift order is best charged to a card that earns bonus
rewards on general online shopping or gift purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card for
simplicity.

stores.name: High Sierra
stores.intro:
High Sierra is an American brand of backpacks, luggage, and travel bags built for
durability, popular for school backpacks and outdoor and travel gear, sold through
highsierra.com and a range of retail and sporting goods partners. There is no High
Sierra co-brand credit card, so a purchase is best charged to a card that earns bonus
rewards on general online shopping or sporting goods, or a flat-rate cashback card if
you prefer not to track categories.

stores.name: Hill's Pet Nutrition
stores.intro:
Hill's Pet Nutrition makes Science Diet and Prescription Diet pet food, a veterinarian-
recommended line of dog and cat food owned by Colgate-Palmolive, sold direct at
hillspet.com as well as through vet clinics, pet retailers, and online marketplaces.
There is no Hill's co-brand credit card, so a direct order is best charged to a card
that earns bonus rewards on pet supplies or general online shopping, or a flat-rate
cashback card for simplicity.

affiliate.offer: 40% off your first order with code SUMMER40

stores.name: Hilton
stores.intro:
Hilton Honors covers roughly 8,000 properties worldwide across 22 brands, from budget
Hampton Inn and Tru up through Waldorf Astoria and Conrad luxury. Booking direct at
hilton.com or in-app earns the full 10x base points plus any co-brand multiplier, while
third-party bookings (Expedia, Booking.com) earn nothing in the program and don't count
toward elite status. The Hilton co-brand cards lean heavily on perks — free weekend
nights, automatic Gold or Diamond status, statement credits — rather than headline
earn rates, so the value calculation depends on whether you'll actually use the
certificate and the status. Hilton points typically value around 0.5 cents each.

stores.name: Hims
stores.intro: Hims sells prescription and over-the-counter products for hair loss, skin, sexual health and mental wellness, shipped through its telehealth subscription model. Because most orders run through its website, charges generally code as online shopping or general merchandise rather than a drugstore, so a card that rewards online or everyday spending usually earns the most here.

stores.name: Hobby Lobby
stores.intro:
Hobby Lobby is a U.S. arts-and-crafts retailer with about 1,000 stores, selling
fabric, home decor, art supplies, and seasonal goods. In-store charges code as
department stores or specialty retail; hobbylobby.com codes as online shopping.
Hobby Lobby doesn't run a loyalty program but consistently posts a 40%-off-one-
item coupon that's the main rewards lever per visit. Pair with a flat-rate
cashback card or department-store-bonus card.

stores.name: HOKA
stores.intro:
HOKA is a performance running shoe brand known for its thick, cushioned midsoles,
owned by footwear and apparel company Deckers Brands alongside UGG and Teva. The brand
has grown from a niche ultramarathon favorite into one of the fastest-growing names in
running and everyday athletic footwear, sold on hoka.com and through specialty running
and sporting goods retailers. There is no HOKA co-brand credit card, so shoppers buying
direct should lean on a card with a sporting goods or online shopping category bonus,
or a flat-rate cashback card for simplicity.

stores.name: Holland America Line
stores.intro: Holland America Line is a long-running premium cruise brand recognized for classic itineraries and lengthy voyages. Charges from the line generally code as travel and earn on cards with a travel multiplier, but cruise coding is not always consistent. A flexible travel card, or a flat-rate card, covers both the fare and any onboard purchases without category guesswork.

stores.name: Hollister
stores.intro:
Hollister is the teen and young adult apparel brand under Abercrombie & Fitch Co, with
a Southern California beach identity and around 500 stores worldwide. Most general
purpose cards code Hollister purchases as department store in mall and as online
shopping for hollisterco.com checkouts. The Club Cali loyalty program is free and
layers on top of credit card rewards, but Hollister does not currently offer its own
branded credit card.

stores.name: Hollywood Feed
stores.intro: Hollywood Feed is a specialty pet retailer focused on premium food and natural products, with a growing store footprint and an online shop. Online orders typically code as online shopping or general retail and earn on cards with an online or flat-rate bonus, while in-store purchases usually post at flat-rate. A dependable everyday card is the simplest approach for recurring supplies.

stores.name: Home Chef
stores.intro:
Home Chef is the meal-kit subscription owned by Kroger since 2018, offering customizable
recipes including Fresh and Easy 15-minute kits, Family Menu portions, and the Express line
of microwavable meals. Home Chef kits also sell through Kroger-banner grocery stores. When
ordered online, Home Chef charges typically code as online retail or direct food shipping
rather than groceries or dining, so flat-rate cards (Citi Double Cash 2%, Wells Fargo Active
Cash 2%) often beat category cards. In-store purchases at Kroger code as supermarkets.

stores.name: Home Depot
stores.intro:
The Home Depot is the largest U.S. home-improvement retailer, with about 2,000 stores
and a heavily-used Pro Xtra business loyalty program. The right card here depends a lot
on whether you're a homeowner doing one project a year or a contractor running steady
volume — the gap between the best home-improvement category card and a generic 2% card
is meaningful at contractor spend levels. Home Depot codes as home improvement at the
register and on homedepot.com.

stores.name: HoMedics
stores.intro:
HoMedics is a personal wellness brand selling massagers, humidifiers, air purifiers,
sound machines, and small health and comfort devices for the home, sold through
homedics.com and widely at mass retailers and drugstores. There is no HoMedics
co-brand credit card, so a direct order is best charged to a card that earns bonus
rewards on general online shopping or electronics-type purchases, or a flat-rate
cashback card for simplicity.

stores.name: HomeGoods
stores.intro:
HomeGoods is the home-furnishings off-price chain owned by TJX Companies, with
about 900 U.S. stores selling discount furniture, decor, and bath. Codes as
department stores on most cards. The TJX Rewards Mastercard offers 5% back at
HomeGoods and the rest of the TJX family. For everyone else, a department-store
category card (Wells Fargo Active Cash, Citi Custom Cash if it's your top
category) or flat-rate cashback card is the standard pairing.

stores.name: HomeSense
stores.intro: HomeSense, the TJX off-price home brand and sister to HomeGoods, sells ever-changing decor and furniture that generally codes as general retail, not a home-improvement warehouse, so those bonuses often do not apply. Its US site at homesense.com is limited, but online orders code as online shopping. A flat-rate or online-shopping card is the dependable choice here.

stores.name: Hot Topic
stores.intro:
Hot Topic is a mall-based specialty retailer focused on pop culture apparel, music
merch, and fandom licensed goods, with around 600 U.S. stores plus a sister chain
BoxLunch. The Hot Cash loyalty program runs seasonally with print and digital
certificates that stack with credit card rewards. Most cards code Hot Topic as
department store in person and online shopping for hottopic.com checkouts, and there
is no Hot Topic branded credit card today.

stores.name: Hotels.com
stores.intro:
Hotels.com is one of the largest global online travel agencies, owned by Expedia
Group. Bookings code as travel on every card we track. The honest catch: hotel
chains typically do NOT credit Hotels.com stays toward elite-night or status
counts, and many properties exclude OTA stays from earning their loyalty points
— so a Hilton/Marriott/Hyatt loyalist almost always books direct. Hotels.com's
own One Key Rewards (formerly Hotels.com Rewards) is now unified with Expedia's
OneKeyCash and stacks across the Expedia family.

stores.name: Hotwire
stores.intro: Hotwire, part of Expedia Group, specializes in opaque Hot Rate deals where the exact hotel or rental brand is revealed only after booking. These purchases typically code as travel and earn on cards with a travel multiplier, though the underlying supplier can occasionally drive the coding. A flexible travel card rewards both prepaid hotel and rental-car deals well.

stores.name: Houzz
stores.intro: Houzz is a home design platform that pairs project inspiration with a marketplace for furniture, fixtures, and decor. Marketplace purchases generally code as online shopping rather than as a home-improvement store, so do not count on a hardware-store bonus. A flat-rate card or one rewarding online retail is the dependable way to earn on renovation buys.

stores.name: HP
stores.intro: HP sells PCs, printers, ink and accessories through its online store and authorized retailers. Buying direct from HP.com usually codes as online shopping or general merchandise, so a card with an online or everyday-spending bonus is a solid choice, and routing the purchase through a shopping portal can add a further rebate.

stores.name: HSN
stores.intro:
HSN, the Home Shopping Network, is a multichannel retailer selling via 24-hour
television, hsn.com, and mobile, owned by Qurate Retail Group alongside sister
brand QVC. The product mix covers beauty, fashion, jewelry, kitchen, and home goods,
with a heavy emphasis on live demonstrations and host-led pitches. HSN purchases code
as online shopping or general merchandise for credit card networks, so an
online-shopping category bonus card will earn its multiplier. The FlexPay installment
program is available at checkout against the saved card.

stores.name: Huel
stores.intro:
Huel is a UK-founded nutrition brand best known for its powdered and ready-to-drink
meal replacement shakes and bars, designed to deliver complete, balanced nutrition in
place of a traditional meal, sold direct at huel.com on a one-time or subscription
basis. There is no Huel co-brand credit card, so an order is best charged to a card
that earns bonus rewards on general online shopping, or a flat-rate cashback card if
you don't want to track categories.

stores.name: Hugo Boss
stores.intro:
Hugo Boss is a German luxury fashion house selling tailored menswear, women's
ready-to-wear, and accessories under its BOSS and HUGO lines, distributed through its
own stores, hugoboss.com, and department stores like Nordstrom and Saks. There is no
Hugo Boss co-brand credit card, so shoppers ordering online should lean on a card that
earns bonus rewards on online shopping, while in-store purchases at a partner
department store can sometimes route through a store-specific card. A premium travel
or flat-rate cashback card also works well for higher-ticket purchases like these.

stores.name: Hulu
stores.intro:
Hulu is a U.S. streaming-video service offering on-demand library content and a
live-TV bundle, owned by Disney. Monthly subscriptions code as streaming on
every card we track. Streaming-bonus cards (Blue Cash Preferred 6%, U.S. Bank
Cash+ 5% on selected categories) are the strongest pairings. Hulu (with-ads)
is also bundled with Disney+ and ESPN+ as the Disney Bundle for the lowest
per-service cost.

stores.name: Hungryroot
stores.intro:
Hungryroot is a personalized grocery and recipe delivery service that ships a mix of
fresh produce, proteins, and pantry staples along with quick recipes tailored to a
customer's food preferences, functioning as a hybrid between a meal kit and an online
grocery order. There is no Hungryroot co-brand credit card, so a subscription is best
charged to a card that earns bonus rewards on dining or groceries, or a flat-rate
cashback card if your card's category definitions don't clearly cover grocery delivery
services.

stores.name: Hy-Vee
stores.intro:
Hy-Vee is a Midwest, employee-owned supermarket chain with roughly 280 stores across
Iowa, Illinois, Kansas, Minnesota, Missouri, Nebraska, South Dakota, and Wisconsin.
Stores are large-format and often include pharmacies, food courts, and Hy-Vee Fuel
Saver gas stations in some markets. Hy-Vee supermarkets code as U.S. groceries, so any
grocery category card (Amex Gold, Blue Cash Preferred, Citi Custom Cash) will earn its
posted multiplier. The Fuel Saver + Perks loyalty program is a useful supplement when
shoppers also fill up at participating Casey's or Hy-Vee Fast & Fresh locations.

stores.name: Hyatt
stores.intro:
World of Hyatt is the smallest of the major U.S. hotel programs by footprint — about
1,400 properties — but consistently rates as the most rewarding on a points-per-dollar
basis, with award nights starting at 3,500 points at Category 1 properties. Hyatt
points are widely valued at 1.7 to 2.0 cents each, the highest in the hotel space, so
even modest co-brand earn rates produce strong cashback-equivalent value. Direct
bookings at hyatt.com earn points and elite credit; OTA stays earn neither. The Chase
World of Hyatt cards are also one of the few flexible-points pipelines into Hyatt,
since Chase Ultimate Rewards transfer to Hyatt 1:1 — making Sapphire Preferred and
Reserve indirect Hyatt earners as well.

stores.name: Hydro Flask
stores.intro:
Hydro Flask is an Oregon-founded brand of insulated stainless steel water bottles and
drinkware popular with hikers, campers, and everyday users, now owned by consumer
products company Helen of Troy. Bottles and accessories are sold direct at
hydroflask.com as well as through outdoor and sporting goods retailers. There is no
Hydro Flask co-brand credit card, so a direct order is best charged to a card that
earns bonus rewards on sporting goods or general online shopping, or a flat-rate
cashback card for simplicity.

stores.name: Icebreaker
stores.intro:
Icebreaker is a New Zealand-founded outdoor apparel brand built around merino wool
base layers, hiking and running gear, and everyday clothing, owned by VF Corporation
alongside brands like The North Face and Vans. Gear is sold direct at icebreaker.com
and through outdoor specialty retailers. There is no Icebreaker co-brand credit card,
so a direct order is best charged to a card that earns bonus rewards on sporting goods
or general online shopping, or a flat-rate cashback card for simplicity.

stores.name: Igloo
stores.intro:
Igloo is a longtime American brand of hard and soft-sided coolers, ice chests, and
drinkware built for camping, tailgating, and everyday outdoor use, sold direct at
igloocoolers.com and widely through sporting goods and general merchandise retailers.
There is no Igloo co-brand credit card, so a direct order is best charged to a card
that earns bonus rewards on sporting goods or general online shopping, or a flat-rate
cashback card for simplicity.

stores.name: IHG
stores.intro:
IHG Hotels & Resorts spans about 6,000 properties across 19 brands, anchored by Holiday
Inn and Holiday Inn Express on the mass-market end and InterContinental, Regent, and
Six Senses on the luxury end. The Chase IHG One Rewards Premier card is one of the
highest-leverage hotel co-brands available — its annual free-night certificate (good
at any property up to 40,000 points) often pays back the annual fee on a single use,
and the fourth-night-free benefit on award stays effectively cuts redemption costs by
25%. Direct bookings at ihg.com or in-app earn points and elite-night credits; OTA
stays generally earn nothing. Points value lands around 0.5 cents but redemptions can
exceed that on aspirational properties.

stores.name: IHOP
stores.intro:
IHOP, owned by Dine Brands Global alongside Applebee's, operates about 1,700 US restaurants
best known for buttermilk pancakes, the Rooty Tooty Fresh 'N Fruity breakfast, and a 24-hour
service model at many locations. The menu also covers omelets, French toast, burgers, and
dinner entrees. IHOP transactions code as dining/restaurants whether dine-in, To Go, or
via the International Bank of Pancakes loyalty app. Cards with strong dining bonuses (Amex
Gold at 4x, Chase Sapphire Preferred at 3x, Capital One SavorOne at 3% cash) earn full rate.

stores.name: IKEA
stores.intro:
IKEA is the largest global home-furnishings retailer with about 50 U.S. warehouse
stores plus a planning-studio network. In-store charges typically code as
furniture or home improvement; ikea.com codes as online shopping. The IKEA
Family loyalty program is free and issues member-only pricing plus a free
coffee/tea on store visits. The IKEA Visa Credit Card offers 5% back at IKEA
on purchases — typically the strongest rate for heavy buyers.

stores.name: In-N-Out Burger
stores.intro:
In-N-Out Burger is a West Coast cult favorite founded in Baldwin Park, California
in 1948, with around 400 locations across California, Nevada, Arizona, Utah,
Texas, Oregon, Colorado, and Idaho. The minimalist menu of Double-Doubles, fries,
and shakes plus the off-menu Animal Style ordering is the chain's identity. In-N-Out
transactions code as restaurants on Visa, Mastercard, and Amex networks.

Dining rewards cards capture In-N-Out well. The Amex Gold earns 4x points on U.S.
restaurants and is the top earner for frequent visitors, while the Chase Sapphire
Reserve pays 3x. No-annual-fee dining picks include the Capital One SavorOne at 3
percent and Wells Fargo Autograph at 3x. In-N-Out does not run a customer rewards
program, so credit card rewards are the only structured path to cash back.

stores.name: Innisfree
stores.intro:
Innisfree is a South Korean skincare and cosmetics brand known for products built
around natural ingredients sourced from Jeju Island, owned by Korean beauty
conglomerate Amorepacific and sold direct at innisfree.com along with select retail
and marketplace partners. There is no Innisfree co-brand credit card, so an order is
best charged to a card that earns bonus rewards on general online shopping, or a
flat-rate cashback card if you prefer not to track categories.

stores.name: Instacart
stores.intro:
Instacart is the largest U.S. grocery delivery and pickup marketplace, partnering
with Costco, Wegmans, ALDI, Publix, and many regional chains. Orders placed through
the Instacart app or instacart.com generally code as groceries on most cards, but
some banks classify the charge as the underlying merchant — worth confirming on
a small order before relying on a 6% grocery card. Several premium cards (Sapphire
Reserve, Venture X) include Instacart+ memberships and statement credits.

stores.name: IPSY
stores.intro:
IPSY is a beauty subscription service that ships members a monthly bag of curated
makeup, skincare, and hair sample and full-size products chosen from partner brands,
alongside an online marketplace for full-size purchases. There is no IPSY co-brand
credit card, so a subscription charge or marketplace order is best charged to a card
that earns bonus rewards on general online shopping, or a flat-rate cashback card if
you prefer not to track categories.

stores.name: ITA Airways
stores.intro:
ITA Airways is Italy's flag carrier, formed in 2021 to take over international and
domestic routes previously flown by Alitalia, operating out of Rome Fiumicino with
service to destinations across Europe, North America, South America, and Asia. There
is no ITA Airways co-brand credit card issued in the U.S., so booking a flight is best
done with a general travel rewards card that earns bonus points on airfare, or a
premium travel card if you want airport lounge access and trip protections on an
international itinerary.

stores.name: J. Jill
stores.intro:
J. Jill is an American specialty retailer selling women's apparel positioned around
relaxed, comfortable everyday styles, sold through its own stores, catalog, and
jjill.com. There is no J. Jill co-brand credit card, though the retailer offers its own
store loyalty and private-label financing program separate from general-purpose credit
cards. Shoppers who want travel or cashback rewards on a J. Jill purchase should
instead use a card that earns bonus rewards on general online or apparel shopping, or a
flat-rate cashback card for simplicity.

stores.name: J.Crew
stores.intro:
J.Crew is a U.S. apparel retailer with about 130 stores plus a strong online
presence. In-store codes as department stores; jcrew.com codes as online
shopping. The J.Crew Rewards loyalty program issues credits at spend tiers
separate from any card bonus. Strongest pairings are an online-shopping-bonus
card or flat-rate cashback for in-store purchases.

stores.name: Jack in the Box
stores.intro:
Jack in the Box operates roughly 2,200 locations concentrated in the western United States,
built around a 24-hour menu that mixes burgers, tacos, breakfast sandwiches, and late-night
munchie meals. Drive-thru and mobile-app orders code as standard quick-service restaurants,
so any card paying a dining bonus (3-5% on cards like the Capital One Savor or Amex Gold)
earns full rate. The Jack app supports Apple Pay and Google Pay funding, which lets you
stack a card's dining multiplier with mobile-wallet bonuses on cards that offer them.

stores.name: Jackery
stores.intro:
Jackery is a fast-growing maker of portable power stations and solar generators, sold mostly
direct-to-consumer at jackery.com alongside Amazon and select electronics retailers. There
is no Jackery co-brand credit card, and because a power station is an infrequent,
higher-dollar purchase, shoppers typically come out ahead with a card that pays a bonus on
online shopping or a flat-rate cashback card for the one big order.

stores.name: Jamba
stores.intro:
Jamba, rebranded from Jamba Juice and now part of Focus Brands, operates about 750 US
locations focused on real fruit smoothies, fresh juices, bowls, and plant-based options.
Signature blends include the Strawberry Surf Rider, Razzmatazz, and Aloha Pineapple. Jamba
purchases at standalone stores, mall locations, or through the Jamba app code as
dining/restaurants. That makes any dining-bonus card a fit: the Amex Gold earns 4x Membership
Rewards, Chase Sapphire Preferred earns 3x, and the Capital One SavorOne earns 3% cash back.

stores.name: James Allen
stores.intro: James Allen lets buyers inspect diamonds in 360-degree detail online, and orders at jamesallen.com code as online shopping or general retail. Engagement rings here can be a couple's largest single purchase, so the rewards rate compounds quickly on one transaction. With no jewelry bonus category, a card that rewards online spending or a high flat rate delivers the most.

stores.name: JanSport
stores.intro:
JanSport is an American backpack and bag brand owned by VF Corporation, sold direct at
jansport.com and through sporting-goods and department stores. There is no JanSport co-brand
credit card, so purchases earn best on a card with a strong online shopping multiplier or a
flat-rate cashback rate.

stores.name: Jared
stores.intro:
Jared, formally Jared The Galleria of Jewelry, is the upscale destination banner of
Signet Jewelers, with around 240 standalone stores featuring on-site design and repair
services plus jared.com. The Jared Diamond credit card from Comenity offers
promotional financing for high-ticket purchases. General-purpose cards typically code
Jared as department store in person and online shopping for web orders, and the free
Vault Rewards program is shared across Signet.

stores.name: JCPenney
stores.intro:
JCPenney is a mid-tier department store with about 660 stores and a smaller online
catalog at jcpenney.com. Like other mid-tier department chains, the bulk of purchases
happen at promotional discount, so card rewards stack on already-reduced prices.
JCPenney codes as a department store in-store and online shopping on the website, so
cards bonusing on either category will trigger here.

stores.name: JD Sports
stores.intro:
JD Sports is a UK-founded sneaker and athletic apparel retailer that has grown into one
of the largest sportswear chains in the world, carrying brands like Nike, adidas, and
Jordan across its stores and jdsports.com in the U.S. There is no JD Sports co-brand
credit card, so a purchase is best charged to a card that earns bonus rewards on
sporting goods or general online shopping, or a flat-rate cashback card if you prefer
simplicity over chasing categories.

stores.name: Jersey Mike's Subs
stores.intro:
Jersey Mike's Subs is a fast-growing sub sandwich chain founded in Point Pleasant,
New Jersey in 1956, with more than 2,800 locations across the United States. The
chain is known for cold subs sliced to order, hot Philly cheesesteaks and Chicken
Philly options, and the annual Month of Giving charitable campaign. Transactions
code as restaurants on most credit card networks.

The Amex Gold leads at 4x points on U.S. restaurants and is the highest base
earner for regular Jersey Mike's visits, while the Chase Sapphire Reserve pays 3x
on dining. No-annual-fee picks like the Capital One SavorOne at 3 percent and
Wells Fargo Autograph at 3x are practical defaults. Jersey Mike's also runs the
Shore Points loyalty program, which layers in-app rewards on top of credit card
cash back.

stores.name: JetBlue
stores.intro:
JetBlue runs an East Coast and Caribbean network with TrueBlue as its loyalty program.
TrueBlue points are revenue-based — about 1.3 cents each — so they're easy to value
but rarely produce outsized award redemptions. JetBlue tickets code as airfare on
every card we track. The co-branded Plus and Premier cards bundle a 5,000-point
anniversary bonus, free checked bag, and Mosaic-status accelerators that can swing
the math for regulars; the Premier adds a Mint upgrade benefit on transcons.

stores.name: Jewel-Osco
stores.intro: Jewel-Osco is a Chicago-area institution under the Albertsons family, combining a grocery store with an in-house Osco pharmacy. Your grocery total codes as supermarket on most cards, so lean on a card that bonuses groceries. Pharmacy and household items rung up at the main registers typically fall under the same grocery code.

stores.name: Jiffy Lube
stores.intro:
Jiffy Lube is the largest quick-lube franchise in North America with over 2,000
service centers offering oil changes, tire rotations, fluid services, and air
filter replacements. Most Jiffy Lube transactions code as auto services rather than
a dedicated rewards category, so flat-rate cards like the Citi Double Cash often
win on simplicity.

Cards with selectable categories that include auto can do better. Citi Custom Cash
pays 5 percent on the top eligible category and may capture Jiffy Lube for drivers
whose top monthly spend is auto services. The U.S. Bank Cash+ also offers wider
auto-related categories. Jiffy Lube's loyalty program does not currently offer
significant points that stack with credit card rewards.

stores.name: Jimmy John's
stores.intro:
Jimmy John's is an Illinois-based sub sandwich chain known for its "Freaky Fast"
delivery, day-baked French bread, and a streamlined menu of cold subs including
the Vito, Turkey Tom, and Italian Night Club. The chain operates over 2,600
locations nationwide and is owned by Inspire Brands. Jimmy John's transactions code
as restaurants on Visa, Mastercard, and Amex networks.

The Amex Gold pays 4x points on U.S. restaurants and leads the field for daily
Jimmy John's lunches, while the Chase Sapphire Reserve pays 3x on dining and
includes travel-friendly redemptions. No-annual-fee dining picks like the Capital
One SavorOne and Wells Fargo Autograph each earn 3 percent or 3x on restaurants.
The Freaky Fast Rewards program adds points and free sandwiches on top of credit
card cash back.

stores.name: Jo Malone London
stores.intro:
Jo Malone London is a British luxury fragrance and scented-home brand owned by The
Estee Lauder Companies, known for its layerable colognes, candles, and bath lines and
sold through its own boutiques, jomalone.com, and prestige department stores such as
Nordstrom, Bloomingdale's, and Saks. A purchase direct from the website or a Jo Malone
boutique typically codes as online shopping or a department store, while the same
bottle bought inside a larger department store can instead lean on that retailer's
category bonus if one applies. There is no Jo Malone co-brand credit card, so shoppers
get the most value from a card with a strong online shopping multiplier or a selectable
department store category, and because fragrance restocks are small purchases a
flat-rate cashback card works well too.

stores.name: JOANN
stores.intro: JOANN is a fabric and crafts chain with both large physical stores and online ordering. In-store spending typically codes as general retail, while website orders code as online shopping, so a crafts-specific bonus is not something to expect. A flat-rate card, or one rewarding online purchases, earns the most on sewing and crafting supplies.

stores.name: Johnston & Murphy
stores.intro:
Johnston & Murphy is an American footwear and accessories brand dating back to 1850,
known for dress shoes, boots, and increasingly casual and hybrid styles aimed at
professional men, alongside a smaller assortment of belts, bags, and apparel. The
company has operated as a standalone specialty retailer for most of its history and
has no co-brand credit card of its own. Shoppers buying dress or business-casual
shoes here are usually best served by a general online shopping or select-clothing
rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card, rather than expecting any store-specific
financing beyond a standard retail account.

stores.name: Jonathan Adler
stores.intro:
Jonathan Adler is a design-led home furnishings brand built around the namesake
potter and interior designer, selling ceramics, furniture, lighting, textiles, and
decorative accessories with a distinctive maximalist, pop-influenced aesthetic. It
sells primarily through its own boutiques and website rather than mass retail
partners, and there is no Jonathan Adler co-brand credit card. Furnishing a room from
this brand is a good spot to lean on a card that bonuses furniture or general online
shopping purchases, or simply a flat-rate cashback card given the brand's
above-average price points.

stores.name: Jos. A. Bank
stores.intro:
Jos. A. Bank is a longtime American menswear retailer specializing in suits, dress
shirts, and business-casual clothing, sold through mall and strip-center stores as
well as online. It has been under the same parent company as Men's Wearhouse for
more than a decade, but the two brands operate separately and neither carries a
co-brand credit card tied to CreditOdds' tracked card set. A card that earns bonus
rewards on clothing or general online shopping purchases is the most useful pairing
here, since Jos. A. Bank frequently runs its own storewide promotions independent of
any bank partnership.

stores.name: Journeys
stores.intro: Journeys is a mall-based footwear chain catering to teens and young adults with skate, casual, and lifestyle shoes. Its website codes as online shopping or retail, where a rotating bonus or portal can help, while in-person purchases usually default to your flat-rate everyday card.

stores.name: K-Swiss
stores.intro:
K-Swiss is an American athletic footwear brand founded in the 1960s, best known for
its classic leather tennis sneakers as well as later training and lifestyle shoe
lines, sold direct-to-consumer online and through athletic retailers. The brand has
no co-brand credit card, so a shopper buying sneakers here should look to a card that
bonuses sporting goods or general online shopping spend rather than expecting any
brand-specific financing. K-Swiss has changed ownership several times over the years
but continues to operate as a standalone footwear label.

stores.name: Kaplan
stores.intro:
Kaplan is a long-running test preparation and professional education company,
offering courses and materials for standardized tests like the LSAT, MCAT, and GRE
as well as licensing exams for fields such as nursing and financial services. It
operates under Graham Holdings and has no co-brand credit card. Since Kaplan
purchases are typically a single online transaction rather than a recurring
category, a general online shopping rewards card or a flat-rate cashback card is the
most practical way to earn on tuition or course fees paid directly to Kaplan.

stores.name: Kay Jewelers
stores.intro:
Kay Jewelers is the largest banner inside Signet Jewelers, with around 850 U.S. stores
plus kay.com selling engagement rings, fashion jewelry, and watches. The Kay Jewelers
Long-Term Financing credit card from Comenity offers promotional financing on bigger
purchases. General-purpose cards typically code Kay as department store in person and
online shopping for web orders, and the free Vault Rewards program stacks with credit
card earnings.

stores.name: Kayak
stores.intro: Kayak is primarily a metasearch engine that compares flights, hotels, and cars across many sites, so most bookings actually complete on the airline, hotel, or partner agency. Where Kayak processes the charge directly it typically codes as travel and earns on a travel-bonus card, but the final coding often follows whichever provider ultimately bills you.

stores.name: Keds
stores.intro:
Keds is a classic American sneaker brand dating to 1916, known for its simple
canvas slip-on and lace-up shoes marketed mainly to women and long associated with
preppy, casual style. It is owned by Wolverine World Wide, which also owns brands
like Merrell and Sperry, and Keds carries no co-brand credit card of its own.
Shoppers stocking up on canvas sneakers here should default to a card that bonuses
clothing or general online shopping purchases, since there is no shoe-specific
rewards category to target.

stores.name: KEEN
stores.intro:
KEEN is an Oregon-based outdoor footwear brand best known for its toe-protecting
hybrid sandals, hiking boots, and work boots under the KEEN Utility line, sold
through its own site and outdoor specialty retailers. The company is privately held
and has no co-brand credit card. Shoppers buying hiking or outdoor gear here should
reach for a card that bonuses sporting goods purchases, or a general online shopping
or flat-rate cashback card if no category match applies.

stores.name: Kendra Scott
stores.intro:
Kendra Scott is an Austin-founded jewelry and accessories brand known for its
colorful stone-inlaid earrings, necklaces, and its in-store "Color Bar" customization
concept, sold through mall boutiques and its own website as well as select
department stores. The brand is privately held and has no co-brand credit card, so
there is no store-specific bonus category to chase. A card that earns elevated
rewards on general online shopping or department-store purchases, or a flat-rate
cashback card, is the best fit for jewelry and gift purchases here.

stores.name: Keurig
stores.intro:
Keurig is the single-serve coffee brewer company behind the K-Cup pod system,
selling brewing machines and a wide range of licensed coffee, tea, and cocoa pods
directly through its own site as well as major retailers. It's part of Keurig Dr
Pepper and has no co-brand credit card. Since purchases here are typically small
appliances or grocery-adjacent coffee pods rather than a distinct bonus category, a
general online shopping card or a flat-rate cashback card is the most reliable way to
earn rewards on Keurig orders.

stores.name: KFC
stores.intro:
KFC is a U.S. fried-chicken fast-food chain with about 4,000 domestic locations,
owned by Yum! Brands. Charges code as dining on every card we track, so any
dining-bonus card applies. Heavy regulars use the KFC Rewards loyalty program
for tiered point earning and free menu items, which stacks on top of any card's
bonus rate.

stores.name: Kiehl's
stores.intro: Kiehl's, the apothecary-style skincare label owned by L'Oreal, sells moisturizers and serums through kiehls.com, where purchases generally code as online shopping or general retail. Beauty does not carry its own bonus category, so a card that rewards online spending or a flat everyday rate tends to do best. In-store boutique visits usually fall to your flat-rate earnings.

stores.name: Kimpton Hotels
stores.intro: Kimpton is a boutique hotel brand under IHG, known for design-forward properties, evening wine hours, and pet-friendly policies. Stays code as a hotel or travel purchase, so a hotel-bonus or broad travel card earns well. Because Kimpton sits inside IHG, the co-branded IHG cards and loyalty program can add value on top of your card's standard travel rewards.

stores.name: King Soopers
stores.intro: King Soopers is Kroger's dominant Colorado chain, with many Front Range stores carrying full grocery plus fuel and pharmacy. Purchases at the supermarket register code as groceries on most cards, so pair it with a card that pays a bonus on supermarkets. Fuel bought at an attached King Soopers gas station instead codes as gas, where a separate fuel bonus can apply.

stores.name: Kirkland's
stores.intro: Kirkland's offers affordable home decor, wall art, and seasonal accents, with store purchases that usually code as general retail rather than a home-improvement warehouse, so hardware bonuses may not apply. Shopping kirklands.com normally codes as online shopping. For decor runs that add up, a card rewarding online spend or a flat everyday rate is the sensible pick.

stores.name: Kobo
stores.intro:
Kobo makes e-reader and e-ink tablet devices and operates a digital ebook and
audiobook store, positioned as one of the main alternatives to Amazon's Kindle
ecosystem. It is owned by Rakuten and has no co-brand credit card. Buying a Kobo
device is best treated as an electronics purchase for card-earning purposes, while
ongoing ebook purchases fall under general online shopping, so a card bonusing
either category, or a flat-rate cashback card, works well for Kobo spend.

stores.name: Kohl's
stores.intro:
Kohl's is a mid-tier department store with about 1,150 stores and an aggressive Kohl's
Cash rewards program. Most regular shoppers stack manufacturer sales, Kohl's coupons,
and Kohl's Cash, so card cashback is the smallest of several layers. The Kohl's Card
(issued by Capital One) gives in-house discounts but no MasterCard/Visa-network rewards.
For most shoppers, a department-store-bonus general-purpose card is the right answer.

stores.name: Kohler
stores.intro:
Kohler is a major American manufacturer of kitchen and bath fixtures, including
sinks, faucets, toilets, and bathtubs, alongside a broader industrial and hospitality
business that includes generators and golf resorts. Kohler products are sold direct
through its own site as well as through home improvement retailers and plumbing
suppliers, and the company has no co-brand credit card. Remodeling or fixture
purchases here pair best with a card that bonuses home improvement spend, or a
flat-rate cashback card for large one-off purchases.

stores.name: Krispy Kreme
stores.intro:
Krispy Kreme operates around 380 US shops plus thousands of grocery and convenience-store
cabinets, with the chain's Hot Light signaling fresh Original Glazed doughnuts coming off
the line. Purchases at a Krispy Kreme storefront or drive-thru, including dozens ordered
via the Krispy Kreme Rewards app, code as dining/restaurants. Cards with elevated dining
bonuses earn at full rate: Amex Gold (4x Membership Rewards), Chase Sapphire Preferred (3x
Ultimate Rewards), and Capital One SavorOne (3% unlimited cash back) are all strong picks.

stores.name: Kroger
stores.intro:
Kroger is the largest dedicated U.S. supermarket operator, running about 2,700 stores
under Kroger and a stack of regional banners (Ralphs, Fred Meyer, Smith's, King Soopers,
Harris Teeter, Fry's, and others). All code as groceries at the register and most do
online shopping via the Kroger app/site. Any standard supermarket-bonus card earns here.
Kroger's own fuel-points program layers on top of card cashback.

stores.name: Kwik Trip
stores.intro:
Kwik Trip (branded Kwik Star in Iowa) is a family-owned convenience and fuel chain
with more than 800 stores across Wisconsin, Minnesota, Iowa, Illinois, Michigan, and
South Dakota. The chain is known for its in-house bakery, dairy, and prepared foods
alongside a busy fuel operation. Pumps code as gas for credit card networks, so a gas
category bonus card will earn its multiplier. The Kwik Rewards loyalty program adds
per-gallon discounts and points on food, which can stack with a separate rewards card
at the pump.

stores.name: Kylie Cosmetics
stores.intro:
Kylie Cosmetics is the direct-to-consumer beauty brand founded by Kylie Jenner,
selling makeup, skincare, and fragrance products primarily through its own website
along with select retail partners like Ulta. The company has no co-brand credit
card, so there is no dedicated bonus category tied to the brand. A card that earns
elevated rewards on general online shopping, or a flat-rate cashback card, is the
most practical way to earn on purchases made directly through the Kylie Cosmetics
site.

stores.name: L.L.Bean
stores.intro:
L.L.Bean is a Maine-based outdoor retailer famous for its Freeport flagship and the
Bean Boot, operating about 60 U.S. stores plus a heavy catalog and DTC business at
llbean.com. The L.L.Bean Mastercard from Citi earns elevated points on direct L.L.Bean
purchases and offers a free shipping perk for cardholders, while general-purpose cards
code in-store visits as department store and web checkouts as online shopping.

stores.name: L'Occitane
stores.intro: L'Occitane en Provence stocks hand creams, shea butter, and fragrances, and orders at usa.loccitane.com normally code as online shopping or general retail. The French brand runs hundreds of US boutiques plus its site, but beauty has no dedicated rewards tier. Lean on a card with strong online-shopping or flat-rate earning rather than expecting a category multiplier here.

stores.name: LA Fitness
stores.intro: LA Fitness runs hundreds of full-service health clubs with pools, classes and basketball courts across the country. Recurring membership dues seldom trigger a bonus category, so they typically land on a card's flat-rate earning, and a card with a fitness or wellness statement credit is the best way to claw back value.

stores.name: La-Z-Boy
stores.intro: La-Z-Boy is the recliner maker whose namesake chairs and sofas are sold through galleries that usually code as general retail or a furniture merchant rather than a home-improvement warehouse. That distinction means a hardware-store bonus may not apply. Orders at la-z-boy.com normally code as online shopping, so a high flat-rate or online card is the practical choice.

stores.name: Lands' End
stores.intro:
Lands' End is a casual classics apparel brand with deep catalog roots, sold mostly
through landsend.com and via shop-in-shop counters that were historically inside many
Kohl's department stores. Direct site purchases typically code as online shopping,
while Lands' End merchandise bought at Kohl's codes under the host retailer and earns
Kohl's Rewards. There is no current standalone Lands' End branded credit card in the
U.S. market.

stores.name: Lane Bryant
stores.intro: Lane Bryant is a leading plus-size women's apparel retailer offering everything from denim to intimates. Buying on the website codes as online shopping or general retail and can earn via a portal, but in-store transactions usually default to a flat-rate card with no clothing-specific bonus.

stores.name: Laneige
stores.intro:
Laneige is a popular Korean skincare and beauty brand owned by Amorepacific, best known for
its Lip Sleeping Mask and Water Bank line, sold direct at us.laneige.com and through
Sephora, Ulta, and Amazon. There is no Laneige co-brand credit card, so a direct purchase
codes as online shopping, while the same products at a beauty retailer can earn under that
store's rate.

stores.name: LastPass
stores.intro:
LastPass is a password manager and digital identity security service that stores
and autofills logins, generates strong passwords, and offers dark web monitoring and
family sharing plans on a subscription basis. It is sold purely as software, has no
physical retail presence, and carries no co-brand credit card. Subscription charges
here are best captured with a general online shopping or subscription-friendly
rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card, since there is no dedicated software or
security spending category on most cards.

stores.name: LATAM Airlines
stores.intro:
LATAM Airlines is the largest airline group in Latin America, formed from the
merger of Chile's LAN Airlines and Brazil's TAM, and it operates an extensive
network across South America along with routes to North America, Europe, and
Oceania. It is a member of the oneworld alliance, which lets its LATAM Pass loyalty
program interact with partners like American Airlines. LATAM has no U.S. co-brand
credit card, so flyers should rely on a general travel or airline-category rewards
card, or transferable points from a flexible travel card, to offset fares booked
directly with LATAM.

stores.name: Lavazza
stores.intro:
Lavazza is an Italian coffee company, family-owned since 1895, selling roasted
beans, ground coffee, and single-serve pods along with espresso machines through its
U.S. online store and retail partners. It has no co-brand credit card tied to any
major U.S. issuer. Coffee and equipment purchases made directly through Lavazza's
site are best treated as general online shopping for rewards purposes, so a card
bonusing that category, or a flat-rate cashback card, is the practical choice.

stores.name: Lee
stores.intro:
Lee is one of America's oldest denim brands, founded in 1889 and known for jeans,
workwear, and casual clothing sold through its own stores, website, and a wide range
of department and mass retailers. It is owned by Kontoor Brands, which also owns
Wrangler, and Lee has no co-brand credit card. A shopper buying jeans or casual wear
directly from Lee should look to a card that bonuses clothing or general online
shopping purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card if no category applies.

stores.name: LegalZoom
stores.intro:
LegalZoom is an online legal services company that helps individuals and small
businesses handle tasks like LLC formation, wills, trademarks, and legal document
preparation, often paired with attorney consultation add-ons. It operates entirely
online and has no co-brand credit card. Since legal services spending doesn't fall
under a dedicated bonus category on most rewards cards, a general online shopping
card or a flat-rate cashback card is the most sensible way to earn on LegalZoom
purchases.

stores.name: LEGO Store
stores.intro: The LEGO Store sells building sets in mall locations and through lego.com, where most rewards-seekers shop. Online orders generally code as online shopping, while a physical store often rings up as a department store or general retail. There is no broad bonus category for toys, so a flat-rate or online-shopping card usually works best.

stores.name: Lenovo
stores.intro: Lenovo, maker of ThinkPad laptops and a wide PC lineup, sells configurable machines directly through its website with frequent promotions.
