Pizza Hut is a U.S. pizza chain with about 6,500 domestic locations, owned by
Yum! Brands. Charges code as dining on every card we track. Any dining-bonus card
applies. The Hut Rewards loyalty program in the app issues per-dollar points
redeemable for free items, stackable with card bonuses.

stores.name: Planet Fitness
stores.intro: Planet Fitness operates more than 2,400 low-cost gyms known for the Judgement Free Zone and cheap monthly plans. Gym memberships are rarely a standalone bonus category, so dues usually earn at your card's flat rate unless you carry a card that offers a fitness or wellness credit to offset the bill.

stores.name: PlayStation Store
stores.intro:
The PlayStation Store is Sony's digital storefront for PS4 and PS5 games, downloadable
content, PlayStation Plus subscriptions, and movies. Purchases happen directly through
the console or on store.playstation.com. Card networks often code PlayStation Store
charges as digital goods or entertainment, which can trigger entertainment category
bonuses on cards like the Capital One SavorOne.

PlayStation Plus subscription tiers and Game Pass-style game catalogs may also fall
under streaming services coding on some issuers, which means the Amex Blue Cash
Preferred can occasionally earn 6 percent. Online shopping bonus cards such as Bank
of America Customized Cash and Chase Freedom Flex during rotating online quarters
remain reliable defaults for game and DLC purchases.

stores.name: Polk Audio
stores.intro:
Polk Audio is a Maryland founded speaker and home theater brand dating to 1972, known
for bookshelf and floorstanding speakers, soundbars, and subwoofers sold direct online
and through electronics retailers. It operates under SoundUnited, the audio group that
also owns Definitive Technology, Denon, and Marantz. There is no Polk Audio co-brand
credit card, so a card that earns bonus rewards at electronics stores, or one that
boosts online shopping purchases when buying direct from polkaudio.com, is the better
option for audio equipment purchases.

stores.name: Polo Ralph Lauren
stores.intro:
Polo Ralph Lauren is the flagship label of Ralph Lauren Corporation, sold through
about 140 U.S. full-price, factory, and Rugby-style stores along with ralphlauren.com
and an extensive department store wholesale presence. Direct purchases typically code
as department store in person and online shopping for web orders. The free Ralph
Lauren Rewards program offers points and member perks, but the brand does not run a
consumer co-brand credit card in the U.S.

stores.name: Popeyes
stores.intro:
Popeyes is a U.S. Louisiana-style fried chicken chain with about 3,000 domestic
locations, owned by Restaurant Brands International. Charges code as dining on
every card we track. Any dining-bonus card (Amex Gold 4x, Sapphire Preferred 3x,
SavorOne 3%) applies. The Popeyes Rewards app issues per-purchase points
redeemable for free items, stackable with card bonuses.

stores.name: Portillo's
stores.intro: Portillo's is a Chicago-rooted chain famous for its Italian beef sandwiches, Chicago-style hot dogs, and chocolate cake shakes. Like most sit-down and counter-service spots, a tab here codes as dining or restaurants, so the smart move is a card that earns a bonus rate on dining rather than a flat-rate everyday card.

stores.name: Poshmark
stores.intro:
Poshmark is a social commerce marketplace focused on secondhand fashion, beauty, and
accessories, where individual sellers list items and ship through prepaid Poshmark
labels. The platform was acquired by Korean tech company Naver in 2023. Buyer
purchases code as online shopping for credit card networks, so any online-shopping
category bonus card will earn its full multiplier. Poshmark does not have a co-brand
credit card, so the best earn comes from a general 3-5x online-shopping rewards card.

stores.name: Pottery Barn
stores.intro:
Pottery Barn is a U.S. home furnishings retailer with about 200 stores, owned by
Williams-Sonoma Inc. (also West Elm, Pottery Barn Kids). Stores code as
furniture/home improvement; potterybarn.com codes as online shopping. The Key
Rewards loyalty program is free, spans all Williams-Sonoma sister brands, and
earns 2% in Key Rewards points; pairing it with the Key Rewards co-branded
Mastercard adds an additional 5% on Williams-Sonoma family purchases.

stores.name: Pottery Barn Kids
stores.intro:
Pottery Barn Kids is the kids and baby furnishings banner of Williams-Sonoma Inc, with
about 80 U.S. stores plus potterybarnkids.com selling cribs, bedding, nursery decor,
and gear. The Key Rewards Visa from Capital One earns elevated points across all WSI
brands and stacks with the free Key Rewards program, which is helpful for big-ticket
nursery setups. General-purpose cards typically code purchases as home improvement or
department store in person and online shopping on the web.

stores.name: Powell's Books
stores.intro: Powell's Books, the famed Portland independent, sells new and used titles in store and through powells.com. Online orders generally code as online shopping, while the physical City of Books often rings up as general retail. A flat-rate card or one with an online-shopping bonus is the practical choice for book hauls.

stores.name: Price Chopper
stores.intro:
Price Chopper, operated by Northeast Grocery, runs about 130 supermarkets across New
York, Vermont, Connecticut, Pennsylvania, Massachusetts, and New Hampshire. Many
remodeled locations carry the sister Market 32 banner. Both code as U.S. supermarkets,
so grocery rewards cards (Amex Gold, Blue Cash Preferred, Citi Custom Cash) earn full
multipliers. The AdvantEdge loyalty program ties shoppers into fuel discount partners
like Sunoco, which can stack with a separate gas-category card at the pump.

stores.name: Priceline
stores.intro: Priceline is a large online travel agency known for opaque Express Deals and Name Your Own Price bookings. Purchases generally code as travel and earn on cards with a travel bonus, though some flights and hotels booked through it may code under the underlying supplier. A flexible travel card usually rewards these bookings well across both prepaid and post-pay charges.

stores.name: Princess Cruises
stores.intro: Princess Cruises operates a large global fleet and is well known for Alaska and ocean itineraries. Bookings made with the line typically code as travel and earn on cards carrying a travel bonus, but cruise transactions do not always read cleanly. A flexible travel card or a dependable flat-rate card is the conservative choice for both fare and onboard spend.

stores.name: Prose
stores.intro:
Prose is a direct-to-consumer haircare brand that sells custom-formulated shampoo,
conditioner, and styling products based on an online quiz that asks about hair type,
goals, and local climate. Each order is mixed and bottled to match the individual
customer rather than sold as a fixed product line, and the brand ships on a recurring
subscription basis. There is no Prose co-brand credit card, so a card that earns bonus
rewards on online shopping purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card, is the simplest
way to earn something back on a Prose order or subscription renewal.

stores.name: Public Goods
stores.intro:
Public Goods is a membership based online retailer selling sustainably sourced
household staples, personal care items, and pantry goods under its own uniform, minimalist
branded packaging rather than reselling other companies' products. Shoppers pay a
yearly membership fee for access to member pricing across categories like cleaning
supplies, toiletries, and kitchen basics. There is no Public Goods co-brand credit
card, so a card that earns bonus rewards on online shopping purchases, or a flat-rate
cashback card, is the best way to earn rewards on an order.

affiliate.offer: 10% off your first purchase with code RMN10

stores.name: Publix
stores.intro:
Publix is the largest employee-owned U.S. supermarket chain, with about 1,400 stores
concentrated in the Southeast. Pricing skews higher than at mass-market grocers like
Walmart Neighborhood Market or Aldi, which makes a strong grocery-category card more
valuable per visit. Publix codes cleanly as groceries on every card we track.

stores.name: Puffy
stores.intro:
Puffy is a direct-to-consumer mattress and bedding brand that ships foam mattresses,
pillows, and sheets in a box, competing with other bed-in-a-box companies like Casper
and Nectar on long sleep trials and lifetime warranties sold entirely online rather
than through physical showrooms. The lineup has expanded into adjustable bed frames
and bedroom furniture over time. There is no Puffy co-brand credit card, so a card
that earns bonus rewards at furniture stores, or one that boosts online shopping
purchases, is the better fit for a mattress or bedding purchase.

stores.name: Purple Carrot
stores.intro:
Purple Carrot is a meal kit delivery service built entirely around plant-based
recipes, shipping pre-portioned vegan ingredients and instructions to subscribers on
a weekly basis as an alternative to omnivore-focused kit services like HelloFresh or
Blue Apron. The company markets itself to both longtime vegans and meat eaters
looking to add more plant-based meals into a regular rotation. There is no Purple
Carrot co-brand credit card, so a dining category card, or one that boosts online
shopping purchases, is the practical choice for a recurring meal kit subscription
charge.

stores.name: Qatar Airways
stores.intro: Qatar Airways is the Doha-based Oneworld carrier celebrated for its Qsuite business class and a wide network from several US cities. Ticket purchases code as an airline or travel charge, the sweet spot for an airline or general travel bonus card. Points from flexible transferable-currency cards can move to its Privilege Club, often making transfers more rewarding than paying cash directly.

stores.name: Qdoba
stores.intro:
Qdoba Mexican Eats operates roughly 750 fast-casual locations, competing directly with
Chipotle in the burritos-bowls-tacos lane while differentiating with free queso and
guacamole on entrees. Orders placed in-store, at the counter, or through the Qdoba Rewards
app all code as dining/restaurants. That makes any card with a dining bonus a strong fit:
the Chase Sapphire Reserve earns 4x on dining, Amex Gold earns 4x, and the Capital One
SavorOne earns 3% unlimited cash back on every Qdoba purchase.

stores.name: QFC
stores.intro: QFC, short for Quality Food Centers, is Kroger's upscale Seattle-area banner known for fresh and prepared foods. Spending at the store codes as groceries on most rewards cards, so reach for whatever supermarket multiplier you carry. As with other Kroger banners, any attached fuel center rings up as gas rather than groceries, so plan accordingly.

stores.name: Quiksilver
stores.intro:
Quiksilver is a boardsports apparel and footwear brand under the Boardriders group, sold
direct at quiksilver.com and through surf, sporting, and department stores. There is no
Quiksilver co-brand credit card, so a card with a clothing or online shopping bonus, or a
flat-rate cashback card, is the best way to earn.

stores.name: QuikTrip
stores.intro:
QuikTrip is a South-and-Midwest gas-and-convenience chain with about 1,000 stores,
consistently ranked among the highest customer-satisfaction convenience operators
in the U.S. Fuel codes as gas on every card we track; in-store purchases typically
code as convenience. The QT Rewards app offers per-gallon savings; pair it with a
4-5% general gas card for the cleanest stack.

stores.name: Quill
stores.intro:
Quill is a business-to-business office supply retailer selling paper, ink and
toner, breakroom items, cleaning products, and technology to small businesses and
offices, largely through catalog and online ordering rather than physical stores.
Staples acquired Quill in 1998 and continues to run it as a distinct brand aimed at
business customers who order in bulk. There is no Quill co-brand credit card, so an
office supply category card, or a flat-rate business cashback card, is the better
option for earning rewards on Quill purchases.

stores.name: QVC
stores.intro:
QVC is a multichannel retailer that sells via live television broadcasts, qvc.com, and
mobile apps, owned by Qurate Retail Group (which also runs HSN). The merchandise mix
spans apparel, jewelry, beauty, kitchen, electronics, and home goods, often pitched
by celebrity hosts in real time. QVC purchases code as online shopping or general
merchandise for credit card networks, so an online-shopping category bonus card will
generally earn its multiplier. QVC also offers its Easy Pay installment program at
checkout, billed against the saved card on file.

stores.name: RaceTrac
stores.intro:
RaceTrac is a family-owned Southeast U.S. convenience and fuel chain with more than
800 stores across Georgia, Florida, Texas, Tennessee, Louisiana, and surrounding
states. Fuel pumps code as gas, so any gas category bonus card will earn its
multiplier. Inside-store food and drinks (the chain leans heavily on coffee and
prepared foods) typically code as convenience store. The RaceTrac Rewards loyalty
program adds per-gallon discounts and points on food that stack with a separate gas
rewards card.

stores.name: Radisson Hotel Group
stores.intro:
Radisson Hotel Group operates a global portfolio that includes Radisson Collection, Radisson
Blu, Radisson, Radisson RED, Park Plaza, Park Inn, and Country Inn & Suites, spanning roughly
1,100 hotels worldwide. In the Americas, Radisson Hotels Americas was sold to Choice Hotels
in 2022, which complicates loyalty mapping: US stays now mostly earn Choice Privileges,
while EMEA/APAC stays earn Radisson Rewards. Radisson stays code as lodging/hotels. Travel
cards with hotel bonuses (Amex Gold 3x on prepaid, Chase Sapphire Reserve 4x direct) earn full rate.

stores.name: rag & bone
stores.intro:
rag & bone is a New York contemporary fashion label founded in the mid-2000s, built its
name on premium denim and has since expanded into a full ready-to-wear line, footwear, and
accessories with a downtown, workwear-influenced aesthetic. The brand sells through its own
stores and site as well as upscale department stores. There's no rag & bone co-brand
credit card, so a department-store rewards card fits purchases made through a wholesale
partner, while a general online shopping or flat-rate cashback card works best for direct
orders.

stores.name: Raising Cane's
stores.intro:
Raising Cane's Chicken Fingers is a Baton Rouge-based quick-service chain serving
a focused menu of chicken finger combos, Texas toast, crinkle-cut fries, and the
signature Cane's Sauce. The chain has expanded from a single Louisiana location to
over 800 restaurants nationwide. Raising Cane's transactions code as restaurants
on Visa, Mastercard, and Amex networks, which lights up most dining bonus
categories.

The Amex Gold pays 4x points on U.S. restaurants for one of the highest dining
rates available, while the Chase Sapphire Reserve and Capital One Savor both pay
3x or more. The Capital One SavorOne is a strong no-annual-fee pick at 3 percent
on dining. Cane's runs no in-house loyalty program of its own, so credit card
rewards are the primary cash back path.

stores.name: Rakuten
stores.intro: Rakuten is a cash-back shopping portal that earns rebates when you click through to partner retailers, plus its own marketplace. Any direct purchases code as online shopping, but the bigger play is stacking Rakuten's cash back on top of a rewarding card at checkout. Treat the portal as a multiplier layered over your usual online-shopping card.

stores.name: Raley's
stores.intro:
Raley's is a family-owned, Northern California based supermarket chain with about 120
stores across California and Nevada, including the Bel Air, Nob Hill Foods, and Raley's
O-N-E Market banners. The chain emphasizes fresh produce, in-house meat and seafood,
and a strong private-label program. Raley's codes as a U.S. supermarket, so any
grocery category card (Amex Gold, Blue Cash Preferred, Citi Custom Cash) earns full
multipliers. The Something Extra loyalty program adds digital coupon savings and
occasional fuel discounts at partner stations.

stores.name: Ralphs
stores.intro:
Ralphs is the Southern California banner of Kroger, operating roughly 180 stores across
the Los Angeles and San Diego areas. It codes cleanly as a U.S. supermarket for credit
card networks, so grocery bonus cards earn their full rate. Ralphs is also part of the
Kroger fuel points program (the chain runs co-located fuel centers in some locations),
and the store sells third-party gift cards that can stretch a grocery multiplier into
other spending categories.

stores.name: Raymour & Flanigan
stores.intro: Raymour & Flanigan is a large Northeast furniture retailer whose showroom purchases tend to code as general retail or a furniture merchant, not a true home-improvement warehouse, so improvement-store bonuses may not apply. Online orders at raymourflanigan.com usually code as online shopping. Given big furniture tickets, a strong flat-rate or online card maximizes the return.

stores.name: Razer
stores.intro:
Razer is a Singapore and California based gaming hardware company known for its
green snake logo and Chroma RGB lighting, selling gaming laptops, keyboards, mice,
headsets, and accessories aimed at PC and console gamers. The company is publicly
traded and also runs Razer Gold, a virtual currency used across many game platforms.
There is no Razer co-brand credit card, so a card that earns bonus rewards at
electronics stores, or one that boosts online shopping purchases when ordering
direct from razer.com, is the better fit for gaming gear purchases.

stores.name: RC Willey
stores.intro:
RC Willey Home Furnishings is a large-format furniture, mattress, electronics, and
appliance retailer based in Utah with stores across the Mountain West, Nevada, and
California. Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway acquired the company in 1995 and has
kept its original management and regional footprint largely intact since. There is no
RC Willey co-brand credit card, so a card that earns bonus rewards at furniture
stores, or one that boosts home improvement or electronics purchases, is the best fit
for a large furniture or appliance purchase.

stores.name: Rebecca Minkoff
stores.intro:
Rebecca Minkoff is a New York founded fashion label started by its namesake designer,
known for handbags like the MAB and Mini M.A.C. as well as ready-to-wear clothing,
shoes, and accessories sold through its own stores, website, and department store
partners like Nordstrom and Bloomingdale's. The brand built its early following on
accessible, trend-driven leather goods. There is no Rebecca Minkoff co-brand credit
card, so a card that earns bonus rewards on online shopping or clothing purchases is
the better fit for a handbag or apparel order.

stores.name: Red Lobster
stores.intro: Red Lobster is the country's largest casual seafood chain, known for Cheddar Bay Biscuits and its annual Endless Shrimp promotion. Meals run through as dining or restaurant purchases on nearly every card, so reach for one that rewards restaurant spending if you want more than the base rate back on dinner.

stores.name: Red Robin
stores.intro:
Red Robin Gourmet Burgers operates about 500 casual-dining restaurants centered on
customizable burgers like the Royal Red Robin and Banzai Burger, plus Bottomless Steak Fries
refills that anchor the brand's table-service value pitch. The chain skews family-friendly
with a kids menu and milkshake program. Red Robin transactions code as dining/restaurants
on every card, including orders placed through Red Robin Royalty loyalty. Dining-bonus
cards earn full rate: Amex Gold (4x), Chase Sapphire Reserve (4x), Capital One SavorOne (3%).

stores.name: Red Roof Inn
stores.intro: Red Roof Inn is a US economy lodging chain with hundreds of pet-friendly properties, popular for budget road-trip stays. Direct bookings and stays code as a hotel or travel purchase, which a card with a hotel or broad travel bonus rewards. Booking through a card issuer's travel portal can boost earnings further, though it may limit any loyalty credit from the Red Roof program.

stores.name: Reef
stores.intro:
Reef is a California born footwear brand best known for its sandals, especially
bottle-opener flip-flops and other surf-culture styles, along with boots and
casual shoes sold online and through outdoor and surf retailers. The brand grew out
of the beach and surf scene and remains closely tied to that lifestyle marketing.
There is no Reef co-brand credit card, so a card that earns bonus rewards on online
shopping or clothing and footwear purchases, or a flat-rate cashback card, is the
practical choice when buying from reef.com.

stores.name: Regal Cinemas
stores.intro: Regal Cinemas operates one of the largest US theater circuits, with hundreds of multiplexes and the Regal Unlimited subscription pass. Tickets and concessions code as entertainment or general merchandise rather than dining, so a card with an entertainment bonus is the better play. Buying tickets through its app or site keeps the charge with Regal instead of a third-party fee.

stores.name: REI
stores.intro:
REI is a member-owned outdoor co-op with about 180 U.S. stores, selling apparel,
camping, climbing, and cycling gear. REI codes under its own merchant category
on several cards (notably the Co-op Mastercard, which earns 5% back at REI for
Co-op Members) and as online shopping for rei.com purchases otherwise. The $30
one-time Co-op membership earns an annual member dividend (~10% of purchases),
plus member-only sales — typically the most valuable lever for heavy REI buyers
beyond any card bonus.

stores.name: Rent the Runway
stores.intro:
Rent the Runway is a clothing and accessory rental service that lets members borrow
designer dresses, workwear, and outerwear for a set period or on a rotating monthly
subscription instead of buying them outright, returning items in prepaid packaging
when finished. The company is publicly traded and works with hundreds of designer
and contemporary brands. There is no Rent the Runway co-brand credit card, so a card
that earns bonus rewards on online shopping or clothing purchases, or a flat-rate
cashback card, is the best fit for a rental order or membership charge.

stores.name: ResortPass
stores.intro:
ResortPass is a booking platform that sells day access to hotel and resort
amenities, such as pools, cabanas, and spas, to guests who are not staying overnight
at the property. It partners with hotel chains and independent resorts across the
United States to sell passes by the day, letting travelers and locals use luxury
facilities without booking a room. There is no ResortPass co-brand credit card, so a
general travel category card, or a flat-rate cashback card, is the practical way to
earn rewards on a day pass purchase.

stores.name: Restaurant.com
stores.intro:
Restaurant.com sells discounted gift certificates redeemable at thousands of
independent and chain restaurants across the United States, letting diners buy a
certificate online for less than its face value and use it toward a meal at a
participating location. The site also bundles certificates with local deals and gift
cards for other retail and entertainment categories. There is no Restaurant.com
co-brand credit card, so a dining category card, or a flat-rate cashback card, is the
better choice for purchases made on the site.

stores.name: Restoration Hardware
stores.intro:
Restoration Hardware, now branded as RH, is an upscale home furnishings retailer with
around 70 large-format Galleries plus rh.com selling furniture, lighting, textiles,
and outdoor. The RH Members Program is a paid annual membership that grants 25 percent
off most full-price items and stacks with credit card rewards. General-purpose cards
typically code RH as home improvement or department store in person and online
shopping for web orders.

stores.name: Reverb
stores.intro:
Reverb is an online marketplace dedicated to musical instruments and gear, where
individual sellers and music shops list new, used, and vintage guitars, keyboards,
amps, and pedals for musicians to buy directly from one another. A large share of the
listings are used or vintage equipment, giving it a resale marketplace feel similar
to eBay but focused entirely on music gear. There is no Reverb co-brand credit card,
so a card that earns bonus rewards on online shopping purchases, or a flat-rate
cashback card, is the practical way to earn rewards on a purchase.

stores.name: Revolve
stores.intro: Revolve is an online fashion retailer targeting younger shoppers with trend-driven clothing, shoes, and accessories. Purchases code as online shopping, and while it can resemble a department store, there is no broad apparel bonus on most cards. A flat-rate or online-retail card is the most reliable way to earn on your orders.

stores.name: Ria Money Transfer
stores.intro:
Ria Money Transfer is one of the largest international remittance networks, moving money
to more than 190 countries through a mix of physical agent locations, retail partners, and
a direct app and website. It's owned by Euronet Worldwide and competes head to head with
Western Union and MoneyGram on cross-border transfers, bill pay, and mobile top-ups. There
is no Ria co-brand credit card, so the practical move is a flat-rate cashback card for the
transfer fee itself, though shoppers should confirm with their card issuer whether a money
transfer service is coded as a purchase or a cash advance before relying on it for rewards.

stores.name: Rite Aid
stores.intro:
Rite Aid is the third-largest U.S. pharmacy chain at about 1,700 stores, primarily in
the Northeast and West Coast. Rite Aid codes cleanly as a drugstore on every card we
track, so any drugstore-bonus card will earn here. The chain's BonusCash and wellness+
rewards programs layer on top of card cashback.

stores.name: Ro
stores.intro: Ro runs a direct-to-consumer telehealth platform covering weight management, men's health and primary care, billing through online subscriptions rather than a retail pharmacy counter. Its charges tend to code as online shopping or general retail, so the right play is a card with an online or flat-rate everyday bonus rather than chasing a drugstore category.

stores.name: Roborock
stores.intro:
Roborock is a Beijing based robotics company best known for its lineup of robot vacuums and
vacuum and mop combo units, which compete directly with iRobot's Roomba line on navigation,
suction power, and self-emptying dock features. The brand also makes handheld and cordless
vacuums and has expanded into other smart home cleaning devices. Roborock does not have a
co-brand credit card, so shoppers buying direct at roborock.com or through a retailer like
Amazon or Best Buy are better off leaning on an electronics or general online shopping
rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card, to earn on the purchase.

stores.name: RockAuto
stores.intro: RockAuto is an online-only retailer of auto parts, selling everything from filters to brake kits at deep discounts with no brick-and-mortar locations. Since every order runs through rockauto.com, the charge codes as online shopping or general retail rather than as an auto service. A card that rewards online purchases, or a solid flat-rate card, is the simplest way to earn on parts you install yourself.

stores.name: Rooms To Go
stores.intro:
Rooms To Go is a Florida-based furniture retailer that pioneered the room-package
concept, bundling sofa, loveseat, tables, lamps, and rug into a single price. The
chain runs over 250 showrooms across the Southeast, Texas, and Puerto Rico. Furniture
purchases code as general retail on most rewards cards, so they sit outside dedicated
home improvement bonuses.

Five-piece living room packages and bedroom sets often clear $2,000, making Rooms To
Go a useful place to hit signup bonus thresholds on cards like the Chase Sapphire
Preferred or Capital One Venture. The retailer also markets a Synchrony-issued Rooms
To Go credit card with deferred-interest financing, but a rewards card paid in full
generally produces more value.

stores.name: Rosetta Stone
stores.intro:
Rosetta Stone is a language learning company that built its brand on distinctive
immersion-based software, once sold in boxed CD-ROM kits and now offered as a subscription
app and website covering dozens of languages. It's owned by IXL Learning, an education
technology company that also operates Wyzant and other learning platforms. There's no
Rosetta Stone co-brand credit card, so a subscription purchase or lifetime plan is best
paid with a general online shopping or flat-rate cashback card, since language learning
doesn't map to any dedicated bonus category most issuers track.

stores.name: Ross Dress for Less
stores.intro:
Ross Dress for Less is a U.S. off-price retail chain with about 1,800 stores
selling apparel, accessories, and home goods at discount prices. Codes as
department stores on most cards. There's no co-branded Ross credit card, so the
best pairings are general department-store-bonus cards (Wells Fargo Active Cash
flat 2%, U.S. Bank Shopper Cash Rewards if Ross is selected) or flat-rate
cashback cards. Ross doesn't run a loyalty program.

stores.name: Round Table Pizza
stores.intro: Round Table Pizza is a West Coast favorite known for its Garlic Parmesan Twists and the King Arthur's Supreme. Whether you dine in, carry out, or order delivery, the purchase codes as dining on most cards, so a restaurant bonus card earns more than leaving it on a flat-rate option.

stores.name: Rover
stores.intro: Rover is an online marketplace connecting pet owners with dog walkers, sitters, and boarding hosts. Payments run through its platform and generally code as online shopping or general services rather than a dedicated bonus category, so they earn on cards with an online or flat-rate bonus. A reliable everyday card is the practical pick for booking pet care here.

stores.name: Roxy
stores.intro:
Roxy is a women's surf, snow, and beach apparel brand under the Boardriders group alongside
Quiksilver and DC Shoes, sold direct at roxy.com and through sporting and department stores.
There is no Roxy co-brand credit card, so purchases earn best on a card with a clothing or
online shopping category bonus.

stores.name: Royal Caribbean
stores.intro: Royal Caribbean runs some of the largest cruise ships afloat, and fares booked through the line generally code as travel, earning on cards with a broad travel bonus. Cruise lines sometimes code inconsistently, so a flat-rate travel card or one that counts cruises as travel is the safe play, and onboard spending may post separately.

stores.name: Royal Farms
stores.intro: Royal Farms is a Mid-Atlantic convenience chain known for its fried chicken and large fuel canopies across Maryland, Delaware, and nearby states. Fuel purchases code as gas, while the store and food counter often code as convenience or retail, so the split matters. Use a gas rewards card at the pump, and a flat-rate or dining-leaning card for the famous chicken and snacks inside.

stores.name: RTIC Outdoors
stores.intro:
RTIC Outdoors makes coolers, tumblers, drinkware, and outdoor gear positioned as a
lower-priced alternative to premium brands like YETI, selling largely direct to
consumers online along with placement in big-box retailers. The lineup spans hard
and soft coolers, insulated bottles, and camp accessories aimed at hunters, anglers,
and tailgaters. There is no RTIC co-brand credit card, so a card that earns bonus
rewards on sporting goods, or one that boosts online shopping purchases, is the
better choice when buying direct from rticoutdoors.com.

stores.name: Rue La La
stores.intro:
Rue La La is a members-only flash sale retailer, launching limited-time discounted events
on apparel, accessories, home goods, and beauty from both established and boutique brands.
Founded in Boston in 2007, it's now part of Rue Gilt Groupe, the same corporate family that
also runs Gilt and Shop Premium Outlets. There's no Rue La La co-brand credit card, so the
best rewards angle for a flash sale purchase is an online shopping or general apparel
category card, or a flat-rate cashback card if the specific category isn't covered.

stores.name: Rugs USA
stores.intro:
Rugs USA is a direct to consumer online retailer specializing in area rugs, runners, and
outdoor rugs, spanning machine made, hand tufted, and hand knotted styles across a wide
range of price points and design aesthetics. The company sells almost exclusively online,
regularly running large sitewide sales on its inventory. There's no Rugs USA co-brand
credit card, so a home goods purchase there is best charged to a home improvement rewards
card if one is available, or otherwise a general online shopping or flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Rural King
stores.intro: Rural King is a farm-and-home chain selling everything from feed and tools to firearms and apparel, and its stores can code as a home-improvement or farm-supply merchant or as general retail. A home-improvement bonus may apply but is not assured given the mixed inventory. Purchases at ruralking.com generally code as online shopping, so a flat-rate or online card is a safe default.

stores.name: Ruth's Chris Steak House
stores.intro:
Ruth's Chris Steak House, now owned by Darden Restaurants, operates about 150 upscale
steakhouses known for USDA Prime steaks served on 500-degree plates with sizzling butter,
plus signature sides like the Sweet Potato Casserole. Average check sizes run high enough
that a well-chosen dining card meaningfully changes the bill economics. Ruth's Chris codes
as dining/restaurants on every credit card. Top picks here include the Amex Gold (4x
Membership Rewards), Chase Sapphire Reserve (4x Ultimate Rewards), and Capital One Savor (3%).

stores.name: Safeway
stores.intro:
Safeway is one of Albertsons' largest banners, running about 900 stores primarily in the
Western U.S. Safeway codes as groceries at the register and most online ordering happens
through safeway.com or DoorDash partnerships. Safeway's own Just for U digital coupons
stack with whatever card-side cashback you earn at checkout.

stores.name: Saks Fifth Avenue
stores.intro:
Saks Fifth Avenue is a high-end department store with about 40 full-line stores plus the
much larger Saks Off 5th outlet network. Average ticket sizes are higher than at
mass-market department chains, which makes a high-rate department-store category card
more valuable here than at, say, JCPenney. Saks codes as a department store in-store and
online shopping on saksfifthavenue.com, so general-purpose category bonuses apply.

stores.name: Saks OFF 5TH
stores.intro:
Saks OFF 5TH is the off-price arm of Saks Fifth Avenue, with about 90 outlet stores
across the U.S. and Canada plus its own e-commerce site. The format carries designer
apparel, shoes, and accessories at substantial discounts to the parent retailer. Saks
OFF 5TH codes as a department store, so a department-store category bonus card will
earn its multiplier. The SaksFirst Mastercard (issued by Capital One) earns elevated
points at both Saks Fifth Avenue and Saks OFF 5TH, with tiered status benefits at
higher spend thresholds.

stores.name: Sally Beauty
stores.intro:
Sally Beauty is the U.S. retail banner of Sally Beauty Holdings, with about 2,400
stores plus sallybeauty.com selling professional hair color, salon supplies, and at
home beauty products. The free Sally Beauty Rewards program offers points and member
pricing, while a separate Pro Card serves licensed cosmetologists. General-purpose
cards typically code Sally Beauty as department store in person and online shopping
for web orders.

stores.name: Sam Edelman
stores.intro:
Sam Edelman is a contemporary footwear and accessories brand owned by Caleres, sold direct
at samedelman.com and through department stores. There is no Sam Edelman co-brand credit
card, so a purchase codes as clothing or online shopping, and shoppers earn the most with a
card that carries a clothing or online shopping bonus.

stores.name: Sam's Club
stores.intro:
Sam's Club is Walmart's warehouse-club arm, with about 600 U.S. clubs and a steadily
growing online and Scan & Go catalog. Member tiers (Club vs. Plus) affect free-shipping
and curbside perks but not the rewards math at the register. Sam's accepts Visa,
Mastercard, Amex, and Discover, and Sam's Club gas pumps code as wholesale clubs rather
than gas stations — the same rule as Costco.

stores.name: Samsonite
stores.intro:
Samsonite is the world's largest luggage maker, selling suitcases, carry-ons, and travel
bags direct at samsonite.com and through department stores and travel retailers. There is no
Samsonite co-brand credit card, so a purchase codes as a department store or online shopping
charge. Since luggage is bought ahead of travel, a good pairing is a card with a department
store or online shopping bonus, and some travel cards also treat luggage as a
travel-adjacent purchase.

stores.name: Samsung
stores.intro: Samsung sells phones, TVs, appliances and accessories through Samsung.com and its retail partners. Purchases on its site usually code as online shopping or general merchandise, so a card with an online or everyday bonus works best, while in-store buys at third-party retailers often fall to a flat-rate or that store's category.

stores.name: Sanuk
stores.intro:
Sanuk is a California footwear brand built around casual sandals and shoes made from
unconventional materials, most famously flip-flops with yoga mat footbeds, positioned
around a laid back, surf inspired lifestyle image. It's owned by Deckers Brands, the same
parent company behind UGG and HOKA, though Sanuk operates and sells as its own brand. There
is no Sanuk co-brand credit card, so a purchase there is best charged to an apparel and
footwear or general online shopping rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Scheels
stores.intro: Scheels is an employee-owned chain famous for stadium-sized stores with Ferris wheels and aquariums, and its sprawling locations generally code as a department store. A trip there will not earn grocery or dining bonuses despite the variety on offer. Online purchases at scheels.com typically code as online shopping, so a flat-rate or online card returns the most.

stores.name: Schnucks
stores.intro:
Schnucks is a St. Louis based regional supermarket chain with around 110 stores across
Missouri, Illinois, Indiana, and Wisconsin. The family-owned chain codes as a U.S.
supermarket, so a card with a grocery bonus (Amex Gold, Blue Cash Preferred, Citi
Custom Cash) will earn its full rate at the register. Schnucks runs its own loyalty
program with fuel rewards redeemable at participating gas stations, which can stack
with a separate gas-category card at the pump.

stores.name: Scholastic
stores.intro:
Scholastic is a major publisher and distributor of children's books and educational
materials, best known to generations of families for its in-school Scholastic Book Fairs
and Book Clubs, and as the longtime US publisher of the Harry Potter series and Clifford
the Big Red Dog. It also produces classroom magazines, curriculum resources, and media for
schools and libraries. There's no Scholastic co-brand credit card, so book and classroom
supply purchases through its website are best charged to a general online shopping rewards
card, or a flat-rate cashback card if no bonus category applies.

stores.name: Schwinn Fitness
stores.intro:
Schwinn Fitness sells indoor exercise bikes, treadmills, and other home cardio equipment
under the well-known Schwinn name, a line that operates separately from the Schwinn
bicycle brand sold in bike shops and mass retailers. The exercise bikes in particular have
a long following among indoor cycling and spin class enthusiasts looking for a home
alternative. There is no Schwinn Fitness co-brand credit card, so equipment purchases from
its site are best charged to a general online shopping rewards card, or a flat-rate
cashback card, since fitness equipment doesn't fall under a dedicated bonus category.

stores.name: Scooter's Coffee
stores.intro: Scooter's Coffee is a fast-growing Omaha-born drive-thru chain built around its signature Caramelicious latte and quick coffee kiosks. Orders code as dining or restaurants on most rewards cards, so a dining multiplier is the right tool. Mobile app reloads can sometimes code differently, but a straight tap at the window stays in the dining bucket.

stores.name: Sealy
stores.intro:
Sealy is one of America's best-known mainstream mattress brands, part of Tempur Sealy
International alongside Tempur-Pedic and Stearns & Foster, sold direct at sealy.com and
through mattress specialists, furniture stores, and department retailers. There is no Sealy
co-brand credit card. Because a mattress is an infrequent, high-dollar purchase, shoppers
usually come out ahead with a furniture or home-goods category card, a card that pays a
bonus on online shopping, or a flat-rate cashback card for the one big charge.

stores.name: SeatGeek
stores.intro: SeatGeek aggregates primary and resale tickets and grades listings with its Deal Score tool for concerts, sports, and theater. Spending codes as entertainment or ticketing, so a card carrying an entertainment bonus earns at the higher rate. It is a frequent partner for sports leagues and teams, which can mean exclusive presales worth pairing with the right card.

stores.name: Seed Health
stores.intro:
Seed Health is a microbiome science company best known for its DS-01 Daily Synbiotic, a
probiotic and prebiotic supplement sold on a subscription basis directly to consumers, along
with other gut and women's health products developed with academic research partners. The
brand positions itself at the science-forward end of the wellness supplement market rather
than as a mass retailer. There's no Seed co-brand credit card, so a subscription order is
best charged to a general online shopping or flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Segway
stores.intro:
Segway makes personal electric transportation, including electric scooters, self-balancing
personal transporters, e-bikes, and off-road all-terrain vehicles, a lineup that grew well
beyond the original two-wheeled personal transporter that made the brand a household name.
The company has been owned by the Chinese mobility firm Ninebot since 2015, and the two
brands now operate under the combined Segway-Ninebot name. There's no Segway co-brand
credit card, so a purchase is best charged to an electronics or general online shopping
rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Sephora
stores.intro:
Sephora is the largest U.S. beauty specialty retailer at about 600 stores, plus a
separate shop-in-shop presence inside Kohl's. Beauty doesn't have its own reward
category on most general-purpose cards, so in-store purchases at Sephora typically fall
back to a flat-rate cashback card. Online purchases at sephora.com code as online
shopping. Sephora's own Beauty Insider points layer on top of card cashback.

stores.name: Shake Shack
stores.intro:
Shake Shack is a U.S. fast-casual burger chain with about 350 domestic locations,
with higher average ticket sizes than traditional fast-food. Charges code as
dining on every card we track, so any dining-bonus card applies (Amex Gold 4x,
Sapphire Preferred 3x, SavorOne 3%). Shake Shack runs a Shack Track loyalty
program in its app with progressive item rewards stackable with card bonuses.

stores.name: Shane Co.
stores.intro:
Shane Co. is a family-owned jewelry retailer founded in Colorado in 1971, known for
engagement rings, diamonds, and fine jewelry sold through its own showrooms and website
rather than through department stores or mall jewelry chains. The company markets itself
on cutting out wholesale middlemen to offer more competitive pricing on diamonds it sources
and sets in house. There is no Shane Co. co-brand credit card, so a jewelry purchase is
best charged to a general online shopping or flat-rate cashback card, since jewelry
typically doesn't fall under a dedicated bonus category.

stores.name: SharkNinja
stores.intro:
SharkNinja is the consumer products company behind two well-known home brands: Shark, which
makes vacuums, steam mops, and air purifiers, and Ninja, which makes kitchen appliances
like blenders, air fryers, and coffee makers. The company sells through its own site as
well as major retailers like Amazon, Walmart, Target, and Best Buy, and became an
independently traded public company after spinning off in 2023. There's no SharkNinja
co-brand credit card, so a direct purchase is best charged to an electronics or general
online shopping rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Shaw's
stores.intro: Shaw's is a New England supermarket chain owned by Albertsons, with a long history across Massachusetts and the surrounding states. Spending codes as groceries on most rewards cards, so a supermarket multiplier captures the most value. Fuel purchased at a Shaw's gas station codes separately as gas rather than groceries.

stores.name: Sheetz
stores.intro:
Sheetz is a Mid-Atlantic gas-and-convenience chain known for its made-to-order food
("MTO" subs, sides, smoothies). Fuel codes as gas on every card we track; in-store
food typically codes as gas or convenience and only rarely as dining. The free
Sheetz Rewardz app stacks per-gallon discounts and free food rewards on top of any
card's bonus rate, which usually beats hunting for a Sheetz-specific credit card.

stores.name: Shein
stores.intro:
Shein is a Singapore-headquartered fast-fashion retailer that sells direct to U.S.
consumers, mostly shipped from China and other manufacturing hubs. The model is built
on extremely fast trend cycles, low prices, and a constant churn of new SKUs. Shein
purchases code as online shopping for credit card networks, so any online-shopping
category bonus card will earn its multiplier. There is no Shein co-brand credit card,
so the best play is a general 3-5x online-shopping rewards card or a rotating-bonus
card during an online-shopping quarter.

stores.name: Shell
stores.intro:
Shell is one of the largest U.S. gasoline retailers by station count (about 13,000
locations), with a Fuel Rewards loyalty program tied to the Shell app. Shell pumps code
cleanly as gas on every card we track. The Shell Fuel Rewards program offers stackable
per-gallon discounts that are calculated separately from card cashback.

stores.name: Sherwin-Williams
stores.intro:
Sherwin-Williams is the largest U.S. paint manufacturer and retailer, with over
4,700 company-owned stores serving residential painters, contractors, and designers.
Sherwin-Williams typically codes as home improvement on Visa, Mastercard, and Amex
networks, which makes paint purchases eligible for the home improvement bonus on
cards that carry the category.

The U.S. Bank Cash+ and Bank of America Customized Cash can both route Sherwin
purchases into a selectable 3 to 5 percent bonus. The chain runs frequent 30 to 40
percent off paint promotions that stack with credit card cash back, and Sherwin's
PaintPerks loyalty program adds member pricing on top of any rewards a credit card
delivers.

stores.name: Shipt
stores.intro:
Shipt is a same-day delivery service owned by Target, with grocery, household, and
some specialty retail (Petco, CVS) partners. Most Shipt charges code as groceries
or as the underlying merchant — worth checking your statement before relying on
a category-bonus card. Shipt is bundled into Target Circle 360 (Target's premium
membership), so heavy Target shoppers often pair it with a Target REDcard for
the in-store discount.

stores.name: Shiseido
stores.intro:
Shiseido is a Japanese cosmetics and skincare company founded in 1872, one of the oldest
beauty companies in the world, known for its own namesake skincare and makeup lines as well
as owning brands like NARS, Clé de Peau Beauté, and Drunk Elephant. Its products are sold
through its own website, department store beauty counters, and specialty beauty retailers
like Sephora and Ulta. There's no Shiseido co-brand credit card, so a purchase is best
charged to a department store or general online shopping rewards card, or a flat-rate
cashback card depending on where it's bought.

stores.name: Shoe Carnival
stores.intro:
Shoe Carnival is a value-oriented family footwear chain with about 380 stores
concentrated in the Midwest, South, and Southeast plus shoecarnival.com. The retailer
recently acquired Rogan's Shoes and now also operates Shoe Station. The Shoe Perks
loyalty program is free and offers points and birthday bonuses that stack with credit
card rewards. Most cards code purchases as department store in person and online
shopping for web checkouts.

stores.name: SHOKZ
stores.intro:
SHOKZ, formerly known as AfterShokz, makes open ear, bone conduction headphones designed to
leave the ear canal free, a feature that made the brand popular with runners and cyclists
who want to hear traffic and surroundings while listening to music or calls. The product
line has since expanded into more conventional open ear earbuds and sport headphones. There
is no SHOKZ co-brand credit card, so a purchase from the brand's site or a retail partner is
best run through an electronics or online shopping rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Shopbop
stores.intro:
Shopbop is an online fashion retailer specializing in designer and contemporary apparel,
shoes, and accessories, curating collections from hundreds of brands for a customer base
that skews toward higher-end, trend-driven fashion. Based in Madison, Wisconsin, it has
been owned by Amazon since 2006 but continues to operate as its own distinct storefront and
brand. There's no Shopbop co-brand credit card, so purchases are best charged to an apparel
or general online shopping rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: ShopRite
stores.intro:
ShopRite is a cooperative grocery chain with about 280 stores across the Northeast
and Mid-Atlantic. Codes cleanly as groceries on every card we track. Strongest
pairings are Blue Cash Preferred (6%), Capital One SavorOne (3%), and U.S. Bank
Shopper Cash Rewards (6% if ShopRite is one of the two selected retailers). The
Price Plus Club loyalty program runs digital coupons and per-gallon fuel savings
at participating partner stations on top of card bonuses.

stores.name: Shutterfly
stores.intro: Shutterfly turns photos into prints, books, cards, and personalized gifts ordered entirely online. These purchases code as online shopping, with no photo-specific bonus on mainstream cards. A flat-rate card or one with an online-shopping multiplier works best, and the site runs frequent promotions worth stacking with card-linked offers.

stores.name: Sierra
stores.intro: Sierra, the off-price outdoor and active brand under TJX, sells discounted gear and apparel that often codes as a department store at its stores, so supermarket and restaurant bonuses do not apply. Shopping sierra.com normally codes as online shopping. With deals already built in, pair a card that rewards online spending or carries a reliable flat rate.

stores.name: Sinclair
stores.intro: Sinclair, recognizable by its green Dinoco-style dinosaur logo, is a longtime western US fuel brand with stations across many states. Pay at the pump and the charge codes as a gas or fuel purchase, which is exactly what a card with a gas bonus rewards. Inside convenience-store buys may code separately as retail, so use a fuel-focused card at the pump and a flat-rate card for snacks.

stores.name: Singapore Airlines
stores.intro: Singapore Airlines is a Star Alliance carrier renowned for its Suites and service, flying ultra-long-haul routes from US hubs. Airfare codes as an airline or travel purchase, well matched to a card with an airline or travel bonus. Its KrisFlyer program partners with the major transferable-points programs, so funding awards via transfers is frequently a stronger play than buying tickets outright.

stores.name: Sixt
stores.intro: Sixt is a German-headquartered rental company expanding across the US, often stocking premium and luxury vehicles. Its charges code as car rental, a travel subset, and earn on cards with a travel or rental-car bonus. Travel cards frequently include rental collision coverage, so check your benefits before adding the counter insurance to a Sixt booking.

stores.name: Skechers
stores.intro:
Skechers is a comfort and lifestyle footwear brand with around 550 U.S. concept,
factory outlet, and warehouse stores plus skechers.com. Direct purchases typically
code as department store in person and online shopping for web orders, while Skechers
bought at Famous Footwear, DSW, or Kohl's codes under those host retailers. The free
Skechers Plus loyalty program offers points and a birthday reward and stacks with
credit card earnings.

stores.name: Skillshare
stores.intro:
Skillshare is an online learning platform built around short, project-based video classes
covering creative skills like design, illustration, photography, and writing, along with
business and productivity topics, taught by working professionals rather than traditional
academic instructors. Access is sold as a recurring subscription rather than individual
course purchases. There's no Skillshare co-brand credit card, so a subscription payment is
best charged to a general online shopping or flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Skullcandy
stores.intro:
Skullcandy is an audio brand making headphones, earbuds, and speakers, sold direct at
skullcandy.com and through electronics and mass retailers. There is no Skullcandy co-brand
credit card, so a direct purchase earns best on a card with a strong online shopping
multiplier, while the same pair bought at an electronics store can lean on that retailer's
category bonus.

stores.name: Sleep Number
stores.intro:
Sleep Number makes adjustable air chamber mattresses and smart beds sold under its own
numbered firmness system, along with the SleepIQ technology that tracks sleep quality and
automatically adjusts firmness overnight. The company sells almost entirely direct to
consumer through its own retail stores and website rather than through third-party
furniture or mattress retailers. There's no Sleep Number co-brand credit card, so a
mattress or bedding purchase is best charged to a furniture or general online shopping
rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Smart & Final
stores.intro:
Smart & Final is a California-based hybrid warehouse/grocery chain with about 250
stores across the Western U.S. and Mexico. Unlike Costco or Sam's, no membership is
required, so any shopper can walk in and buy bulk packaged goods alongside normal
grocery items. Merchant category coding is the gotcha here: most Smart & Final stores
historically code as warehouse club rather than supermarket, which means a grocery
bonus card (Amex Gold, Blue Cash Preferred) may not trigger its multiplier. Coding can
vary by location, so check a recent statement before assuming a category bonus applies.

stores.name: Smartwool
stores.intro:
Smartwool is an outdoor apparel brand founded in Steamboat Springs, Colorado, built around
merino wool socks, base layers, and midlayers designed for hiking, skiing, and everyday
wear. The brand is a fixture in outdoor and ski shops as well as its own direct-to-consumer
site, prized for wool's moisture-wicking and odor-resistant properties compared to cotton
or synthetic fabrics. There's no Smartwool co-brand credit card, so a purchase is best
charged to a sporting goods, apparel, or general online shopping rewards card, or a
flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Smith's
stores.intro: Smith's Food and Drug is Kroger's Intermountain West banner across Utah, Nevada, and neighboring states, pairing groceries with pharmacy and fuel. Checkout at the supermarket codes as groceries on most rewards cards, so a supermarket bonus is ideal. Gas bought at an attached Smith's fuel center instead codes as a fuel purchase.

stores.name: Snapfish
stores.intro: Snapfish is an online photo service for prints, photo books, and custom products, competing directly with Shutterfly. Orders code as online shopping rather than any specialized category, so a flat-rate or online-retail card earns the most. Its steady stream of discount codes pairs well with a card that rewards web purchases.

stores.name: SOCCER.com
stores.intro:
SOCCER.com is an online specialty retailer focused entirely on soccer gear, carrying
cleats, jerseys, balls, training equipment, and apparel from major brands like Nike,
adidas, and Puma alongside club and national team kits. It's one of the larger dedicated
soccer e-commerce sites in the US market, serving both individual players and team or club
orders. There's no SOCCER.com co-brand credit card, so shoppers should lean on a sporting
goods or general online shopping rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card, when checking out.

stores.name: SodaStream
stores.intro:
SodaStream makes home sparkling water machines and CO2 canister refills that let users
carbonate tap water and flavor it with syrups, positioned as a reusable alternative to
buying bottled soda and seltzer. Founded in Israel, the company has been owned by PepsiCo
since 2018, which uses it to push a more sustainable, less plastic-heavy beverage option.
There's no SodaStream co-brand credit card, so a machine or refill purchase is best charged
to a general online shopping rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card, and occasionally a
grocery category card if bought through a grocery retailer.

stores.name: Sonesta
stores.intro: Sonesta has expanded rapidly into one of the larger US hotel groups, spanning brands from extended-stay to upscale resorts after several portfolio acquisitions. Room charges code as a hotel or travel purchase, fitting a card with a hotel or general travel bonus. Booking direct usually keeps Sonesta Travel Pass loyalty intact, while a card portal may trade those perks for extra points.

stores.name: Sonic Drive-In
stores.intro:
Sonic Drive-In is a U.S. drive-in fast-food chain with about 3,500 domestic
locations, primarily in the South and Midwest. Charges code as dining on every
card we track. Any dining-bonus card applies (Amex Gold 4x, Sapphire Preferred 3x,
SavorOne 3%). The Sonic app's My SONIC Rewards program issues per-purchase points
and limited-time half-price drink and burger promotions that stack on top of card
bonuses.

stores.name: Sony
stores.intro: Sony sells electronics, PlayStation gear, cameras and audio products across its various online storefronts. Direct purchases generally code as online shopping or general retail, so a card with an online or everyday-spending bonus is the better fit, and a shopping portal can sometimes add a small rebate on top.

stores.name: Southwest Airlines
stores.intro:
Southwest Airlines is the largest U.S. domestic carrier by passenger volume and the
home of the Rapid Rewards program. Tickets code as airfare on every card we track,
and the Companion Pass — earned by hitting an annual points threshold — is widely
considered the best perk in U.S. domestic travel. Co-branded Chase Rapid Rewards
cards count signup bonuses toward Companion Pass qualification, which is the main
reason most travelers carry one. Southwest doesn't sell tickets through OTAs.

stores.name: Spectrum
stores.intro: Spectrum, Charter's cable brand, serves millions across more than 40 states with internet, TV, and home phone. Your monthly bill posts as a cable or internet charge, so it earns at the bonus rate only on a card that rewards internet or cable spending, with everything else falling to your flat-rate card. Setting it on autopay to a wireless or utility bonus card can capture extra points each cycle.

stores.name: Speedo
stores.intro:
Speedo is a swimwear and swim gear brand closely tied to competitive swimming, having
outfitted numerous Olympic swimmers over the decades and become a default choice for
swim team apparel, goggles, and training equipment. The global Speedo brand originated in
Australia and is now part of the UK-based Pentland Group, which licenses the brand
separately in different regions. There's no Speedo co-brand credit card, so a purchase from
its US site is best charged to a sporting goods, apparel, or general online shopping
rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Speedway
stores.intro:
Speedway is a Midwest-and-East gas-and-convenience chain operated by 7-Eleven
parent 7-Eleven, Inc. Pumps code as gas on every card we track. The Speedy Rewards
program is unusually generous on convenience-store snacks and drinks, often more
valuable per visit than the gas-card rebate. Heavy Speedway customers pair a 4-5%
general gas card with the Speedy Rewards app rather than chase a co-brand option.

stores.name: Sperry
stores.intro:
Sperry is a footwear brand credited with inventing the original boat shoe in 1935, and it
remains best known for that siped-sole deck shoe design alongside a broader lineup of
casual and cold-weather footwear. It's owned by Wolverine World Wide, which also owns
brands like Merrell and Saucony, though Sperry sells and markets under its own name. There
is no Sperry co-brand credit card, so a shoe purchase is best charged to an apparel and
footwear or general online shopping rewards card, or a flat-rate cashback card.

stores.name: Spirit Airlines
stores.intro:
Spirit Airlines is a U.S. ultra-low-cost carrier known for stripped-down base fares
with à la carte pricing for bags, seat selection, and refreshments. Tickets code as
airfare on every card we track, but for cards that offer trip-delay or trip-cancel
insurance the small base fare often falls below useful coverage thresholds. There's
no widely-issued co-branded Spirit credit card, so general travel cards (Sapphire
Preferred, Venture, Capital One Savor) are typically the best pairing.

stores.name: Sportsman's Warehouse
stores.intro: Sportsman's Warehouse caters to hunting, fishing, and shooting-sports customers, and its outdoor retail stores commonly code as a department store, leaving grocery and dining bonuses out of reach. Orders at sportsmans.com usually code as online shopping. Because there is no outdoor-gear bonus tier, a card with strong online-shopping or flat-rate earning is the sensible pick.

stores.name: Spotify
stores.intro:
Spotify is the largest U.S. music-streaming service. Monthly Spotify subscriptions
code as streaming on every card we track. Streaming-bonus cards (Blue Cash
Preferred 6%, U.S. Bank Cash+ 5% on selected categories) are the strongest
pairings. The American Express Platinum offers digital-entertainment statement
credits that occasionally apply to Spotify; check current terms before relying
on it.

stores.name: Sprouts Farmers Market
stores.intro:
Sprouts Farmers Market is a natural-and-organic grocery chain with about 400 U.S.
stores, leaning heavily on fresh produce and bulk foods. Sprouts codes cleanly as
groceries on every card we track. The strongest pairings are 6% Blue Cash Preferred
and the U.S. Bank Shopper Cash Rewards (if Sprouts is selected as a chosen retailer);
Capital One SavorOne earns 3% on groceries with no cap. Sprouts doesn't run a
co-branded card, so general grocery cards are the standard answer.

stores.name: Stanley
stores.intro:
Stanley is the drinkware and outdoor-gear brand behind the vacuum-insulated bottles and
tumblers that trace back to a 1913 vacuum flask design, most recently propelled to viral
status by the Quencher tumbler. The catalog also spans coolers, food jars, and cookware
aimed at campers and commuters alike. Stanley has no co-brand credit card, so shoppers are
best served by a flat-rate cashback card or, for those who buy through a broader online
shopping portal, a card that boosts everyday online purchase categories.

stores.name: Staples
stores.intro:
Staples is a U.S. office-supply retailer with about 1,000 stores plus a strong
business-to-business operation. In-store and online charges code as office
supplies on every card we track. The Chase Ink Business Cash earns 5% back at
office-supply stores including Staples (capped at $25K/yr in combined office
supply + telecom spend) — typically the strongest pairing for small-business
cardholders. Staples also runs a Rewards program issuing points-back on
recycling and ink purchases.

stores.name: Starbucks
stores.intro:
Starbucks runs about 16,500 U.S. stores plus the Starbucks app, which is itself one of
the largest closed-loop payment systems in the country. Whether you pay through the app,
with a physical Starbucks card, or directly with a credit card, the underlying Starbucks
transaction codes as dining/restaurants on every card we track. Reloading your Starbucks
balance via card is the cleanest way to capture the dining bonus on cards that pay 3-5%
on dining.

stores.name: Steak 'n Shake
stores.intro: Steak 'n Shake is a Midwest-rooted chain famous for its Steakburgers and hand-dipped milkshakes, now leaning into self-order kiosks. Charges code as dining or restaurants on most cards, so a dining multiplier earns well. Ordering at a kiosk and paying there keeps the transaction in the restaurant category just like the counter.

stores.name: Steam
stores.intro: Steam is Valve's PC game storefront, the biggest on the platform, selling games, downloadable content, and in-wallet credit. Charges code as online shopping or digital entertainment, so a card with an online-purchase bonus tends to earn best. Loading Steam Wallet during a seasonal sale lets you stack a card bonus with discounts of up to 90 percent on titles.

stores.name: Stearns & Foster
stores.intro:
Stearns & Foster is a premium mattress brand dating to 1846, now owned by Tempur Sealy
International, positioned above mainstream Sealy lines with hand-tufted, luxury-tier
construction sold through its own site and select mattress and department retailers.
There is no Stearns & Foster co-brand credit card. Because mattress purchases are
infrequent and large, shoppers typically get more value from a furniture or home-goods
category card, a card with a strong online shopping multiplier, or simply a flat-rate
cashback card for a big one-time purchase.

stores.name: Steve Madden
stores.intro: Steve Madden designs trend-driven footwear and accessories sold direct and through department stores. Buying on its site codes as online shopping or retail, so a portal or rotating bonus can boost earnings, while store purchases generally land on a flat-rate card with no shoe-specific category.

stores.name: Stila
stores.intro:
Stila is a Los Angeles-founded makeup brand known for its Stay All Day liquid lipstick and
waterproof eyeliners, sold direct online as well as through Ulta, Sephora, and various
department stores. It carries no co-brand credit card of its own. Shoppers buying directly
from stilacosmetics.com are best off with a card that rewards general online shopping,
while purchases made in-store at a partner retailer may earn more under that retailer's
own department-store or drugstore bonus category instead.

affiliate.offer: up to 60% off with code SAVEMORE

stores.name: Stitch Fix
stores.intro: Stitch Fix is an online personal-styling service that mails curated clothing boxes you keep or return, charging a styling fee credited toward purchases. Every charge codes as online shopping since there is no storefront, so a card with an online-purchase bonus earns best. Keeping the whole box often unlocks a discount, which pairs neatly with card rewards.

stores.name: StockX
stores.intro:
StockX is a resale marketplace built around sneakers, streetwear, watches, handbags,
and collectibles, with a bid/ask exchange model and mandatory authentication of every
item before it ships to the buyer. The platform is a primary secondary market for
hyped sneaker releases. StockX codes as online shopping for credit card networks, so
an online-shopping category bonus card will earn its multiplier. Buyer fees are baked
into the transaction price, which is the figure that posts to the card.

stores.name: Stop & Shop
stores.intro:
Stop & Shop is a Northeast U.S. grocery chain with about 400 stores, also owned by
Ahold Delhaize. Codes cleanly as groceries on every card we track. Best pairings
are Blue Cash Preferred (6%), Capital One SavorOne (3%), and U.S. Bank Shopper Cash
Rewards (6% on selected retailers including most major grocers). The Go Rewards
loyalty program runs separate per-gallon discounts at Stop & Shop fuel stations
that stack on top of card bonuses.

stores.name: Straight Talk
stores.intro:
Straight Talk is a no-contract prepaid wireless brand that runs on the Verizon, AT&T, and
T-Mobile networks, sold heavily through Walmart stores and online at flat monthly rates
with no credit check required. It has no co-brand credit card. Because it is a prepaid
carrier, the strongest play is a card that earns an elevated rate on phone bills or cell
phone providers, or, if paying inside a Walmart store, whatever category that specific
purchase falls under on a general spending card.

stores.name: Stride Rite
stores.intro:
Stride Rite is a children's footwear brand dating to 1919, known for shoes designed around
early walkers and growing feet, sold through its own online store as well as department
stores and shoe retailers. It does not have a co-brand credit card. Shoppers buying
directly from striderite.com will get the most value from a card with a strong online
shopping or select-clothing bonus category, while in-store purchases at a partner retailer
may fall under that store's own bonus category instead.

stores.name: StubHub
stores.intro: StubHub is a leading resale marketplace where fans buy and sell tickets to live events, with prices that float based on demand. Purchases typically code as entertainment or ticketing, so a card with an entertainment category bonus is the strongest pick. Because resale prices swing widely, timing your buy often saves more than any rewards rate you could earn on the order.

stores.name: Subway
stores.intro:
Subway is the largest restaurant chain in the U.S. by store count, with about
20,000 domestic locations. Charges code as dining on every card we track. Standard
high-bonus dining cards (Amex Gold 4x capped, Sapphire Preferred 3x, Capital One
SavorOne 3%) are the obvious pairings. The MyWay Rewards loyalty program and
recurring digital coupons in the Subway app stack on top of card bonuses.

stores.name: Summit Racing
stores.intro: Summit Racing is a major mail-order and online seller of performance auto parts, tools, and accessories, popular with gearheads building or modifying cars. Orders placed on summitracing.com code as online shopping or general retail, and there is no grocery or dining bonus to chase here. Pair it with a card that boosts online spending, or default to a reliable everyday flat-rate card for the larger builds.

stores.name: Sun Country Airlines
stores.intro:
Sun Country Airlines is a Minneapolis/St. Paul-based ultra-low-cost carrier with a hybrid
business model that combines scheduled leisure flights, cargo flying for Amazon, and ad-hoc
charters for sports teams and casinos. Routes mostly connect MSP to warm-weather US and
Mexico destinations during peak seasons. Sun Country purchases code as airlines/airfare on
every credit card. Cards with airfare bonus categories all earn full rate: the Chase
Sapphire Reserve at 4x, Amex Gold at 3x on direct-booked flights, Capital One Venture X at 2x.

stores.name: Sunbasket
stores.intro: Sunbasket ships organic meal kits and prepared meals with an emphasis on clean, dietary-friendly ingredients. Even though it is food, the charges process as an online subscription and typically code as online shopping rather than groceries, so a card with an online or everyday bonus usually beats chasing a supermarket category.

stores.name: Sunoco
stores.intro:
Sunoco-branded stations cover about 5,200 U.S. locations primarily across the East
and Midwest. Pumps code cleanly as gas on every card we track. The Sunoco Rewards
app stacks 5-10 cents per gallon savings on top of any card's bonus rate. There's
a Sunoco Rewards co-branded credit card, but in absolute cashback most 4-5%
general gas cards beat it.

stores.name: T-Mobile
stores.intro: T-Mobile is one of the big three US carriers, offering wireless plans plus home internet. Wireless bills earn extra only on cards with a phone or wireless bonus, otherwise they code at the flat rate, and a handful of cards throw in cell-phone protection when the bill is paid with that card.

stores.name: Taco Bell
stores.intro:
Taco Bell is a U.S. fast-food chain with about 7,500 domestic locations, owned by
Yum! Brands. Charges code as dining on every card we track. Standard dining cards
(Amex Gold 4x, Sapphire Preferred 3x, SavorOne 3%) earn the bonus. The Taco Bell
Rewards app issues frequent free-item promotions and limited-time member-exclusive
menu items that stack on top of any card's bonus rate.

stores.name: Take 5 Oil Change
stores.intro:
Take 5 Oil Change is a drive-through oil change chain operated by Driven Brands,
with over 1,000 U.S. locations. Drivers stay in the car while technicians complete
the oil change in about five to ten minutes. Most card networks code Take 5 as auto
services rather than as a dedicated bonus category, which puts the chain outside
most rotating and selectable bonus rewards.

For cardholders who want elevated rewards on routine maintenance, Citi Custom Cash
can pay 5 percent if auto services is the top eligible spending category in a given
month. Otherwise, a flat-rate card like the Citi Double Cash, Wells Fargo Active
Cash, or PayPal Cashback Mastercard delivers consistent 2 percent on every Take 5
visit.

stores.name: Talbots
stores.intro: Talbots sells classic women's apparel and accessories aimed at a polished, professional wardrobe. Purchases on its website code as online shopping or general retail, so a shopping-portal or rotating bonus can add value, but in-store buys typically land on a flat-rate card with no dedicated clothing category.

stores.name: Talkspace
stores.intro:
Talkspace is an online therapy and psychiatry platform that connects subscribers with
licensed clinicians through text, audio, and video messaging, billed on a recurring
subscription basis and, in many cases, covered in part by health insurance. Talkspace has
no co-brand credit card. Since it bills like a subscription service rather than a
traditional retailer, a flat-rate cashback card or a card that rewards recurring
subscription or online purchases will generally earn the most on the monthly charge.

stores.name: Target

also_earns.note: If selected as one of your 2 quarterly retailers (up to $1,500/quarter, then 1.5%)

stores.intro:
Target sits in the same awkward bucket as Walmart for general-purpose credit cards: it
codes as a discount store rather than a supermarket or department store, so most cards'
bonus categories (grocery, dining, department stores) miss it entirely. Target.com
purchases code as online shopping on most cards, which is where the highest
non-co-branded earn rates show up. In-store, you're typically falling back to a strong
flat-rate card unless you carry a Target-issued card for the 5% RedCard discount applied
at checkout.

stores.name: Taskrabbit
stores.intro:
Taskrabbit is an online marketplace that connects people with independent taskers for
furniture assembly, moving help, home repairs, and other odd jobs, booked and paid for
through its app or website. It has been owned by IKEA since 2017 and handles a large share
of IKEA furniture assembly bookings in addition to general handyman tasks. Taskrabbit has
no co-brand credit card, so a flat-rate cashback card or a card that earns extra on online
shopping purchases is the most practical way to earn rewards when booking a task.

stores.name: Tatcha
stores.intro:
Tatcha is a Japanese-inspired skincare brand built around ingredients like green tea and
rice bran, sold direct online as well as through Sephora and department store beauty
counters, and owned by Unilever since 2019. There is no Tatcha co-brand credit card.
Purchases made directly at tatcha.com are best matched to a card with a strong online
shopping bonus, while buying through Sephora or a department store may instead earn under
that retailer's own bonus category.

stores.name: Tecovas
stores.intro:
Tecovas is a direct-to-consumer western brand best known for handcrafted leather cowboy
boots, later expanded into western-styled apparel, belts, and bags, sold through its own
website and a growing number of physical stores. It carries no co-brand credit card.
Shoppers should look to a card with a solid online shopping or select-clothing bonus
category when ordering online, or a general flat-rate cashback card for purchases made at
one of its brick-and-mortar locations.

stores.name: Tempur-Pedic
stores.intro:
Tempur-Pedic is the flagship memory-foam mattress brand of Tempur Sealy International,
the same parent behind Stearns & Foster and Sealy, selling its TEMPUR-material
mattresses, pillows, and bedding direct at tempurpedic.com and through mattress
specialists, furniture stores, and department retailers. There is no Tempur-Pedic
co-brand credit card. Because a mattress is an infrequent, high-dollar purchase,
shoppers usually come out ahead with a furniture or home-goods category card, a card
that pays a bonus on online shopping, or simply a flat-rate cashback card for the one
big charge. Tempur-Pedic bought at a department store or mattress chain can instead
earn under that retailer's own category rate.

stores.name: Temu
stores.intro:
Temu is a U.S.-facing online marketplace owned by Chinese e-commerce giant PDD
Holdings, launched stateside in 2022 and aggressively marketed on ultra-low prices and
direct-from-manufacturer shipping. Purchases typically code as online shopping or
merchandise, so a card with an online-shopping category bonus (Capital One SavorOne
promotions, Chase Freedom Flex when activated, Citi Custom Cash on online shopping
month) will earn its full multiplier. Temu has no co-brand credit card, so the best
earn comes from a general online-shopping bonus.

stores.name: Teva
stores.intro:
Teva is an outdoor footwear brand owned by Deckers Brands, best known for its sport sandals
and sold direct at teva.com and through outdoor, sporting, and department stores. There is
no Teva co-brand credit card, so a purchase codes as clothing or online shopping, and a card
with a clothing or online shopping category bonus earns the most.

stores.name: Texas Roadhouse
stores.intro:
Texas Roadhouse is a U.S. casual-dining steakhouse chain with about 700 domestic
locations. Charges code as dining on every card we track. Any dining-bonus card
earns the bonus (Amex Gold 4x, Sapphire Preferred 3x, SavorOne 3%). The chain
doesn't run a high-volume loyalty program, so the card bonus is the main
rewards lever per visit.

stores.name: TGI Fridays
stores.intro:
TGI Fridays operates around 350 US casual-dining restaurants with a bar-and-grill format that
helped define the category, anchored by Loaded Potato Skins, Jack Daniel's Sesame Chicken
Strips, and a full cocktail program. The chain has contracted from its 1990s peak but
remains a recognizable airport and suburban brand. Fridays transactions code as dining/
restaurants on every credit card, including bar tabs and to-go orders. Dining-bonus cards
earn full rate: Amex Gold at 4x, Chase Sapphire Reserve at 4x, Capital One SavorOne at 3% cash.

stores.name: The Body Shop
stores.intro:
